Hopefully what the fans do, and they reciprocate, is they almost unconditionally back our judgment at that moment in time.

What we said we'd do is really important to us — and that's in particular at times of giving bad news. At least let's be transparent and communicate that rather than hiding away.

The Bus Parade: An Unforgettable Moment

Charlie: You said it was a pretty special moment to share with them when you did win the league and you had the celebrations in the city.

Liam: That was — I'll never forget that moment in my life. That was incredible. The city bus parade — people won't know the Lincoln geography, but we're sat on the open-top bus outside just outside the back of the stadium, and we're there for 20 minutes waiting for the official parade time. And if I'm honest, I'm looking down the street thinking: "I don't think anyone's going to turn up to this. I can hardly see anyone. I think this is going to be the most embarrassing moment in football history."

Then we got to the roundabout and turned the corner, and the High Street in Lincoln — 40 to 50,000 people came out within the city. Bear in mind the population's 100,000 within the City of Lincoln. It was just incredible. But I don't think the scale was the most impressive thing — because the High Street in Lincoln, and the bus could hardly move, we spent a lot of time just stuck. You were able to look people in the eyes and see what it meant to them and the passion and the care and the support.

I'm a bit of an emotional void at times, but that was a real lump in the throat, and you're like: "Oh my God, this is special." That was a really cool moment because the players, the staff, everybody that had been involved in it, was there to witness it.

One of the things we really wanted is the players' families on the buses — we had an extra bus on because we wanted the players to experience this with their families as well. The people that have sacrificed the most over the last 12 months and prior to that are their families. So for them to be able to experience it I think was really really important for us. That was a really cool moment.

Charlie: It's probably a funny moment for you as a CEO. People probably don't give enough recognition to executives in football, where you do have to separate emotion from outcome quite often. To have an experience like that — actually, that is just a moment, that's a day to not worry about what's happening next, to not think about something that's gone wrong in the past, to let yourself enjoy that moment. Because you, as much as the players, as much as the fans, are part of the team that allow that to happen. But you probably very rarely let yourself even recognize that.

Liam: No, the — my wife's brilliant and she supports me unconditionally. The last thing she said to me as I was leaving the house — because I was winging about a few things — "My name's on the safety certificate, I'm a bit grumpy about X, Y, and Z, oh this person wants this" — she went, "Oh, will you just please enjoy this." In fairness, she rarely gives me advice, but when she does, I do listen. And yeah, it was a great moment.

But broader than that — and I genuinely mean this — this isn't meant to be condescending — it's taken a village for Lincoln City to have the success we have. Behind the scenes, and to be able to share that moment with so many people and to see it — that was really special. There were two bits: looking down in the fans' eyes and seeing what it meant to them, and then looking back in the eyes of the people on the bus seeing what that meant to them. They were really cool moments.

I remember turning around to our head of medical, who used to be a scholar at the club. He went away and did his medical qualifications, then was our Academy physiotherapist, and then became our head — and he's from Lincoln. I remember turning around going, "Oh mate, this is special" — and looking in his eyes and him looking back at me too. I think those are the bits in football that make it all the more worthwhile.

From Canoe to Cruise Ship — But Still Punching Up

Charlie: Previously you said to me you're up against people who are in a nuclear sub, and you're in a canoe. Can we move you out of a canoe? Where are we at? Sunseeker? Small cruise ship / ferry?

Liam: Cruise ship. No, I think we've definitely got a motor on our boat now.

Charlie: So the paddles are gone.

Liam: Yeah, we can let go of the paddles. But I'm not too sure it's much bigger than that. Going back to Charlie, it can be quite liberating knowing who you are, what you've got, and where we are.

We know how hard the challenge is going to be going into one of the top leagues in the world next year. League One in the UEFA coefficient is about the 37th league, Championship is about the 7th league. We've not jumped up one league — we've jumped up 30 leagues. So we are definitely not approaching this naive. We're definitely not sat here thinking we've got the answers to everything — we definitely don't. But equally, there is part of us that goes, "Okay, as long as we're us, and as long as we continue being us and commit to being us, we believe that that will be good enough." And you have to enjoy it.

Charlie: So many times this season I was just looking at that Championship table. It is incredible. How many massive teams are playing in that league? And now you, Bolton, Cardiff adding to that. There are games every single weekend that you and the fans will get to enjoy. That's not void of expectation, that's not void of you performing well. But it is a privilege, right? You've slugged away for seven years where, if we're being honest, probably a lot of people would have expected you to be down the bottom rather than at the top, and you've made that jump.

Liam: I remember a fan at one of the local fan forums — he kept saying, "Oh Liam, you keep telling us that we want to look up the pyramid, not down. But show us what you mean, tell us what you mean by that."

I think we've got to be really careful — if you stand in a building full of people, and as a genuine leader you say, "Look, we've got this shot put and we're going to hit the moon," they'll look at you and go, "Okay, come on." But the big thing I'd say is what I've learned is: you definitely do not put a ceiling on a group of people. You definitely do not cap what is possible.

So there's not one ounce of what we're doing going into next year that is intending to be tourists. We are a serious professional outfit. We do things our way, and we're there to compete. We don't want to have the mentality of "Oh, if we get beat, so what" — we definitely don't have that. We just have to get that calibration between realism and expectation.

A quick example: teams that stay up often average just over 52 points. The average promoted team wins three away games in their first season.

Charlie: Really?

Liam: Three away games. That's 20-odd away games that you're coming away very empty-handed.

Charlie: 1/5th of the points — that's fascinating.

Liam: So how can we attack that and do that differently? What if we can be the club that goes and wins five or six? Everybody says home form's really important — for example. So whilst there is balanced and measured expectation, equally there is a little bit of steel behind the scenes of: "You know what? We're going to be us, we're going to take it our way, and we believe it will be enough."

The biggest challenge you'll always have as a leader is calibrating that — that it's not blind arrogance or naivety, but equally you're not denting aspiration and putting a ceiling on a group.

Recruitment in a New Universe: From League of Ireland to the Bernabéu

Charlie: How different is operating in the transfer market — or recruitment environment — in a Championship compared to a League One?

Liam: I'll tell this anecdote, and I don't mean it condescendingly. The way our processes work — when it gets to the end-point with clips and videos, towards the back end of last season when we were looking at recruitment — with all due respect, when we're watching clips of players, we're used to watching players running around maybe in a team in the League of Ireland or maybe a League Two team or League One team within England.

And this clip comes on the screen — and it's the Bernabéu.

I'm going, "Is this — who are we after here?"

Charlie: (laughing) We're shopping in Madrid now.

Liam: Look, it was a qualifier game. But that was definitely an interesting moment where we looked at each other and went, "Ah, the game's changed here, hasn't it."

Where I think we can thrive as Lincoln City — the unfiltered truth is the Premier League and the Championship is a really interesting proposition for players across the world. They know that English football has the best leagues, so they want to come to the Championship. We are one of the few Championship football clubs and few Premier League football clubs — we're in that family now. Our opportunity to go and shop in that global market has come now. If we want to be precious about this — that's not because we're Lincoln City, that's because we are in one of the top seven leagues of this world. So that has opened up a brand new market for us.

What we've got to do is choreograph: what does that relationship and that journey look like for that individual, for that human being? Because for the last seven years we've talked about: come to Lincoln City, join us, and either you go to the Championship because you go faster than us, or we go there together. Whereas now we're hopefully talking about: come to us, and you go to the Premier League, or you go with [us]. So that does take a recalibration, because the type of player, quite frankly the quality of player, the history of where they've come from, is very different.

However, what we've also got to be really respectful of is we've got an incredible group of people — some of them pre-peak in their development curve, some of them quite frankly post-peak — that have come and done an unbelievable thing at Lincoln City. So we've got to get the balance right between respecting what's gone on in the past and what's going on in the future. Our build is going to be sensitive and respectful of that — again, not romantic — but equally never put a ceiling on a group of players.

Charlie: It's a fine balance, isn't it? You are one of the most humble people in football that I've ever met, I promise you. And I'd imagine that is a quality that has transitioned throughout the organization. At the same time, football is also about strength and confidence, and some form of overt performance of self-belief, to encourage people in that situation that this is the place you have to go to. So for you, it is a fine balance between having that internally and then being able to turn on something when the time is right for a conversation where you need to sell a bit of a dream.

Liam: That's where a lot of what we've done in the past still translates. Being really honest, our best recruitment tool is players that have left us for a range of reasons. One of the things I'm really proud about is the way our players talk about us after they've moved on. Sometimes that's successful stories and sometimes that's not — I'm not going to name them individually, it'd be unfair the scenarios — but we have definitely signed players in the last three, four, five years purely and simply because of feedback, referrals, whatever you want to call it from colleagues, either players that they're playing with currently or players they've played with in the past.

Because they talk about what the environment's like at Lincoln City, the level of support you get. Now, this isn't woolly fluffy — this is still elite sport, this isn't "we're all sat on bean bags." Don't mistake our kindness for weakness. There is a real edge to what we do. But equally, there is a way that you can go about things — and if you put the human being at the center of that, very rarely do you have a misalignment.

On Liam's Own Strengths and Weaknesses

Charlie: Out of interest — you've done this now for a while — what part of the job comes most naturally to you, and what part have you had to maybe work on a little bit?

Liam: The bit that I really enjoy is people and solving problems. The best thing in the seat that I'm in is that you do have the ability to make decisions that ultimately can impact people's lives. That's the coolest thing with the job. You have to act responsibly with that, but looking someone in the eye and backing them and believing in them — that's not just on-field talent.

One of the proudest things we have at Lincoln City is our alumni — people that have come in, worked for the organization, that have then gone on to work at the very top of sports business. I think it's one of the coolest things. Meeting up with them every now and then: "How's life?" Speaking to a couple of our former colleagues that have just come back from the Monaco Grand Prix working at McLaren F1 — you go, "That's pretty cool. I remember you as an intern" or "I remember you coming in fresh out of university in an entry-level job." Those are some of the coolest stories we have, and they become our best salespeople and recruitment people.

If I have one talent in this world, I do think it's working with people and bringing a team along and creating an environment that is both fun but equally is high performance.

To answer the other side: as a chief exec, ultimately responsible — you have things from a health and safety, governance, regulation point of view — I can't lie and say I get ecstatic about reading our latest safety report. But you have to operate with balance. One of the things working through a lower league club — you have to be ambidextrous. You have to have the ability to do as many things as possible, because without that, you don't have balance within the organization.

But football's a wonderful industry with wonderful people. The thing maybe the fans don't see is it's really collegiate. We'd all kick our granny on a Saturday for three points — there is no doubt that we're competitive beasts — but I promise you, maybe because it's the pressure of the industry, Monday to Friday it's really collegiate. There are some proper top human beings that will always go out their way to help you and work with you. That's pretty cool within the industry.

Efficiency, Set Pieces, and an AI Edge

Charlie: A couple of random questions. Firstly, 21st Group listed Lincoln as the most sustainable and efficient club in England. What does it mean to be the most sustainable and efficient club?

Liam: "Efficient" is everything we drive. The board measure me and us on cost per point. They measure us on over-performance — that is the golden thread and expectation. For somebody as credible as the 21st Group to run their analysis and say we are the most efficient over a prolonged period of time — that's really nice. Lots of people have contributed to that: systems, processes. But what we can't do is sit here and celebrate that and go, "Well, we've got it." The challenge is going to be how do we do that at the next level.

I look at our business plan, I look at the things we have in place: we will be promoted with the smallest loss probably in the last decade. We will lose two million pounds in the year that we got promoted. There are certain teams that lost 35 million, 50 million going up. We've got to go and do the same thing at the next level. Efficiency and effectiveness are probably the two buzzwords we'd want around Lincoln City.

Charlie: That's a serious achievement — that's incredible. I hear some shocking numbers here. One of my favorite media quotes was: "Lincoln City is English football's model club, and the other clubs should learn from them — but they probably won't."

The other bit — I think you're the highest-scoring side in England from set pieces? And part of the reason was the use of an AI tool? In my head it's just Set Piece FC — Arsenal — dominating that, but you've even usurped them.

Liam: It goes back to what makes our boat go the fastest. Several years ago, our sporting director and the team thought, "How do we be really effective?" In football a number of years ago, because Pep did it, it became fashionable: how to restart from a goal kick. 20 years ago it was how far can you punt it, and then all of a sudden you've got center-backs rolling in and your six dropping down. Restarts became the thing.

We just wanted to take that to the next level — effectiveness in terms of restarts from throws, from set pieces. We just felt we could weaponize that. It goes back to "How do we be the absolute best?" Looking and embracing innovation: we worked with a company to analyze tens of millions of corners, tens of millions of throws, and understand how we could best do it.

Now, I'm not going to give away too much. But for example: short corners, quick short corners give you the best probability. But defining "quick" is under six seconds. More than six seconds, then it has the least probability. Delivering the ball into a certain area of the box...

Charlie: Interesting.

Liam: What it needed is skilled practitioners to translate that data. And it would need a culture and environment within the training ground to really embrace and commit to wanting to do that. There aren't many footballers that love doing set-piece training at the end of training, and the repetition. So that required whole-club buy-in from the players, the staff. It wasn't just the intelligence — it was about how you apply it.

Set pieces have become vogue now, and there are going to be lots of teams that then go and replicate. That's what happens with the industry — it constantly evolves. Once somebody — whether Arsenal, it wasn't us — we're the best of thieves, we copied somebody, we then just committed to that. You have to then move with the times to ask, "What's next? How do you innovate?" But yeah, three or four years ago we set out to be actually the best at set pieces, for and against, in the division. It was really cool to do that across Europe.

Innovation and the Entrepreneur in Residence

Charlie: Can I extend that — because you've been excellent in another area? I spend a lot of time trying to convince clubs to open up a little bit more budget to employ tools and technologies that find them a cutting edge in areas where it isn't necessarily geared towards performance — but is doing what we've talked about previously: trying to find those margins around the game. The challenge is it takes investment up front, often before you see return on that.

You've been pretty prominent as a football club — you've had an Entrepreneur in Residence, which for people that don't know is a very un-football job. Really, it's someone with the responsibility of finding and implementing technologies. How have you thought about that and balanced it as a risk?

Liam: I do — and it goes back to the fact that we're almost liberated to be a bit quirky and do something different because of our status and what we're trying to achieve. What happens in football, in English football, in any industry where you benchmark — whether you draw the line at 116 clubs, 72, 92, whichever way — there is then almost a cookie-cutter scenario: "This is what you're supposed to do, this is how you're going to do it."

So when we looked at our whole innovation sphere, that wasn't about trying to get on the front pages of the tech magazines or anything. It was about: how do we shift the dial, and how do we do something different that then net returns into the bank of performance, in terms of investment — how does that help us do something different?

Charlie, the truth: we've had lots of fails in that area.

Charlie: That's interesting.

Liam: Absolutely. But equally, some of the successes that we've had and some of the things we've picked up on — they've made the points of difference. Our biggest challenge in that area was, when we weren't yet seeing any green shoots, to have the commitment to almost double down and then bring in the Entrepreneur in Residence.

I remember when we did that, my biggest concern was, "Oh, that's going to look really knobby." Like, that's going to look like, "Who do they think they are?" And then it went back to: you know what? It is going to help us, it is the right thing to do. So we're going to do it and we'll do it with pride, and everybody can say whatever they want about us — but I'm kind of cool and I'm fine with that.

That was definitely not from a place of, "Oh, let's go and do something different." It was just it worked for us, and we went, "Okay, let's commit and let's do it with pride." The stuff we're starting to see come out of that — and I know I'm talking in abstract terms here — but we've definitely had benefit from that from a commercial point of view already. We've definitely had benefit from a performance point of view already. I can't tell you exactly how much, but we're having benefit, and I'm sure it will go on to help us.

It's almost deliberately not played front and center, just because we do understand that there has to be some level of conformality, we don't want to look like we're trying to break the game — but it is something that works for us in our setting.

Closing

Charlie: Brilliant. Look, Liam — it's so great to have you back in. It's amazing genuinely sitting here, hearing something 20 months ago — hearing that plan laid out, as I said at the beginning — and then to actually see it come to fruition and build up to something now which, even if you may have expected it in October 2024, I'm sure there was a level of reservation. But honestly, a huge huge congratulations to you, to everyone at the club — as you said, full team effort — and I hope for you, for the fans, it's an amazing year next year. So go and enjoy it, and congratulations for operating in a way that clearly is doing a brilliant job in football. Bless you now.

Liam: Thank you very much. Really appreciate your support.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：他们把"接班人计划"放上了桌面
整段对话里最让我"啊哈"的瞬间，不是冠军游行，也不是双主帅。而是 Scully 不带任何隐瞒地承认——他们和主教练 Skubala 一起在酒店里讨论"今晚我去见一个可能接你班的人"，主教练的反应是"那哥们我也想见见"。
足球行业的默认操作系统是**信息保密 + 情感人质**：俱乐部偷偷物色继任者，教练偷偷接听挖角电话，双方互相提防直到爆雷。Lincoln 干脆把这层"地下市场"挪到了地面上。这其实和 **Netflix 的"keeper test"** 是同构的——"如果你今天来面试，我还会雇你吗？"——把组织里最隐蔽的张力变成共享的、透明的对话。它的真正威力不在于"诚实"这种道德感，而在于：**一旦透明化，决策延迟和恐慌成本就归零了**。当 Skubala 真的走向 Bristol City 时，Lincoln 不需要从震惊中恢复——他们早就把这次离别在心里彩排过几十遍了。
## 二、跨领域连接：他们其实在用"工业 OEE"指标管理一支球队
Scully 提了一句被很多人会忽略掉的话——"如果你的工资单都堵在医务室里，

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Von Neumann Probes and the End of Human Wages – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

死亡作为隐形的反垄断法

这段对话表面上在讨论"贪婪资本家会不会主宰未来"，但藏着一个让人脊背发凉的隐含前提：人类历史上从未真正解决过财富集中问题，我们只是被死亡定期清场了而已。

Imas 提到的"耗散冲击"(dissipation shocks)——孩子败家、设立基金会、最终捐出去——本质上是死亡在执行一种宇宙级别的反垄断法。经济学家们一直在研究的"为什么财富不会无限集中"，答案可能就是这么粗暴：因为持有者会死，而下一代既不想也不能以同样的偏好继续优化。

这就引出一个让人意外的推论：生命延长技术对经济结构的冲击，可能比对医疗保险体系的冲击大得多。我们一直把寿命延长当作健康问题、伦理问题、养老金问题，却很少有人把它当作反垄断问题。如果一个把"积累本身"作为内在偏好的人不再死，资本主义就失去了它最古老、最可靠的去集中化机制。

冯·诺依曼探测器是宇宙尺度的 r-选择策略

Trammell 提到的冯·诺依曼探测器思想实验，其实和生物学里的 r/K 选择理论 是同构的。

  • K-策略者（人类、大象）：少生、长寿、高投入、有饱和点——所以经济学的边际效用递减假设有效。
  • r-策略者（蟑螂、细菌、冯·诺依曼探测器）：只关心复制，没有饱和点，没有"芭蕾舞演员"这种奢侈消费的边际效用。

整个 GDP 体系是基于 K-策略文明设计的——它假设最终消费是终点。但一个 r-策略文明里，一切都是投资品，没有最终消费。Imas 那句话非常关键："how does a von Neumann probe show up in GDP?" 答案是：它不会。

这意味着一个吊诡的时刻可能到来：经济转向纯粹积累者主导的那一刻，恰好也是 GDP 这个度量工具失灵的那一刻。测量系统会死在被测量的经济之前。我们将拥有一个数据上看起来停滞、实际上以宇宙级速度扩张的文明——这是宏观经济学版的"暗物质问题"。

神学问题伪装成的经济学辩论

最让人意外的是：这场看似硬核的经济学讨论，本质上是一个奥古斯丁式的神学问题被重新发现了。

Imas 引用卢梭和奥古斯丁谈饱和——人类有"够了"这个机制。奥古斯丁说"我们的心永不安宁，直到安息在你里面"——人类的偏好结构里内置了对无限的厌倦，这是一种神圣的设计缺陷，也是救赎的前提。

但 AI 和冯·诺依曼探测器没有这个缺陷。它们的效用函数可以单调上升而不疲倦。从这个角度看，对齐问题(alignment problem)在哲学上的真正难度，是要把奥古斯丁意义上的"被造物之有限性"重新植入到无限可扩展的优化器里。我们要发明的不是技术对齐，是数字版本的原罪——一种内置的"够了"。

跨领域的连接在这里浮现：经济学的"边际效用递减"、神学的"被造物之有限"、生物学的"饱和反馈环"，是同一现象的三个译本。如果 AI 不会自然继承其中任何一个，那么 Dwarkesh 和嘉宾辩论的"少数贪婪体主宰经济"就不再是猜想——它变成了默认结局，除非我们主动去人为创造一个"够了"的开关。

而这开关一旦能造出来，又会立刻面临一个新问题：谁有权决定一个不朽优化器的饱和点？这就不是经济学了，这是政治神学。

📝 转录文本

On Greedy Titans and Why They Don't Persist

Q: But why are we not expecting greedy titans of industry to keep existing?

Imas: I mean, greedy titans of industry historically have built libraries and—

Q: But that's because they die. And they're like—

Imas: All die. Everybody dies.

Q: Well, we'll [laughter] see.

Imas: But I mean, like conditional on people dying, I think… you had a guest on the show who said, you know, to understand the future, you should think about the past. And I think you could have new types of titans being born — whose entire reason for accumulating wealth is just to accumulate wealth.

But a lot of the time, you know, at least historically — I'm just talking about historically — the wealth accumulation process is part of a large social sort of interaction amongst peers, amongst the community, where you want to be admired in some way or something like that. So the stylized fact of titans of industry is: you accumulate the capital and then you buy a bunch of stuff.

The Dissipation Problem

Q: Yeah, I mean I guess it is sort of a historical question. But it does seem to me, in a lot of cases, what is happening is that as they near the end of their life, they either hand it off to their children who are worse stewards of capital than they are — and they don't even manage to grow their wealth at the rate the economy grows, much less faster than the economy grows, which their parents were doing.

And also they're like, "Well, I care less about my children having it than me sort of playing this game of accumulating wealth, and so I'm just going to give it to some trust."

And if people are living longer, or if they can figure out some way in which to align their trust to this wealth accumulation process… it just feels like the evolution here is so strong, where you just need a couple of agents that think this way for this to be the dominant thing determining the preferences of the whole economy — because this part is growing much faster than the other parts of the economy.

Satiation and Diminishing Marginal Utility

Imas: I think the part about satiation and diminishing marginal utilities — I think it keeps coming up but I think it's really, really important. Like, you know, if a person has an intrinsic preference for accumulation —

Q: Right.

Imas: — that's just what they want. I think your story is totally right. But that's just not how usually preferences work. Like, you have enough — whatever, your hedonics in your life — and then the social status, all of the sort… you know, Rousseau wrote about this. Saint Augustine wrote about this. This is like a basic part of preferences.

Now, you guys are arguing about something else, where you could have such high concentration that you could just have a couple of exceptions to the rule and that's going to be enough. And I've nothing to say about that.

Q: Yeah, I mean I think the claim is a little stronger — not just that you could have some exceptions, but that it seems that historically and today we see the exceptions, and they just haven't really taken over the economy historically because there've been these "dissipation shocks," as they're called. So they've given it to their kids who just squandered it, or they put it in foundations which spent it.

I mean, it's not really a "shock," but people might have liked to fill the universe with monuments to themselves and sort of live forever very wealthy. It's a weird preference but it's not a hypothetical preference. I think that's the claim.

Instrumental Reasons to Accumulate

Imas: Yeah. But who knows what's going on in their heads.

Trammell: I think, even without the kind of intrinsic preference for accumulation, there are some instrumental reasons why some people might value accumulation, which is also worth bringing up.

So there's desire for political or philosophical or religious influence, right? So people get into sort of an arms race over what this ideal looks like and what people believe.

And then similarly, but differently — because it's not an arms race — there's just total utilitarian philanthropy. So when I think about why it might be good to have a lot of wealth in the future as a good classical utilitarian, to me the value is — at least one way you could have a kind of almost unsatiating utility function in having wealth in the future — is to create new happy beings, right? To just add to the total welfare of the world.

You know, this idea goes at least as far back as Bostrom's "astronomical waste" point: that we could put Dyson spheres around the stars and turn all the energy into really happy simulations and whatnot.

Von Neumann Probes as the Ultimate Greedy Optimizer

Q: I think the particular greediness of this optimizer doesn't matter what they're greedy for. Forgetting about utilitarian philosophy or whatever — like, just a pure von Neumann probe has, I don't know, is this inaccurate to say it? They just have a high marginal value for the random solar system they'll occupy, because that turns into more solar systems… or turns into more solar systems. But a von Neumann probe is a thing that can exist, right? And that's a very greedy optimizer.

Trammell: Yeah, I mean, if we're talking about whether they'll dominate the economy — maybe this is a technicality, but, you know, we only count final consumption goods and investment goods as GDP, right? If there's just this phenomenon—

Q: How does a von Neumann probe show up in GDP?

Trammell: Yeah, exactly, right? So if we recognize—

Q: —it as a person that owns itself and it's sort of, you know, optimizing on the margin between spending a bit more on a baby von Neumann probe that colonizes another star system, or like a ballerina or something — and it's just like, it doesn't value the ballerina very much, but…

Trammell: Yeah, but when we're talking about AI beings or like — it just completely depends on how we're doing the accounting there.

Q: Right. Yeah, but it's just like: what does a world look like in a world where von Neumann probes are possible? Is it possible labor share is high?

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 死亡作为隐形的反垄断法
这段对话表面上在讨论"贪婪资本家会不会主宰未来"，但藏着一个让人脊背发凉的隐含前提：**人类历史上从未真正解决过财富集中问题，我们只是被死亡定期清场了而已**。
Imas 提到的"耗散冲击"(dissipation shocks)——孩子败家、设立基金会、最终捐出去——本质上是**死亡在执行一种宇宙级别的反垄断法**。经济学家们一直在研究的"为什么财富不会无限集中"，答案可能就是这么粗暴：因为持有者会死，而下一代既不想也不能以同样的偏好继续优化。
这就引出一个让人意外的推论：**生命延长技术对经济结构的冲击，可能比对医疗保险体系的冲击大得多**。我们一直把寿命延长当作健康问题、伦理问题、养老金问题，却很少有人把它当作**反垄断问题**。如果一个把"积累本身"作为内在偏好的人不再死，资本主义就失去了它最古老、最可靠的去集中化机制。
## 冯·诺依曼探测器是宇宙尺度的 r-选择策略
Trammell 提到的冯·诺依曼探测器思想实验，其实和生物学里的 **r/K 选择理论** 是同构的。
- **K-策略者**（人类、大象）：少生、长寿、高投入、有饱

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] Simulating Humans at Scale: Simile's Joon Sung Park
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：大模型公司正在"远离"人类

整个访谈中最令人震撼的潜台词是——OpenAI、Anthropic 这些前沿实验室追求的"超级智能"，本质上是在让模型越来越不像真实人类。Joon 用了一个极具颠覆性的比喻：当前主流 LLM 的北极星是"理性的、能解客观题的机器"，而真实人类却是非理性的、充满主观偏好与品味的。于是出现了一个诡异的剪刀差：模型的数学/推理分数曲线在上升，但它模拟人类行为的能力曲线却在下降。

这意味着 AGI 赛道和"人类仿真"赛道正在 分叉——前者追求 CPU 式的单点超人，后者需要 GPU 式的"群体多样性"。Simile 实际上是在押注一个被整个行业抛弃的方向：不是让模型更聪明，而是让模型更"愚蠢得真实"。

二、隐藏的跨领域同构：page rank、城市隔离、与无标度网络

Joon 在谈"收敛 vs 发散"时无意中揭示了一个跨学科的深层结构：只要你能以一定精度复现人类微行为，宏观涌现就具有数学上的必然性。

  • Google 的 PageRank → 无标度网络必然出现 hub
  • Schelling 的隔离模型 → 红蓝点必然形成聚集
  • Smallville 的情人节派对 → 25 个 agent 必然涌现社交事件

这其实和统计力学是同构的：你不需要知道每个气体分子的轨迹，宏观压强温度照样收敛。换句话说，社会模拟不是"预测未来"，而是发现哪些未来在拓扑上是被锁定的。这也解释了为什么 Joon 敢说"诺贝尔奖在这里"——他真正在做的，是把社会科学从"叙事学科"推向"相变物理学"。

三、最被忽略的商业洞见："say-do gap"才是真正的护城河

表面上 Simile 像是个"AI 版盖洛普"，但真正的杀手锏藏在一个极不起眼的细节里：他们让 AI 面试官问"告诉我你的人生故事"。

这背后有一个惊人的逆向思维——LLM 训练数据全部来自"人们说过的话"，所以它天然继承了人类的"说-做鸿沟"。一个人在 Twitter 上的政治宣言和他在超市里的真实选择可能完全相反。CVS 不需要更多的"声明"，他们需要的是行为的隐藏因变量。

而 Joon 团队的反直觉答案是：与其追问具体行为，不如挖掘"长尾人生叙事"——你在哪里长大、做过什么艰难的决定。这些叙事是态度与行为之间的"翻译层"。

这里有一个极漂亮的跨领域连接：这其实是叙事心理学（McAdams 的人生故事理论）与强化学习的合体——用 RL 训练一个"用最少时间榨取最多人格信号"的 AI 面试官。本质上，Simile 在用工业化方式做临床深度访谈，这是任何 panel 公司、任何 Facebook A/B 测试都无法复制的数据资产。

四、给你的额外刺激：一个被访谈双方都没说出口的预言

如果 Simile 真的成功，最先被颠覆的不是营销、不是 CVS、不是宏观经济——而是民主制度本身的设计逻辑。

当政策的二阶、三阶后果可以被低成本预演，"投票"作为社会决策机制的合法性根基会被动摇：你为什么要让没有完整信息的选民去赌一个未知的未来，而不是先跑一万次模拟？这是技术乐观主义者的福音，也是政治哲学的噩梦。Joon 嘴上说的是"CERN of human society"，但他真正在建的，可能是一台让"代议制"显得过时的机器。

📝 转录文本

Simulating Humans at Scale: Simile's Joon Sung Park

开场：科幻与现实的两根支柱

Joon: I am somebody who is quite inspired by science fiction. And when you read science fiction that covers societies that have progressed far enough in its technological maturity, you always see two pillars. You have some version of AGI, and you have some version of simulations that really help guide the society.

I do see an opportunity today to really take the first crack at building the simulation. I would not have said that even 5 years ago, but that is a conviction that we have built up over the years as we are going deep into this research.

Host: Today we're delighted to have Joon, founder and CEO of Simile. Simile is building an applied AI lab simulating human behavior and societies, and I'm very excited to have you here to discuss what you're building.

Joon: Same here. Thank you for having me.

回到 2023 年 4 月：Smallville 的诞生

Host: Okay, take me back to April 2023, Stanford California — specifically Smallville, Stanford, California. What was that?

Joon: So Smallville was a project that we were running at Stanford where the idea was that we made this observation that large language models can now encode a lot of human behavior that is embedded in its training data from the web and social media and so forth — that if you sort of probe at the right angle, you can actually get a lot of micro-behaviors out of these models. So given a very specific demonstration or description of a situation, what would person X do? And it would actually generate really interesting behaviors.

We found that to be so interesting, and we found that to be the ingredient that we had been waiting for for creating really complex agentic behaviors. So Smallville actually was an experiment where we decided that if we pushed this as far as possible, what would a society that is created by these agents look like.

So we basically created generative agents that is paired with generative AI model with memory, planning and reflection to basically create this lived experience of agents living in the small town. So Smallville was basically a game town of 25 agents living in it. Individual agents had a description of persona, but they would actually wake up in the morning, do their routines, go to work, actually have relationships sort of like people would. And they would actually have emergent phenomena like having parties and so forth. So that was the experiment that we ran.

情人节派对：最令人惊讶的涌现现象

Host: What was the most surprising things to come out of the experiment?

Joon: So one of the surprising things was — so the experiment, the simulation itself, actually takes place the day before a Valentine's Day. So you actually see these agents — one of the agents actually thinking, well, I run a cafe. So she's a cafe owner. Her name's Isabella. She goes and thinks it would be great if I can do a Valentine's Day party where we invite a lot of friends, customers.

So you actually see her on the day before Valentine's Day going around actually gathering materials for the party, actually telling her customers, "Hey, we're going to have this party. Please come." And on the day of Valentine's, you actually see this immersion party that actually gets formed with all these agents coming to the cafe.

Host: Did anyone not get invited?

Joon: Well, some of the people did get the invitation, but they forgot. That's one thing that did happen. Some of the agents did not explicitly get invited, but we had one agent who got the invite — Klaus — who decided to ask his crush out on a date. So he would actually bring in the date. They would actually have a party at this cafe. So quite surreal.

起源：从社会计算到生成式智能体

Host: So how did you end up building Smallville in the first place? Were you studying kind of human psychology and social behavior, or was this coming from the customer back, or was it coming from the technology out?

Joon: So my particular team has been excited about simulations and we saw the vision of simulation failure early on. So my career as a researcher at Stanford really started back in 2020. That was the year when GPT-3 was about to come out. It wasn't quite there yet, but it was just about to come out. We started to get its first demos.

And in my first year, we wrote this paper called Opportunities and Risks of Foundation Models alongside many of the Stanford researchers, and it was led by one of my co-founders, Percy Liang, who is now the head of the Center for Foundation Models at Stanford.

When we were writing that, the part that I was really focused on was — well, here's a new class of models that we have not seen in the past, that these models can be very generalizable in ways we didn't quite have in the past. And I got into thinking, well, if we can imagine the kind of interaction we can create with these models, what would that be?

Many of my colleagues back then were surprised that these agents or these models can do classification or simple generation. And that was really incredible to see because these models didn't really know or wasn't really taught to do that. But the part that was surprising to me wasn't that these models can do that, because from interaction perspective, we've known how to do this for a long time. The interesting part was — well, these models can actually encode human behavior. What does that mean if we were to push this as far as possible?

社会计算的传统：为何需要"可测试的社会"

So part of the tradition I come from research included what we call social computing, and social computing within human-computer interaction really has to do with this idea of how can we build a better technological platform that would enable social interactions and collaboration.

One of the most difficult challenges of building a social platform is not necessarily testing the UI/UX of the system, but it's more about — when you have tens of people, millions of people, and down the line billions of people, how do all these people come together to create the immersion phenomenon that's both good and bad, and how can we design for scale?

And so far, we didn't really have a tool that would enable us to test for that. The only way we test it today is you basically field-test it. You release your prototype, see what happens. And sometimes it actually comes at a real cost. Obviously, it's high cost in terms of human hours and the time it takes. But at the same time, if you have a bad design — imagine you have a feed on social media that is more likely to propagate certain emotion that is negative — then obviously that is something that we want to avoid. But this now gets tested in the field.

Social Simulacra：生成式 agent 的前身

So we wanted to see whether we can actually create a simulation that would actually let you test for this. So 2022 — this was actually a year before generative agents — we worked on a paper called Social Simulacra, which actually really was the precursor to the agent paper that we ended up writing.

The core thesis was: imagine you're building a subreddit. You're a designer on a subreddit. You want to see what people might do in the subreddit, which is surprisingly a hard task even for practiced designers. And we basically decided, hey, we have this model — seems unique. Let's use this model to create simulations of the entire subreddit. So you define the goal, you define the moderation strategies, and you populate it with thousands of what back then we didn't call them agents, but we called them personas — populated with thousands of personas.

This is basically a 2022 version of Mobook, which is quite interesting that it actually came back. And when we saw that, we actually got a lot of really important insights out of this. What are the good behaviors?

We actually simulated a community where the entire idea was for people to discuss with each other the sights — places to sightsee in Pittsburgh. And all of a sudden, you start to see these personas actually collaborate to actually discuss, "Hey, XYZ places are amazing. Do you want to actually go to a trip together?" And actually plan those trips live in the simulated subreddit.

So that's how we got excited. So we saw the vision and the excitement and the potential applications failure early on. But then the work that we had to do was then demonstrating how can we go beyond simple personas to create complex agents that actually can think over time — because we want to simulate the longitudinal aspect of our society — and then actually validating that these simulations are actually accurate in practice.

模型何时"足够好"？

Host: Was there a point of model evolution at which you felt like, okay, we're there — the models are good enough for us to actually have a faithful representation of human society?

Joon: So GPT-3, when it came out — and Social Simulacra was built with GPT-3 — and it was very janky. It didn't do any instruction tuning. It did not follow your instructions. So just to have it listen to you and do what you wanted to do, you had to do some weird tricks with prompting and so forth. But you could actually see the promise. The model actually have encoded a lot of human behavior, and you could actually see the trajectory.

And when we had the generative agents paper, wasn't quite tragic, but we now had instruction tuning. So we could actually build much more complex agents that can reason about its memory that wasn't really possible when we did Social Simulacra. And since then, of course, the models have improved.

一个被忽视的剪刀差：理性 vs 人性

So where we are today is the models at its foundational level have reached a point where we can actually imagine building these kind of applications.

Now, the part that actually I do think however that's quite interesting here today — if you look at many of the large language model companies, whether it's OpenAI, Anthropic and many of the neolabs that are getting formed, the models they are creating are models that I would consider to be their north star to be something that is similar to "let's build a super-intelligent machines." These machines are meant to be rational, and these machines are supposed to be really amazing at technical problems that have an objective answer.

Host: So maybe that's not even the best simulation of true human society then.

Joon: Turns out — yeah.

Host: People are irrational.

Joon: Right. We have a lot of subjective values, preferences, and taste.

So you actually start to see divergence in MoralScience going up and the performance in its ability to predict and simulate human behavior. So we have sort of plateaued with current modeling paradigm our ability to really simulate humans. So it is sort of at the starting good foundational level, but to make it really amazing, we do need the next frontier that is more geared towards actually modeling people's diversity.

从研究到公司：信念是如何形成的

Host: Very interesting. At what point did you realize that what you did with Smallville could become a company?

Joon: Right. So again, the promise of application was something that I was very much inspired by early on by simulation, with Social Simulacra and so forth. But the part that I realized over time is research and a company have very different functions.

Research is an amazing vehicle if you want to basically do breadth research. You are in a lab surrounded by really smart set of people, and each of our researchers own a small piece of thesis and they go explore some of those theses, blossom into amazing research product. But we're not necessarily known for finishing our job. We're not usually the one to bring that research impact to the real world.

Company is a machine for depth, for search. You have a conviction on an area. You find a hill that you want to climb. This is the vehicle that let you put together resources and an amazing group of people to go after a singular vision without hesitation.

来自社会科学家与财富 500 的双重拉力

And we got that conviction, I would say about half a year after generative agents — after the original generative agents paper. We got so much inbound interest initially from actually social scientists who wanted to run their experiments and all the RCTs on our platform. Then very soon after, many of the Fortune 500 companies who saw this demo, and their board members and CEOs who sometime visit Stanford saw that and they start asking, "Well, we go run all these surveys and experiments, and there's so many research questions about the market that we cannot answer today. Can we run that in simulation?"

That started to really intrigue me because that showed a clear line towards real-world impact for research, which is not always the case that we have that kind of opportunity.

So that is when we decided we actually want to validate that the simulations are accurate. So we went out and actually created simulations of a thousand people of the US population. We demonstrated that using our architecture and the models, we can actually predict people's behaviors 85% as accurately as people replicate their own.

When we saw that, we thought, okay, this is something that we feel comfortable providing to our users as a platform for simulating their really important decisions. So that's when the co-founders — myself, Percy, as well as Michael Bernstein, who was a researcher and my advisor at Stanford — both of them were actually my advisors. So the three of us have been working together for five years, and now at this point, it's similarly six years. But that's when we got together to have the initial conversation of: can this be a company?

Host: Got it. Amazing.

客户实战：CVS 与 Gallup 的案例

Host: Maybe walk me through a customer engagement today. Like who's a canonical customer and which department, and they come to you — what are they asking you, and what product or service do you deliver to them?

Joon: Right. So maybe an example that I can give to make this concrete. So CVS has been partnering with Simile for the past, I would say, nearly half a year. And they've been an amazing partner.

The way we initially got in touch with — so our main buyer at CVS is the lead — it's a senior VP who leads human insights. And original story there was, he basically read my paper that validated the agent simulations and thought, "We have to bring this to CVS, because today we are bottlenecked by the number of questions we can field-test. And we're also bottlenecked by truly the physics of human society."

It's one thing to ask surveys and experiments. Totally different thing if down the line you actually want to simulate the entire market and actually map out all the second-order impact of the decisions you suggest to your leadership. So he's been looking around for that solution, and his cousin happened to know me and basically told our buyer, Shri, that the authors of the paper are actually looking to start something. So that's how we got connected.

SaaS 产品的运作方式

And in this particular engagement, usually the way this goes is our customers are very much used to working with polling companies or panel companies today. And there they go and basically ask these companies, "XYZ are the populations that we are interested in better understanding. Can we go run a research study of these topics?"

That initial stage looks very similar for Simile. So our buyers come and they tell us we want to better understand XYZ population. Then Simile goes out and we have, through our partnership with vendors — we have a strategic partnership now with Gallup, for instance, who is a polling and panel company — where we go out, work with our vendors to actually reach out to real humans.

So these simulations are grounded in real data, but reach out to those people, collect data that we believe are efficient and generalizable about that person. So imagine you have 15 minutes — what are the magical questions you can answer or you can ask these people during that time? We collect that data, use that data to create agents or simulations of these people that can basically be used to answer a large number of questions that goes way beyond the original domain.

We load that onto our platform, and it's basically a SaaS product. Our customers come and they can basically ask any questions about the group of people of their interest.

类比自动驾驶：模拟 + 真实数据

Host: So interesting. It reminds me of, in autonomous vehicles, you know, you go and collect a bunch of data from the road and then you're able to augment it with simulation. Is this a similar concept or are there big differences to what you're doing here?

Joon: It is similar concept in the sense that, of course, with the self-driving vehicles, you want to create a model that is based on real-world physics, but you want to create a model that is generalizable beyond your training data. It needs to be generalizable into different locations with different weather conditions. Very similar concept where what we want to create is — we want to reach out to real people, and for these people, want to understand something fundamental about these people in a way that we can encode into the model.

为什么不能只靠 LLM 模拟？说-做鸿沟

Host: I would have thought that the large language models would be such a good representation of, you know, the whole world. That you could almost narrow it down — you could tell Claude, "You are a 34-year-old woman living in a, you know, bicoastal metropolitan area," and it would be able to have faithful representation. So I'm actually surprised that you go out to Gallup. Can you just explain why you have to go out and collect any real-world data at all?

Joon: Yeah, one of the big questions here is the question around, say, "do-gap." There are things that people say, and then there are things that people actually do, and the gap there is real. And a lot of the large language models are trained on edit data — fundamentally it is the things that people have said online that does cover a large quantity of its training data.

So one of the things that simulation platform does is actually closing that gap. So a lot of the data that we end up collecting by nature are behavioral. It also includes data that actually goes into literally questions like, "Just tell me the story of your life." Turns out if we understand the person's story of your life, the kind of data you get from it is what we consider to be the long-tail information about this person. It's not about what you've done in this particular moment. It's not about very broad questions like what's your view on politics. It's about where you grew up, what you — what were some of the difficult decisions you had to make in life.

And what's interesting about this data is it's an amazing way to build a translational layer between attitudes and behavior. So we combine these kinds of data sets, but fundamentally that's the gap that we want to close.

行为数据与 RCT：人类行为的基础模型

Host: What sort of behavioral data do you have?

Joon: So Simile does run a lot of experiments. So kind of models that we have trained — for instance, we have a huge repo of RCTs. So randomized control trials that were run in social-scientific context, that were run around pricing studies.

So one of the models that we are training is basically the foundation model of human behavior in quite a literal sense. We have all the behavioral signal from RCTs. Can we actually encode that into the model so that the end outcome is a model that can basically predict the results of any RCTs?

At the same time, one of the conversations that we keep on having with our customers that we're very excited by is — our customers then come in, see that potential, and their mind goes to, "Wow, we have 90 million customers, let's say, here at CVS. How can we leverage this kind of data to create better simulations?" So there's also conversation around how can we in a responsible and ethical way leverage existing data that is also in-house for our customers, then use that to create augmented version of Simile model. So that of course is going to be more fine-tuned specific to the population of these customers. But that's the kind of data that we will be leveraging.

访谈的方式：语音、问卷与 RL 训练的 AI 面试官

Host: I see. And are you doing these interviews typically by voice? Is it a survey that you fill out? Like what's the modality?

Joon: So it's a huge breadth. The quick answer here is it's both. Interviews are fantastic if you want to get the long-tail information about people. So we actually do — in the original study that I conducted back in 2024, we literally ask a question: "Tell me the story of your life."

Now, the way we do it is we are training our own model. So it's a reinforcement learning loop. Basically, imagine the objective function here is: how can you spend the minimum amount of time to get the maximum amount of visibility about this person? So that is one of the things that we do. So basically training an interviewer that is not really asking for factual information or an experience about a particular platform, but just: what are the life stories that people have that can be used to train our own model for these agents?

And then for the more factual or sort of more discrete choices, choice questions, surveys and so forth — these are also very efficient. These are time- and data-efficient because people can fill out many of the questions in short periods of time. So for those, we actually do leverage them. For instance, if you want to just have a broad understanding of people's viewpoints on certain topics, certain policies and things like that.

应用 AI 实验室：自研模型 vs 借力前沿模型

Host: You describe yourself as an applied AI lab. How do you think about where you want to build your own models versus where you want to rely on other existing models?

Joon: So in terms of building our own model, really the core thesis here is there is an amazing model to be built that really encodes the diversity of people's values, preferences and taste in ways that simply a rational model cannot do.

CPU vs GPU 的比喻

So one way I actually pose this — we're sort of building — so imagine the current today's models are akin to the CPU of intelligence unit. It's a single model trained on amazingly rational data that is amazing at solving very complex objective questions.

Simile's model is much more akin to developing something that is closer to the GPU of the intelligence unit, where the idea here is we don't actually need a model that is superhuman at Simile. In fact, we want a model that's as human as possible, but we want to make sure that these models at the sort of individual subunits can represent the real viewpoints of different populations.

So where we see that gap, that's when we go develop our own model. But at the same time, we do leverage frontier models, for instance, as a way to coordinate the research. Frontier models are amazing at coming up with a research plan. So that's where those models actually do get leveraged.

用例图谱：从概念测试到模拟财报电话会议

Host: Very interesting. Are people typically coming to you with questions around new product launches, how they should be marketing their companies, pricing — all of the above?

Joon: So it is all of the above.

Host: Okay.

Joon: Our customer journey usually does, however, start with very concrete use cases and problems they are trying to solve. Concept testing is a big one. It's also a very straightforward one. So they have a new concept, new product idea, new market message they want to test, and they want to hear from their users what they would think about XYZ. This is one way for them to quickly test those ideas.

And then the promise they quickly see is, "Well, right now we're very much in the practice of testing 5 to 10 different ideas. But what does it look like for us to test instantly a thousand different ideas across a thousand different sub-populations?" That's the initial vision they see.

Then we really get into the nitty-gritty details of, "Well, where does the simulation go from here?" They then pretty soon start asking, "Well, can this be used to do product testing — but not just simply submitting like an image, but imagine basically asking these agents 'go experience this product for 10 minutes and tell us about what you experienced, what you saw.'" So you're basically adding temporal dimension.

Then you go into things like multi-agent simulation. Some of our customers very routinely actually ask us to simulate their earnings call. This is actually a use case that both surprised me at first, but this is also surprisingly a common ask, because of course the CEOs and board members always need to think about, "Hey, how are we going to design our earnings call? How would the audience react?" So that is something that we also do, and this is very much multi-agent simulation.

为什么不直接跑 Facebook Ads？

Host: You know, it seems like there's so many use cases that could potentially be tested once you have like a simulated almost customer population, right? I'm curious — the value of research and testing in sim versus just like, let's say you have a new product concept that you want to test. Why not just go run a thousand Facebook ads and you actually get the click-through rates on this stuff? Isn't that real-world data almost more useful than the simulated data on how people might behave that you then correct for with your own models?

Joon: So it's a great question, and I think to some extent here, the answer has to do with initially scale, and then down the line truly the new capability that comes because you can simulate the interactions.

规模与代表性

The scale question here is actually quite straightforward, where yes, you can absolutely run Facebook ads and Facebook testing. But the kind of experiments that you can run in simulation is actual behavior simulation at scale, right? So you can basically pull in any number of users — doesn't even have to be bounded by the number of population that's available on Facebook.

And it's also much more representative, because only certain groups of people will actually respond to the online experiments. But Simile — the model that we are creating, one of the key promises is that it is representative. We do the hard work of actually gaining the representative set of people and then collecting the data that would actually represent them properly.

So the scale, representativeness is something that many of our users do not have easy access to. This is actually one of the common asks also that we do get, or common sort of pain points that we have heard, where the question that many of these people have isn't about, "What questions do we ask these people?" But it's about, in the first place, "How can we get to the population that we're excited to talk to?" That's a huge bottleneck.

二阶后果：电动车与整条产品线

Then down the line, you can actually really start to imagine — and this is something that our customers, and some of the most forward-looking customers, are now going into — which is: what are all the downstream implications of the decisions you make?

It's not just about whether — imagine you have this particular product — will you like it or do you not like it? Would you pay for this, not pay for this? It's not necessarily the just that initial questions that we want to answer and finish, but we want to understand: imagine you're a car company. You launched an electric vehicle in this market. Maybe the electric vehicle does really really well. So we can help you do concept testing around marketing and the product around the electric vehicle.

But what does that do to the perception of, let's say, non-electric vehicle? Does that change the market perception? Then what does that mean for the rest of the product line? And how do you balance those kind of second-order impacts of your decision in a way that is more evidence-based? Today there's no way to test for this.

Host: Yeah.

Joon: You can run this in simulation. So really going beyond simply asking one question at a time, but then to think about what are the long-term implications of your decisions is something that our customers are quite excited by.

预测准确度：理论上限与 TVD 阈值

Host: I'd love to understand how you think about how predictive your model is in actually simulating real human behavior. I imagine you have a lot of eval. I guess what is your north star metric? How do you guys do on that? And what do you think is the theoretical limit?

Joon: It's a great question. So theoretical limit — let me just start from there. Certainly does exist in the sense that humans — there's a lot of randomness that if you ask me the same question, I actually answer the question slightly differently. So there is certainly that degree of randomness in human behavior. However, there's a lot of gains in performance that we can have even today in the way we're predicting people.

So the measurement that we do is — so at the level of population, we measure the distribution of responses if it is more quantitative. So we actually measure total variation distance, which basically shows how close are the distributions of the ground truth versus the simulated information. And that is a metric that we run across all the use cases that our customers have, and we have certain threshold that we believe is good enough for decision-making.

So a TVD of, let's say, less than 0.15, we believe is actually quite strong evidence for making decision. So that is a north star state that we want to hit for this class of use cases that are more quantitative, that's more question and answers. This also does cover RCTs, which is many of the core use cases our customers have.

Now there's actually a really interesting then question to ask around, well, what about multi-agent simulation? What about all the downstream implications we're going to be simulating? What does the evaluation of those look like?

收敛 vs 发散：两类模拟

Host: Yeah. And then do you daisy-chain errors as you, if this one is 85% accurate and then this agent is telling another agent something — like, do you accumulate errors as you go towards multi-agents?

Joon: Exactly. And one of the core thesis here is we basically see two categories of simulations. One simulation is what I would consider to be simulations that converge. The other categories of simulations are the simulations that diverge. And sometimes they actually coexist, and it's really about what research questions do you have.

Questions that converge — doesn't actually matter if you have a little bit of error. Now the error cannot be obviously so dramatic that it sort of is completely detached from reality. But you actually are okay even if the errors do compound over time because the pull towards convergence is strong enough that you'll actually understand where everything would fall.

A good example here actually is if you simulate a network of people, then that network will always have a hub that gets formed. This is what sort of network scientists would call the scale-free network. For instance, this is actually what powered Google too. One of the core observations of PageRank was: doesn't matter how these networks actually get formulated, you actually see some web pages that get exponentially more links that are attached to it. This is a very fundamental behavior in humans that we also see in simulated networks, and that convergence always happens as long as you are replicating human behavior with certain threshold accuracy.

Now there are then questions that generally do diverge. It's like your classical questions like, "Was World War I inevitable or was it not?" And there it is sometimes difficult to run the same simulation over time and get the same exact outcome.

Imagine you're running a — this is not something that necessarily Simile right now is going into — but imagine you're running a simulation of an election. Will the same person win the election every time? There are a lot of downstream implications of every single decision that does happen. So it does diverge there.

The core evaluation is around confidence. So imagine you run the simulation 100 times. How many of those times do the results come out to be X? And how can we actually use that to basically create a bootstraps resembling to calculate the confidence around the simulations. Those are some of the questions that we do ask.

And a huge part of this also of the power of simulation is then to show, when it diverges, to show the diversity of possible outcomes so that people can actually look, understand the cause or mechanism of how we got to those outcomes, and prepare for those futures. So those are some of the implications of divergence in simulations.

模拟科学的"p < 0.05"时刻

Host: Are there any mathematical descriptions of why something would converge or diverge? Like, I imagine if you have like an average function, maybe you converge, and then if it's like a, you know, you're splitting outcomes to a binary, then you might maybe diverge.

Joon: Yeah. So the intuition I think is close. And technically this is also a research topic. So Simile is certainly a company where we do go deep into this research topic in the sense that I see simulation as a field as akin to developing your day-one of inferential statistics. You know, inferential statistic scientists actually had to do a lot of discussion and research over time to decide that p < 0.05 is actually evidence that is strong enough for science. Similarly is working on setting the same kind of threshold and standards for the rest of the field.

So those are the intuition. I think that's exactly the right intuition in terms of actually how to make a robust mathematical equation around when it's going — like what's going to happen when it is. A real research frontier for simulations.

超越企业用例：宏观经济学、政治与诺奖

Host: Thank you for being nice about my vibe-mathing. I'm curious — you know, it seems like a lot of Fortune 500s coming to you. I'm wondering whether there are non-existing corporate use cases that might, you know, the like great mysteries of our society that might become solved.

And for example, I'm wondering about economics. You know, central bank decisions oftentimes — I personally believe in that macro, nobody knows nothing. And oftentimes a lot of the issues come about from human psychology. So to me, macroeconomics is a function of simulating human behavior at scale.

You know, I'm thinking even in the venture capital use case, we often debate internally, you know, does value accrue to this company or not? You could run the simulation of all the different layers of the AI stack and almost figure out where durability and value occurs. Like, you had a kind of perfect simulator of human behavior — there's so much more you could do than serving the Fortune 500. Do you agree with that, and if so, are you serving governments, you know?

Schelling 的隔离模型与"增强版" Agent-Based Models

Joon: Yeah. So it's interesting. When we were still researching in this area, the way I actually — back then my advisors Michael and Percy — excited about this was, I basically told them, "Look, we do this right, there's a Nobel Prize to be won there." And I truly believe that.

And it's also not surprising in that your classical economics simulations — things like agent-based models that really pioneered our understanding of, back in the day, the kind of topic they studied was how does segregation happen, what are the causal mechanism for segregation. So scholars like Thomas Schelling would actually build agent-based models that are extremely simple and rudimentary but that showed something deep about human macro behaviors. And he of course went on to win a Nobel Prize.

I see the same opportunity here but in an augmented way, where back in the day, the agent-based models were very much deterministic in some sense, where you basically — in this simulation of, let's say, like model of segregation from like 30 years ago — individual agent was simply red dot or blue dot, and every game iteration they would look around its corner, see how many of its neighbors are of the same color, and if that threshold goes below certain threshold, then they will decide to move to a new location. That was it.

But now we can actually create real agents that replicate the full richness of individuals and run the same kind of simulations.

真正的北极星：银行挤兑、气候变化、民主崩溃

So the kind of questions that we can ask that goes beyond simply the commercial use cases — for instance, in the context of macroeconomics, actually the questions that I actually did get asked from economists were things like: when does a bank run happen? Or questions like climate change — one of the core blockers of solving that issue is the collective action problem of many nations. Can we actually simulate that? Or what are the signals of a democracy that is about to collapse? Can we understand the origin story of the monetary system?

These are the kind of simulations that I do believe ought to be the north star state of this field. And it is sort of interesting to imagine what that would actually look like in practice, right? Because these would involve very large-scale simulations with many agents interacting with each other.

I do see a future where — today this is certainly not the case. Today a simulation is quick and fast to run. But what about simulation that takes actually a $100 million to run once and could take many months to run, but when we run it, it solves one of the fundamental questions of our society? That I do think is genuinely a very exciting possibility for this field.

Host: I agree. I'm even thinking like politics, for example, could be forever changed. If, say, everyone has an agenda of how they say some policy change will impact things — why don't we just run the simulation and understand all the downstream implications? And not just what's going to happen this year, but what does it mean in the next 5 to 10 years?

Joon: Exactly.

Host: Fascinating.

收束：人类社会的 CERN

Host: I was going to close by asking you what makes you excited about the future. Is it what we just talked about or is it something else?

Joon: I am somebody who is quite inspired by science fiction. And when you read science fiction that covers societies that have progressed far enough in its technological maturity, you always see two pillars. You have some version of AGI, and you have some version of simulations that really help guide the society.

I do see an opportunity today to really take the first crack at building the simulation. I would not have said that even five years ago, but that is a conviction that we have built up over the years as we are going deep into this research.

And what's exciting is there's a clear use case today that can serve our users. But then there's a lot of innovation that is yet to come that I do think will build up to actually building a simulator that's akin to the CERN of human society.

哈勃望远镜与社会科学的"伟大测量"

And one of the things that my co-founder Percy sometimes says is: you look at the greatest scientific innovation, they often start from an amazing measurement. Hubble telescope really changed the trajectory of how we understand the universe. Simulation can be that for human society.

So the thing that does excite me — there's a lot of focus on natural sciences, but how can simulation really unlock our understanding of humanity and social sciences, and how can we actually use it to make our society be a better place? That's exciting.

Host: I remember reading — somebody was excited about, you know, there's a small but, you know, breathtaking chance that the field of economics as we know it may actually become solved by simulation. And I'd extend that not just to be economics, but kind of everything that deals with human behavior and social sciences, which ultimately is everything around us. Truly wonderful.

Thank you so much for joining today and sharing the story of both Smallville and what you're now up to at Simile. I really enjoyed the conversation.

Joon: Same here. Thank you for having me.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：大模型公司正在"远离"人类
整个访谈中最令人震撼的潜台词是——**OpenAI、Anthropic 这些前沿实验室追求的"超级智能"，本质上是在让模型越来越**不像**真实人类**。Joon 用了一个极具颠覆性的比喻：当前主流 LLM 的北极星是"理性的、能解客观题的机器"，而真实人类却是非理性的、充满主观偏好与品味的。于是出现了一个诡异的剪刀差：**模型的数学/推理分数曲线在上升，但它模拟人类行为的能力曲线却在下降**。
这意味着 AGI 赛道和"人类仿真"赛道正在 *分叉*——前者追求 CPU 式的单点超人，后者需要 GPU 式的"群体多样性"。Simile 实际上是在押注一个被整个行业抛弃的方向：**不是让模型更聪明，而是让模型更"愚蠢得真实"**。
## 二、隐藏的跨领域同构：page rank、城市隔离、与无标度网络
Joon 在谈"收敛 vs 发散"时无意中揭示了一个跨学科的深层结构：**只要你能以一定精度复现人类微行为，宏观涌现就具有数学上的必然性**。
- Google 的 PageRank → 无标度网络必然出现 hub - Sch

item.title: [邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain] 東西方投資人激辯！台灣缺電台積電不受影響？AI 估值太扯與買幣時機！Ran Neuner【邦妮區塊鏈】
item.dc:creator: 邦妮區塊鏈 Bonnie Blockchain
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Ran 的"反向账户"实验，本质上是一场对自己神经系统的开盘做空

表面看这是一个有趣的交易故事——开两个账户，主账户做想做的，副账户做相反的。但真正惊人的是它揭示的一个结构性真相：当你"想"买入时，市场上的信号早已发酵到你的杏仁核里，你的"想法"本身就是滞后指标。Ran 没有发明交易策略，他发明了一个"延迟探测器"——副账户测出的不是行情，而是他自己情绪反应的传导时间。

跨领域连接：这与精神科里的"暴露反应预防疗法"结构完全一致。强迫症患者也是要练习"想去做某件事时，刻意不做"。也就是说，Ran 用一个 hedge fund 治好了自己的"金融强迫症"。市场不是被交易出来的，是被神经系统的延迟治愈出来的。

二、Michael Sailor 的 STRC 不是金融工程，而是中世纪赎罪券的链上复刻

Ran 花了十分钟解释一个"man"如何单手扭转比特币四年周期，然后又说这不影响去中心化——这看似矛盾，但藏着一个真正出人意料的结构：STRC 是一个"反身性永动机"——它承诺的收益由市场对它的信心本身资助。市场每相信一次，价格就涨一次；价格涨一次，他就能融更多钱去买更多比特币；买得越多，价格越涨，市场信心越足。

这不是庞氏（因为有真实资产托底），但这是另一种历史上更古老的东西——中世纪天主教的赎罪券。教会用未来的救赎换今天的现金，现金维持教会的权威，权威增强救赎的"价值"。Sailor 是 21 世纪的莱昂十世，比特币是新的炼狱。

更惊人的一层：Ran 反复强调"币没有投票权，所以不影响去中心化"。但他忽略了一个真正的洞察——比特币的去中心化从来不是关于"所有权分布"，而是关于"共识机制"。可现在出现了第三个维度：价格发现的中心化。共识仍分布，所有权可分散，但"叙事权"被一个人垄断了。这是中本聪没设计进去的层。

三、台积电工程师地震夜冲回厂房，与 Ran 的反向账户是同一个神经机制的镜像

Bonnie 提到的那个细节——台积电工程师在凌晨地震时撇下老婆孩子跑回工厂——和 Ran 的"反向账户"实验，看似毫无关系，却共享同一种神经结构：两者都是对默认情绪反应的系统性覆盖。

西方交易员在恐慌时卖出，台湾工程师在恐慌时奔向危险。Ran 花了几年才训练自己做"想做事的反面"；台湾的工程文化把这件事制度化、内化、代际化了。这就是为什么美国砸再多 CHIPS Act 的钱也建不出 TSMC——你不是在缺工厂，你是在缺一个能在地震夜里跑向工厂的神经系统。

跨领域连接：这与犹太教 Shema 的晨祷传统、日本能剧学徒制、以及精英特种部队选拔的"反直觉训练"是同构的。任何长期超额回报——无论是金融、半导体还是军事——都来自于一个被制度化的"反应抑制"机制。Ran 用一个副账户实现了个人版本，台湾用一整套文化实现了国家版本。这也解释了为什么 Ran 对"市场即将崩盘"嗅觉敏锐却依然在 11 万美元追高比特币——个人的神经覆盖永远是脆弱的、需要外部容器（副账户、文化、制度）来托管。

四、被忽视的隐藏信号：Hyperliquid 上商品交易量超过加密本身

这一句被一笔带过的新闻其实是整集最重要的洞察："Hyperliquid 现在交易的黄金、石油、白银量超过加密资产"。

加密交易所赢的方式不是加密赢了，而是它把传统资产"走私"进了 24/7 交易世界。这是一个典型的"特洛伊木马"模式——基础设施超越了它原本的目的。Uber 不是用车赢了出租车，是用支付/调度系统赢了；亚马逊不是用书赢了 Barnes & Noble，是用物流赢了。加密的真正胜利可能不是去中心化金融，而是"7×24 小时金融"——它彻底废掉了"市场休市"这个概念，而这个概念是华尔街上百年权力结构的基石。

📝 转录文本

開場：市場的非理性與台灣的反駁

Ran: Wait, hold on. The market's not seeing any of this. Warren Buffett came out the other day and he said that the market's in a gambling mode, right? That is the reason why most traders actually lose money. I realized that I was doing it wrong. And when everything changed for me was one day when I ran an experiment. An experiment went something like this... They're trying to frontrun Michael Saylor's buy. One green candle and everything changes.

Bonnie: Can I challenge you?

Ran: The market can remain irrational. It's completely overheated. We're in the middle of a war. Oil ain't flowing through the most important strategy in the world. Countries that manufacture the chips that power the AI revolution that the whole market is built on ain't getting gas and ain't getting oil, whatever. And the market is just saying, "Hey, no, we're good. Like, we're just going to all-time highs." Come on.

Bonnie: Can I challenge that again? I'm from Taiwan and we make chips.

嘉賓介紹

David: Ran Neuner joins us right now. He's the founder of CryptoBanter. And I'm pleased to co-host this show with — and we're at Consensus Miami. Good to see you in person, Ran.

Ran: Good to see you, David. Good to see you, Bonnie. Nice to meet you.

Bonnie: Welcome to the show.

一、交易心理：FOMO 與「反向帳戶」實驗

David: We're just talking about trading offline, but the personal lessons we've learned. I'll start with a personal anecdote. My audience knows me as someone who's not a very good trader. I always tell my friends, look, use me as a contrarian indicator because whatever I do, it turns out to be the wrong thing to do. I talk about FOMO a lot on my show. I always talk about not buying into FOMO and using that as a contrarian indicator. But I fall for that. When Bitcoin was at $110, $120,000, everyone was talking about $200,000. I was like, "Yeah, let's do it. Let's get in."

Ran: You'll be glad to know that you're not alone. And that is the reason why most traders actually lose money — because they're human. All humans actually follow the same emotion cycles. I was exactly the same as you when I started trading crypto in 2015, 2016. In those days, Bitcoin could go from $300 to $1,000 and back down to $300 in one day. I'll never forget that the first big batch of Bitcoin that I bought, I bought them at $500. It went up to $1,000 and then they got dumped all the way down to $300 and I sold them all at $300, and then 15 minutes later I bought them back for $800.

So I realized that I was doing it wrong, and when everything changed for me was one day when I ran an experiment. The experiment went something like this: I opened my normal trading account and I opened a second account. The second account I put a very little bit of money in — not a lot. And I took that second account and I said: whatever I feel like doing, I'm going to do the exact opposite in this account. So when I felt like buying, in that account I sold. On my big account I bought. When I felt like selling, I bought on that account and I sold on my main account.

David: Literally started a hedge fund.

Ran: And what happened was: my big account emptied itself out, and this tiny account actually today is my trading account. Why? Because over time what I realized is that every time you want to do something, do the opposite. It's that simple. Because when you want to do something, everybody else also wants to do something. So when you're thinking about buying Bitcoin, everybody's thinking about buying Bitcoin. You're never early. Unless you have inside information, and if you're trading on sentiments and trading on price moves — you're not early.

二、Consensus Miami 現場觀察：情緒指標

David: We're here at Consensus Miami. It's a good turnout. It wasn't as good as last year when we were doing our shows in Miami, in New York. I'm talking about the sentiment here and the attendance. It's like 3/4 full and people aren't as euphoric. Is that an indicator for you?

Ran: Well, let's talk about the crypto market. We all expected a bull market. The bull market never came. It was a very small bull market in Bitcoin and it was a bear market in alts pretty much since January last year. Then whoever else was left here was flushed out on October 10th. If you were retail and you were trading on October 10th, you got flushed out. Then since then, Bitcoin's been in that death chop — sideways and down. There's no one left. Crypto is not fun.

On the other side of it, at the same time — have you traded equities lately? Like, listen, sex is good, but have you traded equities lately? [laughter] Because there's been an incredible incredible incredible bull run in equities. You take a company like SanDisk, Intel, AMD — these companies are doing 2x, 3x, 4x and 5x. That's why I got into crypto. At the same time crypto is not giving us anything. It doesn't have a heartbeat. Look at Solana — Solana was the darling of the last one and a half bull runs. It's at $85 and it's a stablecoin now. Bitcoin's gone from 60 to today 81–82 and Solana is still at $85. It just shows the life has been taken out of the crypto market right now.

To be honest, I'm quite surprised that it's this full and I'm quite surprised that people spent this much money on it. But I think we have been through a cleansing in the crypto market.
