The Attorneys General Subpoena

Well, it is, but there's a bigger one, I think. Which is the New York and New Jersey Attorneys General have a couple of weeks ago subpoenaed FIFA over the manipulation of World Cup ticketing, basically creating what they say is fake scarcity to drive up price.

We love a good statement. So, Jennifer Davenport, the New Jersey Attorney General, said the following: "FIFA has turned buying a ticket for this World Cup into a gauntlet of confusion, fake scarcity, and impossibly high prices, all at the expense of consumers and hard working New Jerseyans."

There is also, on top of this, been accusations of those that bought tickets getting tickets that were not represented in the purchasing process. I.E., they think they've bought a good ticket with X view, and it's turned out to be one that is far less good. Because since those tickets came out in the beginning, FIFA added two new sections of like exclusive high value tickets at the front of stands. And so, some people have basically been pushed backwards or moved sideways in stadium.

So, the AG's had two elements: one, creating fake scarcity. One, selling and misrepresenting a product.

And how much are the host cities paying for the privilege, by the way?

About $250 million, right? Per city to host the game.

Per city, right? So, for you to be one of the World Cup host cities, as a city, you're already paying 250 million bucks. And then you find that FIFA allegedly screwing your own local voters on even their access, let alone, you know, foreign fans coming in, their access to it.

The Big Picture: The Extra $2 Billion

Now, I want to bring this back to what's really happened here. Let's look at the big picture, right? Let's accept the fact that it's a mess, right? I don't think that's controversial, is it? And say, why has it landed up here?

So, they did 7 and 1/2 billion of revenue in Qatar. And because the commercial environment was always going to be much better in the US, and because things like hospitality sells will be better, they've also got more matches, right? So, that in theory should do better. And you've just got a country which a lot of people want to travel to in any case, etc. So, for all sorts of reasons — TV rights as well, likely to do better — for all sorts of reasons, that 7 and 1/2 billion was probably always going to rise to 9 billion. Is that fair?

Definitely.

Right? So, 7 and 1/2 to 9 billion, and Gianni could have stood up and gone, "Record World Cup. We've increased revenues by 20%, okay?" And everyone goes, "Whoa, well done, Gianni."

Now, his determination to drive it from the 9 billion, which probably it should have been, to 11 billion — that is the 2 billion that is attempting to be extracted from fans. And it's that 2 billion that is causing the problem here, right?

Which is if they'd simply gone, "Look, the commercial sponsors are going to pay big bucks. The TV companies may pay big bucks. The one thing that we now need to do is to make sure it's a great event. We need to safeguard the brand of the World Cup cuz we need to make sure that other countries want to host it. We need to make sure that when there's another World Cup, that lots of fans want to go and all this type of stuff."

What they've done is an absolute medium-term strategic disaster, which they've basically brought shame on the World Cup to try and screw an extra couple of billion dollars out of it.

Why? Infantino's Re-election Machine

Really, because that's how Infantino props up his own power base by being able to hand out money to all the little federations around the world. And the more he hands out to them, the more they want to keep Gianni Infantino in position.

And we've said this before with our friend Kirsty at the IOC. In a way, it's almost a bit unfair to blame them, although I do blame Gianni in this case, because the way the system is set up — where each one individual country, rather like the minor race courses, each one has one vote — means that countries for whom an extra couple of million here or there really makes a big difference to them, their vote is going to swing depending on whichever candidate promises them the biggest handout.

Talk about this at another time. That has increased this year. Each team is getting 12 and a half million dollars guaranteed, right? With — even if they go out of the group stages.

But look look look at the payments to the ones who don't turn up. That's the critical one, because don't forget the ones who are most important in the voting process aren't the ones who qualify.

Yes.

They're the ones who don't qualify.

Yeah.

Broadcast.

Yeah. Absolutely.

Okay. Increase. But why are those two groups? Why are they paying more? Because they're buying an amazing experience with a brilliant reputation that is a prestige —

And Qatar was a big success.

And Qatar was a — Yeah. And the success of the last one. So, they can go, "Right. That was brilliant for us. That was exactly what we wanted to buy. We had those moments of absolute incredible fandom."

You've raised 37 and a half percent on sponsorship. You've raised 22 and a half percent on broadcast.

Really? Then now all the minute this sends you right into that next cycle, right? And you're going to be looking at how to increase that. If there's been a [bad] experience for the fans, which is going to dominate a lot of the press in that, your association that comes into this being really positive and is worth all of that increase, that's going to become really challenging for you to justify. So, you're not increasing it. You may even start looking to reduce it cuz you realize that if this is going to be the operational model of this organization, it's not probably the one that's going to get us the best exposure.

And now when — competition we talked about Charlie last week, Formula 1 and you know, top brands — there's now more competition than ever for these dollars. So, in this kind of very short-term targeting, they have completely missed, to me, the long-term value. And they've turned what should be the biggest fan-driven event into a commercial outcome.

Closing: The Structural Problem

Except that it works in the short medium term to get Gianni Infantino re-elected to his position at FIFA. And I think that's where we have to leave it. That is the point of this whole exercise, okay? And that is the structural problem with FIFA is that the president gets re-elected based on the votes of individual small countries. And the more money you give them, the more likely they are to vote for you again. Correct?

That's so interesting. I mean, look, we've just said it's going to be a lot about the performance side. I think there's some interesting business pieces. Ticketing's one huge one. We'll cover a couple of others that we've identified in the coming weeks. So, we'll save them for that.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个被忽视的数学题：那"多出来的20亿"
把视频里的数字摊开来看，会出现一个让人脊背发凉的算式。Qatar 是 75 亿美元，FIFA 自然增长到 90 亿几乎是市场常识——美国商业环境更好、场次更多、转播费天然上扬。但 Infantino 偏要把目标抬到 110 亿，硬挤出来的那 **20 亿差额**，几乎全部压在球迷的门票上。
而**真正令人意外的连接是**：这 20 亿与 FIFA 每年通过"发展基金"流向各小成员国足协的金额量级几乎一致。换句话说，球迷一家四口为看一场比赛付出的 1 万英镑，本质上不是在买座位，而是在替 Infantino 给那些**根本不会出现在世界杯赛场上**的小国足协续票——他的连任票仓。这是一场把消费税伪装成门票的政治献金计划。
## "成熟市场"的认知错觉：FIFA 犯了一个营销学一年级的错误
视频里点出的"美国是成熟体育市场"这个判断，是整件事最锋利的洞察，但还能再往前推一步。FIFA 把"美国人愿意为 Super Bowl 付 5000 美元"当作"美国人愿意为足球付 500 美元"的依据，这相当于看到**日本威士忌市场成熟**就推断

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] AI Should Be Commoditized – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

三个出人意料的观察

1. "电力没有反对者"——一个被误读的历史隐喻

Imas 用电力类比 AI 商品化，但这个类比本身藏着一个被忽略的反讽：电力之所以"没有反对者"，恰恰是因为它的危害被成功地外部化和不可见化了——燃煤电厂的污染、电网工人的触电死亡、电气化对手工业者的灭绝，从未成为"反电力运动"。这不是因为电力无害，而是因为它的伤害没有叙事载体。

这与对话中后半段提到的"负面叙事更容易传播"形成了一个隐秘的闭环：电力的胜利不是技术的胜利，是叙事缺席的胜利。AI 之所以遭遇汹涌反对，不是因为它比电力更危险，而是因为它的失业者会说话、会发推、会上播客。一个技术能否被社会接受，取决于它的受害者是否有麦克风。

2. 安全与公平的"伪权衡"——一个被金融工程偷偷解决的伦理问题

Trammell 抛出了一个极其精巧的论点，但很少有人注意到它的真正含义：安全派和反垄断派的争论其实是一个会计问题，不是一个伦理问题。

传统框架认为：要么少数实验室领先（安全，但财富集中），要么充分竞争（公平，但鲁莽）。Trammell 的破局点是——"领先"和"所有权集中"是两件可以分开的事。上市公司可以同时是技术垄断者和财富分散者。

这个洞见的跨领域连接令人震撼：这正是 19 世纪铁路托拉斯通过股票公开发行所做的事，也是当代主权财富基金试图对石油资源做的事（挪威模式）。AI 安全圈花了十年争论的二选一，金融史早就给出了第三条路：让垄断结构存在，让租金分配社会化。AI 治理界还在用一战前的政治经济学词汇争论二战后已经发明的金融工具。

3. "技术集中度创造政治攻击面"——一个被忽视的二阶效应

最后那个关于 Defense Production Act 的观察是整段对话的真正核弹，但它被轻描淡写地带过了。Dwarkesh 实际上揭示了一个反直觉的安全悖论：

集中化的实验室通常被认为更"可控"（因此更安全），但这恰恰让它们更容易被国家暴力征用。一个能被 DPA 威胁的 Anthropic，本质上是"已经被半国有化"的实验室——它的对齐研究、它的拒答政策、它的部署节奏，都暴露在一个具体的政治杠杆下。

这与冷战时期物理学家的处境惊人相似：曼哈顿计划之后，全美顶尖物理学家被集中在洛斯阿拉莫斯，结果是整个学科被国家安全机器接管了三十年——你想做基础研究？先过 Q-clearance。今天的前沿 AI 实验室正在重演这个剧本，而且没人意识到。

真正的安全论证可能是反过来的：分散的 AI 能力让政府没有单一的扼喉点可抓，从而保护了对齐研究本身的学术独立性。"集中以求安全"可能正在制造它声称要避免的那种系统性风险——只不过风险的来源从模型变成了国家。

一个串起三者的隐藏模式：这段对话表面在谈 AI 经济学，实际上在反复触碰同一个结构——任何关于"权力是否应该集中"的争论，本质上都是在问"谁拥有麦克风、谁拥有股权、谁拥有否决权"。这三个问题在传统社会里是耦合的，但 AI 时代第一次让它们可以被独立设计。Trammell 隐约触到了这一点，但还没有意识到他打开的是一个多大的盒子。

📝 转录文本

一、为什么希望 AI 实验室被商品化

This actually makes me hope even more so than before that the labs do get commoditized. Or at the very least they go public as soon as possible. But hopefully they just get totally commoditized, because I think AI will be much more popular and, more importantly, will be much more likely to lead to broad increases in prosperity if the gains are just not particularly— it is as hard to capture the gains of AI as it is to capture the gains of electrification.

Yeah, exactly. So I think like everybody— there's no anti-electricity people out there, right?

I mean, electricity doesn't take your job. But it takes some people's jobs.

Yeah, yeah.

二、叙事的不对称：失去比想象更容易讲述

And I think it's— you know, this is maybe a tangential to the conversation— I think narratives matter, and there's this really negative narrative around AI right now. But that's because people are not putting out the positive narrative. And there's a reason: it's more difficult to imagine something that doesn't exist that's a good thing than losing something that exists.

Right, yeah.

So it's very easy for somebody to go on a podcast and to say "these jobs that you like, they're going away" than for somebody to spin up a utopia which doesn't exist.

Right.

三、商品化的代价：前沿竞赛的安全张力

I hope this isn't too out of left field, but I think I would be remiss if I didn't point out one big cost of having commoditized frontier AI models, which is the tech race dynamic, right? That for safety purposes you might want fewer frontier companies, so that each one has a buffer in case they want to slow things down to make things safer.

And the way this relates to our point before about the kind of widespread access of the returns— I think there's a lot less of a trade-off there than some people imagine. Some people think: either frontier AI gets commoditized and we all enjoy the benefits, but there might be some risk because the market's really competitive and cutthroat; or things are safer because there's a big gap between the leader and the laggard, but that means that the leaders get fantastically wealthy.

No— you could just have a relatively big gap, but it's a public company, ownership is widely distributed.

Yeah, yeah, yeah.

Yeah.

四、集中化的隐性成本：成为政治目标

More recently I have been thinking that the risk of commodification— which is that it sort of diffuses the ability to use AI to harmful ends— is worth the benefit. I worry that not only having these concentrated labs makes it so that the surplus isn't as widely distributed through society, but also it creates a very tangible, clear political target for the government to—

I mean, we saw this with the Defense Production Act threat against Anthropic. If there wasn't one lab, or a couple of labs, that are clearly ahead of others, this kind of threat would be much harder to make.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

Imas 用电力类比 AI 商品化，但这个类比本身藏着一个被忽略的反讽：**电力之所以"没有反对者"，恰恰是因为它的危害被成功地外部化和不可见化了**——燃煤电厂的污染、电网工人的触电死亡、电气化对手工业者的灭绝，从未成为"反电力运动"。这不是因为电力无害，而是因为它的伤害**没有叙事载体**。

item.title: [Business of Sport] The Fight To Fix British Horse Racing
item.dc:creator: Business of Sport
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反常的洞察：Ascot 退出协会，本质上是"君主制企业"对"民主否决权"的革命

表面看，Ascot 退出 Racecourses Association 是一次行业内政治动作。但深挖一层会发现一个极具讽刺意味的结构性悖论：这场反抗"一人一票制"的运动，恰恰是由英国唯一一家由君主拥有的赛马场发起的。

更耐人寻味的是——保守派（皇室）成了改革派，而所谓"民主"的赛马场协会反而成了停滞的同义词。这映射出一个被广泛忽视的治理规律：当"一人一票"被应用在规模差异极大的成员之间时，它不再是民主，而是变成了"小者绑架大者"的合法工具。这与 1980 年代英超脱离足球联盟的逻辑完全同构——但比足球更彻底，因为这次"造反者"自己背后站着的是国王。

二、跨领域连接：英国赛马的困境，本质上是"长尾经济学"被错误应用到供给端

互联网时代我们习惯了 Chris Anderson 的"长尾理论"——尾部的总量可以超过头部。但英国赛马展示了长尾理论的反面教材：当长尾不是由消费者自发选择形成的，而是由补贴机制（Levy）人为维持时，长尾会反向掏空头部。

90 million 英镑撒在 1,450 场比赛里，对应香港 170 million 英镑撒在 88 场——这个数字游戏背后，是一个被严重低估的产业规律：奖金浓度（prize money density）才是体育全球化时代真正的竞争力指标。这一逻辑同样适用于： - 学术界（科研经费过度撒胡椒面 vs. 集中资助顶尖实验室）； - 风险投资（"广撒网"组合的回报往往低于集中下注）； - 公共媒体（BBC 节目预算被分散到无数小栏目，反而削弱了旗舰内容）。

英国赛马的问题，不是"钱不够"，而是"钱被均匀稀释到了竞争力为零的地方"。

三、出人意料的观察：Derby 的衰落，是"品牌错位"而非"市场萎缩"——这是一个反 Saturday 神话

直觉上，把赛事从工作日（周三）移到周末（周六）应该带来更多观众。但 Derby 的数据讲了一个相反的故事：周三 25 万人 → 周六 2.2 万人。这里隐藏着一个被市场营销学严重低估的真相：

"独特性"本身是品牌的最大资产，而"便利性"在高端品牌语境下常常是稀释剂。

把 Derby 移到周六，等于让它失去了"全城罢工去赛马"的仪式感，转而进入和板球、网球、英超的红海竞争。这就像爱马仕突然决定在亚马逊上卖包——你看似扩大了渠道，实际上摧毁了渠道之所以存在的理由。

这条规律的跨域延伸： - 苹果坚持自家发布会而非参加 CES； - 米其林餐厅刻意不接受 OpenTable； - 牛津剑桥保留拉丁文授课仪式。

便利的反义词不是麻烦，而是稀有。 Derby 输掉的不是观众，而是稀有性。

四、隐藏的系统性真相：英国赛马没有"赛马问题"，它有"治理问题"

最容易被忽略的一点是——英国赛马并不缺乏需求（World Pool 一年从香港中国市场为顶级赛道带回 2,000 万英镑），也不缺乏供给（1,450 场比赛是世界之最），更不缺乏品牌（皇室加持的 Royal Ascot）。它缺的是一个能够代表"产业价值"而非"成员人头"的决策机构。

这是一个深刻的体育治理元问题：当一个治理结构形成于产业的"小而散"年代，它会在产业的"大而集中"年代成为发展的最大阻力。 Premier League 在 1992 年解决了这个问题，NBA 在 1980 年代解决了，F1 在 Bernie Ecclestone 时代解决了——英国赛马只是这条曲线上最晚醒来的那一个。

而 Jockey Club 的犹豫，则揭示了另一个普遍真相：最有能力推动改革的人，往往同时也是最享受现状最多的人。 这不是赛马的问题，这是所有成熟产业的"创新者困境"——只不过这次穿着马靴登场。

📝 转录文本

开场：生日话题——回到马术产业

"You've given me a little birthday treat." "You're about to get stuck into one of your absolute pinnacles of breakdown topics."

Well, that's what we want to get to. Yes. So quite early on in our breakdown journey, Charlie threw the dog a bone and let me do an in-depth breakdown of the horse racing industry, which sits in a rather strange place within the sporting ecosystem — being, broadly speaking, other than soccer/football, the biggest sport in the world, but often not as apparently so to other sports fans.

It has its own fan ecosystem, but it's a big industry. And we outlined that last time how the bloodstock industry — that's the breeding of horses — makes the football transfer business look like a very small thing indeed. You'll find that even the very biggest transfers in football only amount to one year's stud fees for a big stallion. [laughter] So the value of Frankel is a long way beyond any footballer, and they do it over a much more attractive period of time as well.

So look, we went through that last time, and actually it was very gratifying — the number of people who wrote in saying, "Well, I kind of never really knew that." So this is taking that discussion on, because one of the things that I outlined in that discussion — which happened just before Cheltenham, actually it was in Cheltenham week, wasn't it — was some of the challenges facing the industry, some of the dysfunction existing within it, and pointing up the possibility for some pretty nasty battles down the track as the industry gets to grips with those challenges.

And topically now, the reason it's a good time is because we just had Epsom and we're about to have Ascot.

"Derby last weekend, Royal Ascot next week."

Yeah. So it's front of everyone's minds on the sporting calendar.

一、Epsom Derby：从 25 万到 2.2 万的衰落

"Can I ask you quickly about Epsom? There were a couple of things I was interested in… from what I've seen outlined in broader media is that it's not what it used to be — and the focus is, putting it mildly…"

So, okay, brilliant. So the other piece was the Derby, in particular on radio. You actually explained to me yesterday as well, but moving it from a Wednesday to a Saturday has seriously damaged its brand.

Yeah. So okay, let's just address that thing. The Derby historically was the UK's biggest spectator sport occasion. If you go back to the decade after the Second World War, how many people do you think pitched up on that one day — and it was a Wednesday?

"100,000."

250,000.

"Wow. So what, like the whole size of a Cheltenham meet basically in one day?"

250,000 people used to pitch up to the Derby — basically the whole of the East End and the City.

"The boys."

Imagine the mixture, right? So the East End on the one hand, and then next door to it the City on the other hand. All decamped to Epsom for the day. They closed down the markets, etc. It was like a sort of huge London day out.

By the time that I sort of came along — which, as everyone can now work out, was about 40 years ago…

"1976 we're talking about."

Yeah. But I can't remember that far back. By the time I was alive and awake to what the Derby was, I guess I was in my early teens, so we're talking about the mid-1980s. By this point, it wasn't quite that. Attendances — we were living through an age where people were starting to get a bit more serious about workdays, so getting the entire of London out on a Wednesday afternoon wasn't quite as straightforward as it had once been.

However, it was still totally normal for the stands — effectively the paid enclosures on the outer side of the racetrack — to be sold out at about 40,000-45,000, and then the Hill, which is the inside of the track, on a nice day could have 50,000-60,000 on it. So you're talking about a total attendance of about 100,000 when I was young.

周六化的战略错误

They switched it to a Saturday after a bit, because they were like: "Well, we're starting to struggle a bit for the paid attendance. The Hill does well, but the paid attendance is a bit lower. So if we switch it to a Saturday, a more typical sporting day, it'll do better."

I always felt that was a mistake, because when you own your own brand position, the key thing is to understand that that distinctiveness is part of what makes your event distinctive, different, etc.

"Also, you just saying 'a more traditional sporting day' is exactly that trap, right? Because they don't want to be in a more traditional sporting day — because then you compete with all of the other sports on a Saturday."

Exactly. I mean, really what they had done is they had just stopped marketing it properly. There was a generation of Londoners growing up who weren't really aware of it, but they thought: "Okay, quick fix. Move it to a Saturday. We'll get an extra few punters in because it's their day off."

It kind of worked a bit. By the turn of the century, by the early 2000s, you're probably seeing a stand paid attendance of about 40,000 and then another 10,000-20,000 on the Hill — a total attendance of about 60,000. So then gradually drops off, drops off, drops off, and finally hits a low last year, 2025, with a paid-for attendance — I think I'm right in saying — of about 22,000. Which is, broadly speaking, pardon the word, crap.

There are average race meetings in the calendar — courses you, Charlie Stebbings, will barely have heard of — that get 20,000. So for the Derby to only get 20,000, bearing in mind it's on the outskirts of London with its own railway station, so easy to get to, such a spectacular occasion, so famous — and yet they were only getting 20,000.

500 万英镑的雨天豪赌

So what they decided to do this year was plow a huge amount of money into marketing it. By racing standards, I believe, and I'm told, they spent about £5 million marketing the Derby this year. And sadly, it rained.

"£5 million?"

£5 million, I'm told. Right — I stand to be corrected on that, but I was told this morning by someone inside racing who should know that they spent £5 million marketing the Derby this year, in the hope of getting a massive attendance on the Hill, which obviously is open air — you are vulnerable to the weather.

One can look back with the benefit of hindsight and say: given racing's budgets, which we're going to come on to in a moment, was it a wise thing to devote such a massive amount of money to something which is so weather dependent? That is hindsight. If it had been a gloriously sunny day, then we look back and go: well, that was a brilliant way to spend money. It's early to mid-June, normally nice weather — if you're ever going to have a moment when you can get loads of people to come.

So I'm a bit loath to criticize them on that specific point. But I am concerned about the overall direction of marketing racing, and I'm going to come back to that later on.

二、Ascot：繁荣的另一面

But let's talk about Ascot, because Ascot is an event that is not struggling. Ascot is absolutely booming.

Having just said that there were 25-odd-thousand people at the Derby — at Ascot, almost all of which happens during the week, with one day on the Saturday (Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday during the week, and then the Saturday tacked on the end of it) — 300,000 people will turn up for that. You're talking about an average of 60,000 a day going to the Royal meeting at Ascot, and the revenues are really, really considerable.

So Ascot as a whole — for people who don't know, Ascot as a racecourse is owned by…

"The royal family."

The King. Yeah, exactly. The Ascot Authority is owned by the King / royal family. The total revenue for the year is £120 million. So it's a pretty big sporting concern. It's not much less than some of the Premier League clubs.

"And of that, £80 million is the Royal meeting?"

Wow.

Right. So Royal Ascot, over four days — five days actually — turns over £80 million quid, which is 70% of Ascot's total revenue for the year.

"That's a lot of exposure on your business."

It is. It is. But in a way, like — what can go wrong? The royal family will turn up. [laughter]

Indeed. Prize money: £11 million quid. Which is an interesting little footnote going back to the tennis players' complaints about percentages of prize money — that's about the same percentage the tennis players get, more or less.

三、内战爆发：Ascot 退出 Racecourse Association

And you know, all's rosy in the world. Ascot, fantastic, isn't it, doing well. But all is not rosy in the world, because Ascot has started a civil war inside the horse racing world, which is super super interesting.

Generally speaking, Ascot is very conservative, because it is aware as an entity that it is owned by an owner who doesn't tend to want a lot of controversy around him or her, as it used to be. So when it steps out of line, they can't just decide that. They have to go up to the Palace and say: "By the way, this is what we're going to do."

What have they done? They have resigned from the Racecourse Association. And this happened about three weeks ago. They announced: "We're leaving the Racecourse Association." Might seem like a small enough thing. It's not. It's a big thing.

BHA 的三方权力结构

What is the Racecourse Association? The Racecourse Association is the trade body that represents racecourses. The reason that's important is the way British horse racing is governed at something called the British Horseracing Authority (the BHA) level — there are three entities which have control over the BHA, and therefore what the whole industry does:

  1. Racecourses — currently represented by the Racecourse Association.
  2. Participants — trainers, jockeys, breeders, etc.
  3. Owners — the Racehorse Owners Association. Slightly strange body, actually, but whatever, there we are.

But out of those groups, the Racecourse Association is the dominant bit, because they represent bigger businesses. Whereas a lot of the other associations represent fundamentally sole traders — the Professional Jockeys Association is each individual jockey, the National Trainers Federation is each individual trainer, etc.

So for the Racecourse Association to have a third of the power in racing actually ends up being more like 50%. It really has a blocking vote over British horse racing doing anything.

一人一票制的诅咒

And the way the Racecourse Association votes is the way the Football League used to vote before the Premier League. There are 58 courses (and there were 92 clubs in the Football League) — each course has one vote. So if you've got some tiny little course that you've never heard of that only races six or seven times a year and gets no one through the gate — one vote. Get Ascot — one vote.

What's been happening over a long period of time is that the small courses, of which there are more than big courses (just like in the old Football League), have been coagulating together to block progress and to block the ability of British racing to really compete on the world stage.

四、Levy 制度：经费如何被稀释

"Why — can I bring it back just quickly to something you were saying last time? Is that part of the problem then when you're looking at your solution that you posited last time around — reducing the total number, creating the higher quality of product — and therefore if you're a smaller racecourse you recognize you're going to lose out on that, because you're going to lose your meets and therefore your business is…"

You've got it. You've got it right.

什么是 Levy

There's something called the Levy. The Levy is a government body which takes money from all the money bet on horse racing and redistributes it into prize money. That spits off, for the support of prize money, about £90 million a year.

This is absolutely critical because all the other racing jurisdictions — Hong Kong, Japan, Australia, France, Ireland — have state-run betting organizations which feed money into their sport. In most of those cases, they are monopolies — so you can only bet with that. If you go racing in France, there are not lots of bookmakers. There's just something called the Pari-Mutuel, which is their equivalent to the Tote, and everyone has to bet with the Pari-Mutuel. Then the vast majority of the Pari-Mutuel's profits are just funneled straight into prize money.

In this country, we have bookmakers, and bookmakers have been there long enough — and are embedded in the whole thing long enough — that there's no realistic prospect of getting rid of them. They're a big part of the landscape, big lobbying powers at Westminster, etc. So it's kind of accepted: "Okay, we're never going to get that benefit" (which, sadly, if we did, probably Britain would be the dominant racing jurisdiction in the world).

But the bookmakers have to make up for it a bit — and they do, because they are forced to do so by the government through the Levy. So x amount of what they make out of horse racing gets funneled back into prize money: £90 million. And then the Levy Board decides where that £90 million goes.

1,450 场比赛的稀释陷阱

At the moment, it gets distributed all the way through all these little courses and meetings. Just to give you an idea of how this works, Charlie:

There are 1,500 race fixtures a year. One. Just think about that. 1,500. So if we look just…

"Five a day."

Yeah, basically almost four maybe. I think it's 1,450. So yeah, round it down. So about four a day.

That breaks down roughly into 900 flat fixtures and 600 — sorry, 900 flat fixtures and about 550 jumps fixtures. Just put the jump racing to one side for a moment, because it's on the flat where we have to compete against the rest of the world. Jumps — it's only Ireland really. Only Ireland and Britain have jump racing.

So you've got 900 flat fixtures, of which 600 race on turf and 350-odd are on what we call all-weather tracks. Again, put those to one side a little bit, although they do matter.

So you've got this Levy of £90 million, of which let's say about £60 million is going to go on flat racing and £30 million on jump racing — making the numbers round. That £60 million is being spread throughout 900 fixtures.

香港的对比

Now let's go to Hong Kong, one of the most successful racing jurisdictions in the world. The Hong Kong Jockey Club is the monopoly provider of betting, and they funnel £170 million of their profits into prize money. So you've got £90 million coming in Britain and £170 million coming in Hong Kong.

Britain can make up the difference with better sponsorships and more people on track, etc. Hong Kong has 88 meetings a year. So they have £170 million for 88 meetings. We have £90 million for 1,450 meetings.

Fundamental problem.

Arena Racing Company 的商业模式

On top of that, loads of that money gets funneled to the minor tracks, many of whom are owned by one company called Arena Racing Company (ARC). They actually do have one big track — Doncaster — but all their other tracks are small tracks, and they basically exist on a Levy model:

"Okay, we're going to be given x amount by the Levy Board per fixture. If we keep the prize money really low, and if we keep all our costs really low, then we've got a business."

I won't bore you with what that business looks like, but it's a successful business that makes, I think, sort of £50-60-70 million a year. All of that money is being kept away from the top end of the sport.

That's not true — because the top end of the sport does get some money from the Levy Board, but nowhere near enough, because that's where you get into the ability to compete.

五、奖金的国际对比与"championship 之痛"

If you look at the Epsom Derby — the most famous horse race in the world, probably together with the Kentucky Derby, its equivalent in the US — Epsom Derby prize money: £2 million. Not bad.

  • Japan Cup: £5 million
  • Arc de Triomphe (France): £4.5 million
  • Melbourne Cup (Australia): £5 million

You're seeing that we are well off the pace. And it gets even worse at the races which are just below the top level. This is a critical category of races — for horses who aren't quite good enough to be absolute superstars, but who are still really really good.

"It's the equivalent of the Championship in football, right? It's the equivalent of the bottom-end Premier League and the Championship in football."

At that level — that's where the Levy squeeze (the bulk of it going to lots of bad races and not going into that level) is really hurting English racing. Prize money at that level is so much lower than the equivalent in all these other foreign jurisdictions for horses of that type that these horses are just exodus — an absolute exodus.

It's rather like English footballers in the 1980s. They are all being sold to race in South Africa, Hong Kong, Australia, the Middle East, etc., because there is just not the prize money for them here.

That then has a further impact in the industry: effectively, when you're asking somebody to pay a lot of money to buy a racehorse, you cannot guarantee — however much you pay for that horse, however well bred — that he's going to be a Premier League footballer. But if he's well bred enough, you've got a very very good chance he will at least be able to be a class below that. But if the class of racing below that is not economic, then suddenly you've got a real problem.

六、两条路：加钱或减场

"So if I'm reading this right, you have two options here to correct this. You either increase the prize money total, or you reduce the number of meets and focus that prize money on fewer meetings."

"But when you say you can either increase the prize money — how would you do that?"

Well, exactly. It would just become a bigger number and a bigger percentage of your total revenues.

"Of whose revenues?"

Well, the racecourses.

"But they're not very profitable. My point — whether it's possible or not — is you are, if you're in the situation, you either do that and it screws your business model (let's say football and wages, paying well beyond what they make), or you reduce the number of races and focus the prize money to a smaller group of racecourses and meetings."

And fundamentally, this is what Ascot wants to do. This is what sits behind this political move, in my belief. They haven't publicly said this, but I believe this to be the case. Ascot basically says:

"If we want this sport to flourish in this country, it will only flourish because of the top end. You're not going to get suddenly lots of new people becoming involved in the sport because they want to basically bet on something that looks like a dog track somewhere. They're going to get interest in the sport because they see Royal Ascot on TV. Be realistic about it."

In other words, for them to be able to make the changes — or for the British Horseracing Authority to be able to make the changes to the fixture list that are required, and for the Levy Board to be able to make the changes to the Levy Board distribution that's required — it can't be blocked by the Racecourse Association. At the moment, the Racecourse Association can simply block that. And because the Racecourse Association is operating on one-member-one-vote, it is therefore operating on behalf of the majority of courses, which are the smaller courses.

三大集团：六家独立、Jockey Club、ARC

So just very quickly:

  • Six leading independent courses — big ones: Ascot and Ayr in Scotland;
  • The Jockey Club, who own 7 large courses (Cheltenham, Aintree, Epsom, Newmarket, etc.) and 8 small ones;
  • ARC, which is 16 courses — one big one (Doncaster) and 15 small ones.

ARC, being the group, then gangs up with these small independents — the little ones which don't have a home and aren't part of a bigger company — and acts effectively as a block vote at the RCA.

So the Jockey Club and Ascot said some time ago, and the other leading independents: "We need a change in governance, and you, the RCA, have to come back to us and explain how you're going to change the way you're set up."

1980 年代足球的历史回响

It's interesting, because this happened in football in the 1980s. The big clubs went to the Football League and said: "Guys, this can't carry on." Famously — I can't remember which of the big clubs said: "I'm not having Grimsby Town" (or whichever small club he picked) "telling the rest of us how to run this industry. Tail wagging the dog. It's not going to work."

When the Football League didn't address those concerns, the leading clubs went: "Okay, we're out." That's what's happening here. The RCA did not come back with a change in governance. As a result of that, Ascot have gone: "Well, we told you, we warned you, we're off."

七、Jockey Club 的领导责任

Big question now to the Jockey Club. Because actually, Ascot have been quite bold here, given that they are one course and given the reputational issues surrounding being owned by the King. The Jockey Club has no such constraints.

I've publicly called now in both a couple of speeches I've made and also on this podcast: please, the Jockey Club, show some leadership. This is your sport. The members of the Jockey Club include most of the biggest owners, who in turn employ the biggest trainers, who in turn employ the biggest jockeys — and they have most of the big courses, or at least a big chunk of the big courses, as well.

If the Stewards of the Jockey Club — which are the people who ultimately control the Jockey Club; not the Chief Executive, the Chief Exec has to report into the Stewards — if the Stewards of the Jockey Club said "Ascot's right, we're going to revolutionize British horse racing," then it would happen.

I've spoken to former chief executives of the Jockey Club. I worked for the Jockey Club in many different guises over the years. I've been a consultant there. So I do know a lot of the people who have been there. And the ones I've spoken to are very very clear: ultimately the Stewards of the Jockey Club have to take a lead. You can't expect the Chief Exec of the Jockey Club, who's a paid employee, to drive this. This has to come from the Stewards, and they have to understand their responsibility to the future of racing.

八、Jockey Club 自身的内部矛盾

"So can I just ask from my side again — it looks like this would make a huge amount of sense for the Jockey Club being those massive racecourses. They've also got a lot of small ones. So the negative — what are they looking at internally and saying the negative impact of us deciding to do this is what?"

Well, first of all, they do have small racecourses in their portfolio. My view on that — which is driven because when you've been a sports executive, you know it's difficult to make these businesses work — is you might have to close some of those down. If they don't make sense, or say to them basically: "Let them float away on their own. Give them a board, give them a bit of a cash float, and say if you can make sense for yourself, great. If you can't, we can't keep subsidizing you any longer."

Let me give you an example. By keeping these small courses afloat — who all need a Jockey Club subsidy, effectively — that is then taking money away which is needed for the redevelopment of Sandown Park, which is one of their big ones, which is currently facilities-wise an absolute joke. Like stepping back into the 1980s.

That is just a classic example: you've got a course which should be absolutely part of the elite, but the Jockey Club's balance sheet can't support it, because the Jockey Club's constantly having to shovel money out the side to support the small courses.

既得利益与 point-to-point

So there are vested interests within the Jockey Club who have links to those small courses who will go blah blah, whatever they might say. That absolutely will happen.

I remember making this speech at a horse racing event with a whole bunch of Jockey Club people there, and two or three of them saying: "You're absolutely spot on there." And then you spotted, around the people who are on the boards of the smaller courses, their brows furrowing.

And I said: look, if you want to hold point-to-point racing — which is amateur jump racing — at your courses, which by the way can pull a very big crowd (I've been to point-to-point meetings where there've been 10,000 people), the point-to-point — yeah, etc. But what are you holding? You're holding really low-end racing that no one wants to come to. So what's the point?

I know it's a nice place for you to go to lunch six or seven times a year, whatever it might be, and to invite friends to and to be a local grandee. But it's destroying British horse racing.

九、20 家赛马场的愿景

British horse racing, probably in my view, instead of 58 courses, the Levy needs to be supporting — I'm going to be generous and say — 20 courses. That would then enable us to be competitive at the top end and just below the top end.

And then it would enable us to drive a narrative around the top end of racing, which would make more sense to the general public — rather than saying to the general public: "Oh, by the way, if you want to get interested in horse racing, you only have to follow 1,450 meetings every year."

Extraordinary thing to do. If you actually look in the newspaper, racing regularly has one to two pages devoted to it in the national newspapers, and it takes up the whole of that newsprint in race cards. Why? Because it's got four meetings a day. All of its space that should be taken to drumming up interest in the big race on Saturday — like with all the other sports — is taken up outlining what the card is at, you know, Fontwell or Redcar, which again is just so minority as to be an absolute joke.

十、希望的迹象：World Pool

So look, there's stuff that needs to happen. I will say that there are areas where things are improving.

The big tracks, together with the Tote in this country, have done a big deal over the last 5, 6, 7 years with the Hong Kong Jockey Club to produce something called World Pool. This is effectively a mechanism that allows punters from Hong Kong and China to bet into British racing, and that is starting to kick off some big money.

It's operated by the Tote here, in partnership with the Jockey Club. It's 100 days a year, of which — I can't remember the exact number — let's say 20 or 30-odd are here in the UK. But you also have days in various other racing jurisdictions when British people can bet into those pools as well. And that is now kicking off £20 million quid a year for the tracks here.

So Ascot, Newmarket, Epsom, Goodwood, and York are getting between them an extra £20 million quid a year as their betting off-take, effectively, from the Hong Kong market betting into the British market. That's an example of what you can achieve at the top end.

But we've really got to let go of the bottom end. The bottom end is not going to really get us anywhere in the next 10 years. I know it's bruising. I know it's Darwinian. But that is sport.

"Yeah, but it is sport — and that's business. So you're in support of what Ascot are doing here, basically, for those of us that aren't, as a good thing?"

I think they've been brave. I think that they've been strategic. And they now need the support of the Jockey Club.

收尾：Royal Ascot 见

"Okay, brilliant. Well, you and I are going to be in attendance. We're going to be there together."

So if anyone uh — if anyone's wondering how Charlie's tips get on, I'll let you know next week.

"Hanging off of Charlie's, but it's exactly the kind of thing where the person that knows far less than the one that knows a lot walks out with a bit of cash in hand."

I wouldn't bet on it.

"Oh — many of you are experts, come to me privately. Operation Let's Beat Methven."

Come on, let's do it.

"Uh, thank you again — because that is really interesting, as always, and a development on the last conversation. Really nice to revisit it and have a look at all of that. And the last time we did do it, it got a brilliant reaction. So I look forward to seeing what people say to all of…"

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反常的洞察：Ascot 退出协会，本质上是"君主制企业"对"民主否决权"的革命
表面看，Ascot 退出 Racecourses Association 是一次行业内政治动作。但深挖一层会发现一个极具讽刺意味的结构性悖论：**这场反抗"一人一票制"的运动，恰恰是由英国唯一一家由君主拥有的赛马场发起的**。
更耐人寻味的是——保守派（皇室）成了改革派，而所谓"民主"的赛马场协会反而成了停滞的同义词。这映射出一个被广泛忽视的治理规律：**当"一人一票"被应用在规模差异极大的成员之间时，它不再是民主，而是变成了"小者绑架大者"的合法工具**。这与 1980 年代英超脱离足球联盟的逻辑完全同构——但比足球更彻底，因为这次"造反者"自己背后站着的是国王。
## 二、跨领域连接：英国赛马的困境，本质上是"长尾经济学"被错误应用到供给端
互联网时代我们习惯了 Chris Anderson 的"长尾理论"——尾部的总量可以超过头部。但英国赛马展示了长尾理论的反面教材：**当长尾不是由消费者自发选择形成的，而是由补贴机制（Levy）人为维持时，长尾会反向掏空头部**。
90 mil

item.title: [All-In Podcast] Anthropic's Fable Backlash, Nationalizing AI, Inflation Heats Up & California’s Broken Elections
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Anthropic 的"道德剧场"其实是一场精算过的商业操作

表面上看，Anthropic 是在用"安全"约束自己——但更深一层看，这是一种反身性的商业武器化：通过把自己塑造成"危险到必须被监管的存在"，他们其实在为整个行业划定一条只有大资本玩家才能跨过的合规护城河。

最讽刺的反差点在于：他们一边发布 5000 字的博客警告"递归自我改进会毁灭世界"，一边花重金挖来 Andrej Karpathy 去主导递归自我改进的研究。这不是矛盾，这是结构性的伪善——把"恐惧"作为产品，把"监管诉求"作为竞争策略。

一个让人意外的观察：Anthropic 拒绝实施 KYC（实名认证），却要求政府对模型进行 FDA 式审批。这个组合很奇怪——直到你意识到，KYC 是他们自己可以承担的责任，而政府监管是对手（开源模型）无法承担的成本。他们选择不做能做的事，要求别人做不能做的事。这是经典的"提高对手成本"策略，跟当年烟草公司游说更严格的广告法（因为它们已经占据品牌心智、新进入者无法打广告）是同一个剧本。

二、隐藏的跨领域连接：AI 治理正在重演 1990 年代互联网的"分叉时刻"

播客中没有人点破的一个深层模式：当下的 AI 治理之争，结构上完全复刻了 1990 年代加密技术的"出口管制"之争。

当年美国政府试图把强加密技术列为"军火"禁止出口，结果是什么？真正的密码学创新流向了欧洲、以色列、最后是中国。今天，Anthropic 试图通过监管阻止开源前沿模型，结果将完全一致——Freeberg 已经在节目里实时演绎了这个过程：他作为一家美国基因组公司，正在被迫从美国闭源模型迁移到中国开源模型。

更深的跨领域连接：这与18世纪英国的《机器破坏法》是同构的。当时英国为保护手工业立法禁止机器出口和移民工人外流，结果是工业革命的红利流向了美国和德国。保护性立法在加速技术扩散方面，比自由市场更高效——这是历史上反复出现的反讽。

三、最深的洞见：这一集的"四个故事"其实是同一个故事

这期播客看似讲了四件事——Anthropic、Bernie 的征收提案、通胀飙升、加州选举——但它们其实是同一个底层结构的四个切面：

「中介层的腐化」。

  • Anthropic 是 AI 与用户之间的中介，他们在悄悄"降级"用户却不告知
  • Bernie 提议政府成为公民与 AI 财富之间的中介，强制征收 50%
  • 通胀本质上是货币作为价值储存中介的腐化——政府用印钞作为"隐形税"
  • 加州选举系统是选民意愿与政治结果之间的中介，已经被合法化的舞弊掏空

一个让人毛骨悚然的跨领域连接：Chamath 提到 AI 的"边际生产成本不为零"——这跟传统互联网完全不同。这意味着 AI 时代的垄断者必须租金提取（因为他们每多服务一个用户都在烧 GPU），而互联网时代的垄断者可以靠"免费"建立网络效应。

所以 AI 时代的商业模式天然亲和「中介层抽税」——Anthropic 必须筛选、降级、限制用户，因为不这样就赚不到钱。这跟政府征收、跟央行印钞、跟政治机器筛选选票，在抽象层面是同一种行为：在一个新的稀缺性出现时，谁能控制中介层，谁就能定义谁有资格通过。

最反直觉的推论：这意味着开源 AI 不仅是技术问题，而是对抗这一整代"中介层抽税"模式的唯一杠杆。Chamath 愿意亲自下场建 3 GW 数据中心（300 亿美金的代价！），不是因为他想做基础设施生意，而是因为他直觉地感知到：如果开源 AI 死了，未来三十年人类的认知接口将被三家公司+一个监管机构永久控制。这不是商业判断，是文明判断。

📝 转录文本

开场：Anthropic 与 Fable 5 模型

All right, everybody. Welcome back to the number one podcast in the world. It's your favorite podcast. It's your podcasters's favorite podcast. It's the podcast that everybody likes to rip off for 50 episodes and then they quit. But the All-In podcast is not quitting. We're doubling down with the original quartet. Everybody's here for a big big week, lots of debates, and we got to start with Anthropic.

Anthropic released a mythos level model with some interesting guardrails and uh seems as though Daario is back to blogging. Okay, the model's called Fable 5. No idea why they're calling it that or released on Tuesday and it tops every benchmark nearly every benchmark. The tokens, however, cost twice as much as Opus 4.8, but it should use less tokens overall because it's theoretically that much better. Unclear if this is going to alleviate some of the hand ringing around the cost of tokens, but you remember in April Anthropic famously did not release Mythos because publicly uh it had some strong hacking capabilities. So they gave it to some folks and we had um the CEO of Nesh of Palo Alto Networks who said, "Hey, Mythos was the real deal." and that they used it to seal up some vulnerabilities inside their shop.

Obviously, this new model is sensitive uh to topics like boweapons, hacking, all that stuff are blocked in it. But they got a big developer backlash and then I'll get your feedback after I explain this. While using Fable, Anthropic stores all the prompt data you enter for at least 30 days. And so that's a bit of a privacy issue. And if Fable 5 detects you're doing frontier AI research, in other words, using their model to make a better model, competitive model, they were downgrading you, but they weren't telling you. And this was buried deep in a 319 page document. So many kuruffles, freakouts going on on X over this, but they've since walked it back a bit, quote unquote, in Wired. We're changing Fable 5 safeguards for Frontier LLM development to make them more visible.

Chamath：审查风险与企业治理风险

I'm going to stop there. Chimath, what's your take on anthropic and these extraordinary models? They keep dropping and how they're handling the release of them. Are they being thoughtful? Are they being dramatic and drama queens? A little bit of both. Where do you stand on it? You know, after getting Nesh's feedback and seeing the latest model come out, have you played with it? What's going on at 8090 and the software factory in terms of benchmarking it as well?

One is Anthropic has essentially shown their hand which is that they will increasingly take in prompts, evaluate the prompts and decide what to do with them before they generate output to you.

And I think if you were a person, you should generally now think there's a risk of censorship. If you're a company, I think it's almost a non-starter. And the reason is because you could accidentally trip one of these things without even knowing it. A downstream scientist using the cloud APIs could trip it. A business executive inside your corporation could trip it. A person doing scientific molecular research could trip it. And all of a sudden, you'll get cut off from a very important source of business differentiation for yourself.

So I think if you take both of those two things together, we're at this very unique moment in time where I think companies need to start underwriting this next phase of AI, which is how do I have control? Who am I allowing to learn off of all of this information? Do I want to have single point of failure risk with respect to AI? And I think the answer is that you need broad diversity and a governance approach that's better managed.

One thing I'll say about anthropic is they tell the truth. Yes. It's just that the truth sucks when you actually take it and you eat it and you're like it's in your tummy and you're like no this is not good. I don't like this. And so there's the censorship risk on the one hand and then there's just the governance business risk for enterprises on the other. Both are not good.

Freeberg：原子弹的类比与监管原则

Can I just respond to your comment about Daario? I think because I've heard this from a number of people that I really do respect on their concerns about what models enable. You know, when we discovered that how the atom could be split, you could make nuclear energy and have effectively unlimited cheap energy for the world, you could also make an atomic bomb. If you think about what the models are — we did both. We did both. That's right.

And there was a lot of scientists, altruists who were very concerned about the progress on the atomic bomb and fought back against it who were actually active in the Manhattan Project and post the Manhattan Project express their opinions very soundly, very very loudly. And I think that's the moment we're in right now that there is a weaponization potential of these sorts of tools.

I would argue that there's three kinds of weapons. There's cyber weapons, there's physical weapons, and there's bioweapons. And we can sit here and diagnose how these tools can be used to manifest advantages for creating better physical weapons, for creating novel cyber weapons, and creating novel boweapons. But the truth is that the capabilities that allow that weaponization are the same capabilities that I'm describing that can be used to cure cancer, to make more food, that can be used to make software tools that create extraordinary leverage for people, that increase their income, that give everyone the ability to be an entrepreneur and make millions of dollars and live a good life.

The same tools are hand in hand. And fundamentally I don't think it's about restricting access to the tools which is unfortunately how it is being manifest. But the restrictions and any regulation any observation should be organized around the manifestation of the tools in creating weapons and weaponization of the tools.

If we go back to the Manhattan project, we know what the answer is. Technology is fundamentally deterministic. Whatever is possible will be tried at least once. And so we've already let this thing out of the box. So this idea that all of a sudden we can manage to get it back in and we're going to let a private citizen or a set of private citizens adjudicate what and to whom is insane.

I think it's the output that needs to be adjudicated in some manner. We already have product liability laws. We already have laws that make it illegal to design weapons. We already have laws against cyber espionage and cyber attacks and hacking. We have laws against creating bioweapons. I think the enforcement of those laws, the mechanism by which there can be tracking and early estimation of those uses of the technology. I'm not trying to be naive here. I recognize there have to be guard rails and there have to be stage gates put into these systems. But limiting the systems themselves is denying us both the economic opportunity, the job creation opportunity and also the world changing opportunity that these tools can enable.

So I think that we're going about it the wrong way by trying to create these gates all the way up front on the technology being available. Imagine if we did this with computers.

By the way, they tried to do this with the internet, remember? And there had been efforts.

Yeah. Could you imagine, Freeberg, if the internet worked in the following way? You would put in a URL, you'd hit enter, and then somebody decides whether to send you to that website or a different website.

That's China. That's the firewall in China. That's right. Or like in Google, you search for something because you want to get to ground truth and it just decides based on who it thinks you are, what sites get.

That's what we're — You're exactly right. And what we're what's being debated today with respect to regulation on AI is exactly that manifestation that you're talking about. It's the ability to let people stage gate up front what can and can't be seen, what you can and can't do versus manifesting a regulatory scheme that says you cannot create weapons like which is what we need to focus on.

DNA 合成筛查：一个合理的下游管控点

Well, there's — by the way, there are things you can do on the safety side. So, Freeberg, you didn't mention this, but I'd be curious to get your your take. So, the major AI labs along with lots of other signatories sent this letter recently in support of — it's called in support of mandatory nucleic acid synthetic screening and recordkeeping. And basically what it says is that if you make an order to go to a lab to manufacture a sequence of synthetic DNA or RNA, they already have to check it against a database to make sure that you know they're not creating a bioweapon — a boweapon, Ebola, whatever.

Yep. That is based on an agreement called the international gene synthesis consortium of 2009 in which all the major labs agree to develop and implement voluntary safeguards against misuse. That makes sense to me. And now what they want to do is codify that. So they've had, you know, over 15 years to dial in the system and make it work. Almost everyone abides by it voluntarily. Now they're saying we should make it mandatory. Okay, that seems reasonable to me.

So to Freeberg's point about at what stage do you intervene, maybe it's not at the level of who gets to use the model, but if you try to turn it into output in the physical world. And by the way, a lot of the oligosynthesis company, the companies that make these nucleic acid sequences, when you order RNA or you order DNA to use in your lab for some sort of experiment, which every lab in the world does, all those CEOs have also signed on to this letter saying that they'd like to codify it because of all the fear-mongering that's going on. There's already been this effort for years now to ensure that this sort of thing doesn't happen. But they're just basically underlining it and highlighting like, hey, we can automate this. We can make it part of a regulated or legislative process, whatever you guys want, because we're already comfortable doing it. And it's a good place to put a safeguard because they've made it fast. They've made it efficient and doesn't hold up research.

So, I think these sorts of ideas can extend into these other areas of concern, but the output, I think, is quite a different place than the input than the access. We'll see how this all shakes out, but there's clearly some market structuring effort underway as we're talking about this, right?

But I think what it points to is that there's multiple places where you can intervene and put guard rails on. It doesn't have to be in this very crude way of you ask fable about mitochondria or cancer and GLP1s, which just seems like an incredibly broad restriction. There are places further down where you could basically intervene and that would pose less of a risk to freedom of speech and just gatekeeping. Who's going to have access to these capabilities? Who gets to watch the watchman? Who put Dario in charge of all this? I think is a very valid question and we should be vigilant about it.

And then people can take them and they can evolve them and they can use their own data. It's out there. You can't stop that capability, which is going to be extraordinarily beneficial for the world. So we really do have to kind of rethink how we're planning to step in and ensure that nefarious negative disemployment all the things that we're worried about are addressed downstream from the technology capability. You can't just turn off the internet and you can't just turn off the typewriter and you can't turn off the Gutenberg press.

Freeberg, you hate — they asked for or hate hate hate wealth taxes aka asset seizures as you pointed out in our private group chat with redacted this very week. What's your take on this?

So we have the ship has sailed in terms of the United States government providing a social safety net to its citizens. We've formed the Social Security trust fund decades ago and tens of millions of Americans depend on it for their retirement benefits and to survive. It is one of the last few defined benefit programs left in America. Pretty much all the rest of them are also government retirement programs. All of us private citizens and private companies have defined contribution programs. You put in $10 every week. That $10 sits in an account. It gets invested and it grows over time and when you retire you see exactly how much money you individually have as opposed to a guaranteed benefit coming out from the collective.

This presents a great opportunity and I will repeat this every couple months when another opportunity presents itself to restructure our broken bankrupt social security system. I've said it in the past I will restate it. There is a great moment right now to take the social security trust fund which currently has one certificate in it. It is a certificate that is a very unique US Treasury certificate. The US government owes the Social Security trust fund $4 trillion. That's all it is. It's a bond. They're only allowed to own treasuries.

We should revamp that. We should change the system so that the Social Security trust fund can own equities. That should then turn into an account-based system so every individual has an account rather than a benefit. And then we should use that capital to go and buy stocks. We should buy great stocks in great American companies that are going to reinvent the world.

And I believe that this opportunity to build a sovereign wealth fund for the United States is actually a very smart idea. That capital goes into that enterprise. They earn share certificates. Those share certificates sit in the accounts of American citizens. So now everyone will be an owner in these businesses. Social security should be reformed into a sovereign wealth fund. It should be actively managed. It should make investments in AI companies and everyone should participate that way.

And I will say one more thing. There is no job loss with AI. I will say it again. I've said it a thousand times and I will say it again and again and again. The real opportunity with AI is on the revenue side where suddenly one engineer can do a hundred times or a thousand times what they used to be able to do. Meaning you can make more products at your company, whether those are agricultural seed products or boats and ships or software for companies or clothing or what have you. Because of AI, everyone has the ability to expand their revenue base to create more products and that is the foundation of good economic prosperity. It is called productivity. Now, there is something very important we need to observe about the economics of AI and I think you can understand it best if you compare it to the economics of the internet. The crazy thing about the internet, the reason why the Facebooks and the Googles massively overearned for decades is the marginal cost of production for a new user on the internet is effectively zero. The incremental search user costs nothing. The incremental social networking user costs nothing, but you're able to use them as part of a network effect to sell ads against, make infinite money. It's like a money glitch. Okay?

AI is completely different. There is a real cost for every marginal user. Everyone you stand up is taxing a GPU. Everyone you stand up needs electrons. Everything needs memory. And so you get into this situation where there needs to be this downstream ROI, but there also needs to be all this critical infrastructure that even enables you to be on the field.

That's probably the single best argument that if I were sitting in government, I would make as to why I should own a percentage of these companies. It is not dissimilar to having the first set of transportation companies that run on the interstate highway. And if the interstate highways were built by the federal government writ large and now you had two companies that transported all the goods, a logical question at that time could have been how much of that should I own because you're riding on my rails?

And if you look at it through that lens, there is critical infrastructure and national resilience that the AI labs profoundly need. Otherwise, they're not in a position to be in business. Yet, even still, the marginal cost of production is incredibly high. So, I think that if you were going to go and take a piece of these companies, you have incredible leverage. You have to construct this case precisely. But if it were me running the sovereign wealth fund, yeah, I'd probably own 75% of these companies when I was done. But that's me cuz and I can negotiate. Yeah. And I don't think Bernie can negotiate out of a paper bag. So he'll just throw this out there and it'll go nowhere.

That is the key observation that I hope everybody listening in a position of power understands. The incremental cost of performing AI is excessive and large. That contrasts and compares to the incremental cost before AI which was zero. Do with that information what you will.

Yeah. I mean my interpretation of that would be yeah it's a fixed cost business. You can add people after a certain cost. That doesn't have an incremental cost and that's what locally run open source models would do in this instance. That's what I would take from it.
