So it's rather like a sort of, dare I say it, the English voting public basically only wanting to vote in governments who promise to give them lots of free things. And it's the same thing with Real, where it seems like the only way that you can get elected as president is by promising the fans that you're going to sign dump loads of massively expensive players the club can't afford — Haaland, Roger II, and bring in Jurgen Klopp, I think with a three… Raquelme promises.

So then if you're Perez, then you're saw — you, okay, well, you know, I have to — I had to play that game a little bit, which he always has done, by the way. And so that's where this whole sort of Alvarez thing then comes from. So just — just the whole thing, I think, is starting — what that Atletico riposte says is that they're actually just losing respect in the football industry. Yeah. Is that people now, other clubs now, are feeling free and able to go… bit of a joke.

Atlético 与 Barcelona 的幽默反讽

Do you have the Atletico riposte? Actually, don't. It was something along the lines… I saw it. Firstly, Madrid release a statement on the bid that they've made — kind of weird in a way anyway. And they say it's been rejected, but Atletico were grateful for the bid, something along those lines I have in front of me. Anyway, Atletico's response was basically, "we're grateful for nothing. We're not…" I can't put it all together. They did it in a very funny way. I wish I had it in front of me, but they did it in a very funny way, in a sort of slightly offbeat way that made the rest of the football world laugh with them rather than laugh at them, right?

Barcelona had a similar — they also bid for Julian Alvarez. And I think they had a similar kind of response to that. It was a bit like them saying, "well, we'll trade you Bad Bunny tickets" — who's playing, you know, your old favourite Bad Bunny, the Super Bowl halftime show. He's playing at the Metropolitano. And they said, "well, how about we trade you some Bad Bunny tickets for the Mineral?" So they're getting a good sense of humour.

But if anything, quite interestingly, while this is all a bit stupid, as you said, I don't think Atletico ever believed that Real had any intention. No. It's a statement, so it doesn't really bother them. But what it does do is it puts a market price potentially on Alvarez. So it does help them in the sales process if they turn around and say, "well, look, hey, Madrid bid €150 million. So Arsenal and Barcelona and whoever else wants them, you're going to need to stump that cash up."

It does. I think you're right. It's kind of turning one of the great clubs into a little bit of a laughing stock in football circles. Yeah, particularly as they're not winning anything. It's one thing behaving like a bunch of arrogant, entitled people when you're winning every year and people have to like it or lump it. But when you're getting thumped, it doesn't really work, does it? No, it doesn't. The whole thing just looks stupid. It does. Anyway, there we are.

四、英国赛马业的内战：Ascot 退出 RCA

重访赛马产业话题

Moving on, because we've got some meaty topics to discuss today, Charles, on my birthday. We do indeed, and it's quite exciting, isn't it? Well, it's exciting for me. It's exciting for me. You've given me a little birthday treat. You're about to get stuck into one of your absolute pinnacles of breakdown life topic. Well, that's what we want to get to.

Yes, so quite early on in our breakdown journey, Charlie threw the dog a bone and let me do an in-depth breakdown of the horse racing industry, which sits in a rather strange place within the sporting ecosystem, being broadly speaking, other than soccer, football, the biggest sports in the world, but often not as apparently so to other sports fans. It has its own fan ecosystem, but it's a big industry. And we outlined that last time, how the bloodstock industry — the breeding of horses — makes the football transfer business look like a very small thing indeed. You'll find that even the very biggest transfers in football only amount to one year's stud fees for a big stallion. So the value of Frankel is a long way beyond any footballer and they do it over a much more protracted period of time as well.

So we went through that last time, and actually it was very gratifying the number of people who wrote in saying, "well, I never really knew that." So this is taking that discussion on, because one of the things that I outlined in that discussion which happened just before Cheltenham — actually, it was in Cheltenham week, wasn't it — was some of the challenges that are facing the industry, some of the dysfunction existing within the industry, and pointing up the possibility for some pretty nasty battles down the track as the industry gets to grips with those challenges.

And topically now, just for people, the reason it's a good time is because we just had Epsom and we're about to have Ascot. So again, this is kind of another major point. Derby last weekend, Royal Ascot next week. Yeah. So front of everyone's minds on the sporting calendar. Yeah.

Epsom Derby 的衰落

And… okay, where to start? Can I ask you quickly about Epsom? There were a couple of things that were interesting on that side. So Epsom — from an attendance piece, interested in, from what I've seen outlined in broader media, is that it's not what it used to be. That's putting it mildly. The focus is — that's putting it mildly. Okay, brilliant. My goodness.

The other piece was the Derby in particular. On radio, as you actually explained to me yesterday as well, but moving it from a Wednesday to a Saturday has seriously damaged its brand. Its brand, yeah.

Yeah, so okay, let's just address that thing. The Derby historically was the UK's biggest spectator sport occasion. If you go back to the decade after the Second World War, how many people do you think pitched up on that one day — and it was a Wednesday? A hundred thousand? Yeah. Two hundred and fifty thousand. Wow. Yeah. So what — like the whole size of a Cheltenham meet basically in one day? 250,000 people used to set up the Derby — basically the whole of the East End and the City.

The boys! Imagine the mixture, right? So the East End on the one hand and then next door to it the City on the other hand, all decamped to Epsom for the day. They closed down the markets etc. It was like a huge London day out.

And by the time that I sort of came along, which as everyone can now work out was about 40 years ago — 1976, we're talking about. Yeah, but I can't remember that far back. By the time that I was alive and awake to what the Derby was, I guess I was in my early teens, so we're talking about the mid-1980s. And by this point, it wasn't quite that. And attendances, you know — I think we were living through an age where people were starting to get a little bit more serious about workdays and all that type of stuff, so getting the entire of London out on a Wednesday afternoon wasn't quite as straightforward as it had once been. However, however, it was still totally normal for the stands — which is effectively the paid enclosures, which are on the sort of inner side, or the outer side rather, of the racetrack — to be sold out at about 40,000, that kind of thing, 40-45,000. And then the hill, which is the inside of the track, on a nice day, that could have 50, 60,000 on it. So you're talking about a total attendance about 100,000 when I was young.

从星期三搬到星期六：品牌错误

They switched it to a Saturday after a bit because they were like, "well, you know, we're starting to struggle a bit for the paid attendance. The hill does well, but the paid attendance is a bit lower. So if we switch it to a Saturday, a more typical sporting day, we'll do better." I always felt that was a mistake because when you own your own brand position, the key thing is to understand that that distinctiveness is part of what makes your event distinctive, different etc.

But also you just saying a more traditional sporting day is exactly that trap, right? Because they don't want to be in a more traditional sporting day, because then you compete with all of the other sports on a Saturday. Exactly. I mean, really what they had done is they actually just stopped marketing it properly. So, you know, there was a generation of Londoners growing up who weren't kind of really that aware of it, but they thought, "okay, quick fix — move it to a Saturday, we'll get an extra few punters in because it's their day off etc." And it kind of — it worked a bit.

So by the sort of turn of the century, by the early 2000s, you're probably seeing a stand paid-for attendance of about 40,000 and then another 10, 20,000 on the hill, right? So you're sort of a total attendance about 60,000. So then gradually drops off, drops off, drops off, drops off, and finally hits a low last year, 2025, with a paid-for attendance, I think I'm right in saying, about 22,000, which is broadly speaking — pardon the word — crap. Right? There are average race meetings in the calendar — courses you, Charlie Stebbings, will barely have heard of — that get 20,000. Right? So for the Derby to only get 20,000, bearing in mind it's on the outskirts of London with its own railway station, right? So it is so easy to get to, such a spectacular occasion, you know, it's so famous, and yet they were only getting 20,000 to it.

500 万英镑营销 vs 天气

So what they decided to do this year was plough a huge amount of money into marketing it. By racing standards, I believe — and I'm told — they spent about £5 million marketing the Derby this year. And sadly… it rained. £5 million. £5 million, I'm told, right? Now, I stand to be corrected on that, but I was told this morning by someone inside racing who should know, that they spent £5 million marketing the Derby this year in the hope of getting a massive attendance on the hill — which obviously is open air, right? So you are vulnerable to the weather.

So one can look back with the benefit of hindsight and say, you know, given racing's budgets, which we're going to come on to in a moment, was it a wise thing to devote such a massive amount of money to something which is so weather dependent? That is hindsight, right? If it had been a gloriously sunny day, then we'd look back and go, "well, you know, that was a brilliant way to spend money." You know, it's early to mid-June. It's normally nice weather. If you're ever going to have a moment when you can get loads of people to come… So I'm a bit loathe to criticise them on that specific point. But I am concerned about the overall direction of marketing racing, and I'm going to come back to that later on.

Royal Ascot 的繁荣 vs 治理危机

But let's talk about Ascot, because Ascot is an event that is not struggling. Ascot is an event that is absolutely booming, right? So having just said that there are 25,000 people at the Derby, at Ascot, almost all of which happens during the week, okay, with one day on the Saturday. So you get Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday during the week, and then you've got the Saturday tacked on the end of it. 300,000 people will turn up for that. So you're talking about an average of 60,000 a day going to the Royal Meeting at Ascot. And the revenues are really, really, really considerable.

So Ascot as a whole, for people who don't know, Ascot as a racecourse is owned by the King. Exactly. The Ascot Authority is owned by the King, the Royal Family. The total revenue for the year is £120 million. So it's a pretty big sporting concern, right? It's not much less than some of the Premier League clubs. And of that, £80 million is the Royal Meeting. Wow. Right. So Royal Ascot over four days — five days actually — turns over £80 million quid, which is 70% of Ascot's total revenue for the year. That's a lot of exposure on your business. It is. It is. But in a way, like, what can go wrong? Yeah. Like, the Royal Family will turn up. COVID. Yeah. COVID. Yeah. Right. Indeed. As with everyone.

Prize money: £11 million quid. It's an interesting little footnote going back to the tennis players' complaints about percentages of prize money and all that type of stuff. That's about the same percentage as the tennis players get, more or less.

Ascot 退出 RCA：一场内战的开始

And, you know, all's rosy with the world. Ascot, fantastic. Isn't it doing well? But all is not rosy with the world. Because Ascot has started a civil war inside the horse racing world, which is super super interesting because generally speaking Ascot is very conservative, because it is aware as an entity that it's of its owner who doesn't tend to be somebody who's going to want to have a lot of controversy around him or her, as it used to be. So when Ascot steps out of line, they can't just decide — they have to go up to the Palace and say "by the way this is what we're going to do".

What have they done? They have resigned from the Racecourse Association. And this happened about three weeks ago. They announced that "we're leaving the Racecourse Association." Might seem like a small enough thing. It's not. It's a big thing.

RCA 的治理结构

What is the Racecourse Association? The Racecourse Association is the trade body that represents racecourses. So what? The reason why that's important is the way in which British horse racing is governed at something called the British Horseracing Authority, the BHA level, is that there are three entities which have control over the BHA and what the BHA does and therefore what the whole industry does:

  • Racecourses, currently represented by the Racecourse Association — we'll come back to that later on.
  • Then you've got the sort of the participants, so to speak, trainers, jockeys, breeders etc, etc — all those types of people.
  • Then you've got the owners, the Racehorse Owners Association — slightly strange body actually, but whatever, there we are.

But out of those groups, the Racecourse Association is the dominant bit because they represent bigger businesses, whereas a lot of the other associations represent fundamentally sole traders. The Professional Jockeys Association is each individual jockey. The National Trainers Federation is representing each individual trainer etc. So for the Racecourse Association to have a third of the power in racing actually ends up being more like 50%. So it really has a blocking vote over British horse racing doing anything.

一人一票，与 1980 年代英格兰足球的相似

And the way it votes — the Racecourse Association is the way that the Football League used to vote before the Premier League. Right? Which is there are 58 courses, and there were 92 clubs in the Premier League and each course has one vote. So if you've got some tiny little course that you've never heard of that only races six or seven times a year and gets no one through the gate — one vote. Get Ascot — one vote.

And what's been happening over a long period of time is that the small courses, of which there are more than big courses, just like there would have been in the old Football League, have been coagulating together to block progress and to block the ability of British racing to really compete on the world stage.

改革困境：要么涨奖金，要么减赛事

Can I bring it back just quickly to something you were saying last time? Is that part of the problem then when you're looking at your solution, or that you posited last time around reducing the total number, creating the higher quality of products, and therefore if you're a smaller racecourse, you recognise you're going to lose out on that because you're going to lose your meets, and therefore your business is… You've got it. You've got it, right?

Which is — there's something called the levy. The levy is a government body which takes money from all the money bet on horse racing and redistributes it into prize money. And that spits off for the support of prize money about £90 million a year. And this is absolutely critical because all the other racing jurisdictions — Hong Kong, Japan, Australia, France, Ireland — have state-run betting organisations which feed money into their sport, right? And in most of those cases, they are monopolies, right? So you can only bet with that.

So if you go to France to go racing in France, there are not lots of bookmakers. There's just something called a Pari Mutuel, which is their equivalent to the Tote. And everyone has to bet with a Pari Mutuel, and then a portion of — the vast majority of the Pari Mutuel's profits are just funnelled straight into prize money, right?

In this country, we have bookmakers, and bookmakers have been there long enough and are embedded in the whole thing long enough that there's no realistic prospect of getting rid of them. And they're a big part of the landscape, and they're big lobbying powers at Westminster etc. So it's kind of accepted, "okay, we're never going to get that benefit which is sad", because if we did, probably Britain would be the dominant racing jurisdiction in the world.

But the bookmakers have to make up for it a bit. And they do so — they are forced to do so by the government through the levy. So, you know, X amount percent of what they make out of horse racing gets funnelled back into prize money. Horse racing, £90 million. And then the Levy Board decides where that £90 million goes. Right. And at the moment, it gets distributed all the way through all these little courses and meetings and all this type of stuff.

1,450 场赛事的数学

I'll just give you a bit of idea of how this works, Charlie. There are 1,500 race fixtures a year. Just think about that — 1,500. So if we look just — five a day. Yeah, basically. Yeah, almost four maybe. So if — I think it's 1,450 — so yeah, round it down. So we're about four a day, right? So that breaks down roughly into 900 flat fixtures and 600 — sorry, 900 flat fixtures and about 550 jumps fixtures.

Just put the jump racing to one side for a moment. It's on the flat where we have to compete against the rest of the world. Jumps, it's only Ireland really. Only Ireland and Britain have jump racing. So you've got 900 flat fixtures, of which 600 are raced on turf and 300-odd, 350-odd are on what we call all-weather tracks. Again, put those to one side a little bit here, although they do matter.

So you've got this levy of £90 million, of which let's say about £60 million is going to go on flat racing and £30 million on jump racing. I'm just making the numbers round. And that £60 million is being spread throughout 900 fixtures.

与香港、日本、澳洲对比

Okay. Let's go to Hong Kong, right? One of the most successful racing jurisdictions in the world. The Hong Kong Jockey Club is the monopoly provider of betting. And they funnel £170 million pounds of their profits into prize money. Okay? So you've got £90 million coming in Britain and you've got £170 million coming in Hong Kong. Okay, fair enough, Britain can make up the difference with better sponsorships and more people on track and all this type of stuff. Hong Kong has 88 meetings a year. So they have £170 million for 88 meetings. We have £90 million for 900 — 900, well yeah — that's you right, 1,450 meetings, right?

So fundamental problem. And then on top of that, loads of that money gets funnelled to the minor tracks, many of whom are owned by one company called Arena Racing Company, ARC, who are basically — they actually do have one big track, Doncaster, but all their other tracks are small tracks. And they basically exist on a levy model. "Okay, we're going to get given X amount by the Levy Board per fixture. If we keep the prize money really low, and if we keep all of our costs really low, then we've got a business." Right? I won't bore you with what that business looks like, but it's a successful business. It makes, I think, sort of £50, £60, £70 million pounds a year. All of that money is being kept away from the top end of the sport.

That's not true — because the top end of the sport does get some money from the Levy Board, but nowhere near enough — because that's when you get into the ability to compete. So if you look at the Epsom Derby, the most famous horse race in the world probably, together with the Kentucky Derby, its equivalent in the US:

  • Epsom Derby prize money: £2 million. Not bad.
  • Japan Cup: £5 million.
  • Arc de Triomphe in France: £4.5 million.
  • Melbourne Cup in Australia: £5 million.

So you're seeing that we are well off the pace. And it gets even worse at the races which are just below the top level. And this is a critical category of races. These are for the horses who aren't quite good enough to be absolute superstars, but who are still really, really good. It's the equivalent of the Championship in football, right? It's the equivalent of the bottom Premier League and the Championship in football.

At that level, that's where the levy squeeze — you know, of it going to lots of bad races and not going into that level — is really hurting English racing. Because what it means is that prize money at that level is so much lower than the equivalent in all these other foreign jurisdictions for horses of that type, that these horses are just exodus. It's just absolute exodus. It's rather like English footballers in the 1980s. They are all being sold to race in South Africa, Hong Kong, Australia, the Middle East etc, because there is just not the prize money for them here.

That then has a further impact in the industry because effectively when you're asking somebody to pay a lot of money to buy a racehorse, you cannot guarantee however much you pay for that horse, however well bred that horse is, you can't guarantee he's going to be a Premier League footballer, right? But you've got to — if he's well bred enough, you've got a very, very good chance he will at least be able to be a class below that. But if the class of racing below that is not economic, then suddenly you've got a real problem.

Ascot 真正想做的事

So if I'm reading this right, you have two options here to correct this. You either increase the prize money total or you reduce the number of meets and focus that prize money on fewer meetings. When you say you can either increase the prize money, how would you do that? Well, exactly. So it would just become a bigger number and a bigger percentage of your total revenues, right? Of whose revenues? The racecourses. But they're not very profitable.

To my point, whether it's possible or not, if you're in a situation, you either do that and it screws your business model — let's say football and wages — even paying well beyond what they make, or you reduce the number of races and focus the prize money to a smaller group of racecourses and meetings. And fundamentally, this is what Ascot wants to do.

What Ascot wants is to try — this is what sits behind this political move, in my belief. They haven't publicly said this, but I believe this to be the case — is that Ascot basically says, "if we want this sport to flourish in this country, it will only flourish because of the top end. You're not going to get suddenly lots of new people becoming involved in the sport because they want to basically bet on something that looks like a dog track somewhere. They're going to get interest in the sport because they see Royal Ascot on TV." Be realistic about it. Okay?

So in other words, for them to be able to make the changes — or for the British Horseracing Authority to be able to make the changes to the fixture list that are required, and for the Levy Board to be able to make the changes the Levy Board distribution is required — it can't be blocked by the Racecourse Association. At the moment, the Racecourse Association can simply block that. And because the Racecourse Association is operating on one member, one vote, it's therefore operating on behalf of the majority of courses, which are the smaller courses.

三类赛马场的结构

So just very quickly:

  • There are six leading independent courses, big ones: Ascot, Goodwood, York, Chester, Newbury, and Ayr in Scotland.
  • Then you've got the Jockey Club, who own seven large courses — Cheltenham, Aintree, Epsom, Newmarket so on and so forth — and eight small ones.
  • And then you've got ARC, which is 16 courses, one big one, Doncaster, and 15 small ones.

Right. So ARC, being the group, then gangs up with these small independents — the little ones which don't have a home and aren't part of a bigger company — and act effectively as a block vote at the RCA.

呼吁 Jockey Club 出手

So the Jockey Club and Ascot said some time ago, and the other leading independents, "we need a change in governance. And you, the RCA, have to come back to us and explain how you're going to change the way you're set up." Now, it's interesting because this happened in football in the 1980s. The big clubs went to the Football League and said, "guys, this can't carry on." Famously, I can't remember which of the big clubs said, "I'm not having Grimsby Town" — whichever small club he picked — "telling the rest of us how to run this industry." Tail wagging the dog, it's not going to work, right? And when the Football League didn't address those concerns, the leading clubs went, "okay, we're out."

That's what's happening here, right? Which the RCA did not come back with a change in governance. As a result of that, Ascot could have gone, "well, we told you, we warned you, we're off."

Big question now to the Jockey Club, right? Because actually Ascot have been quite bold here, given that they are one course and given the reputational issues surrounding being owned by the King. The Jockey Club has no such constraints. And I've publicly called now in both a couple of speeches I've made and also on this podcast, please, the Jockey Club, show some leadership. This is your sport. The members of the Jockey Club include most of the biggest owners who in turn employ the biggest trainers who in turn employ the biggest jockeys. And they have most of the big courses, or at least a big chunk of the big courses as well. If the stewards of the Jockey Club — which are the people who ultimately control the Jockey Club, not the chief executive; the chief executive has to report into the stewards — if the stewards of the Jockey Club said "Ascot's right, we're going to revolutionise British horse racing", then it would happen.

Now, I've spoken to former chief executives of the Jockey Club. I've worked for the Jockey Club in many different guises over the years. I've been a consultant there. So I do know a lot of the people who have been there. I think you know a few of them as well. And they are all — all the ones I've spoken to are very, very clear: ultimately, the stewards of the Jockey Club have to take a lead. You can't expect the chief exec of the Jockey Club, who's a paid employee, to drive this. This has to come from the stewards, and they have to understand their responsibility to the future of racing.

Jockey Club 的内部阻力

So can I just ask, from my side, again, it looks like this would make a huge amount of sense for the Jockey Club, being those massive racecourses. But they've also got a lot of small ones. So what are they looking at internally and saying, the negative impact of us deciding to do this is what?

Well, I think first of all, they do have small racecourses in their portfolio. Now, my view on that, which is driven because when you've been a sports executive, you know it's difficult to make these businesses work, is you might have to close some of those down. Right? If they don't make sense, or say to them, basically let them float away on their own — give them a board, give them a bit of a cash float, and say "if you can make sense for yourself, great. If you can't, we can't keep on subsidising you any longer." That's not — like, we can't be giving you extra.

So let me give you an example. By keeping these small courses afloat who all need a Jockey Club subsidy effectively, that then is taking money away, which is needed for the redevelopment of Sandown Park, which is one of their big ones, which is currently, facilities-wise, an absolute joke. It's like stepping back into the 1980s. That is just a classic example where you've got a course which should be absolutely part of the elite, but the Jockey Club's balance sheet can't support it because the Jockey Club's constantly having to shovel money out the side to support the small courses.

So there are vested interests within the Jockey Club who have links to those small courses who will go — whatever they might say, right — that absolutely will happen. I remember making this speech at a horse racing event with a whole bunch of Jockey Club people there, and two or three of them saying, "you know, absolutely spot on there." And then you spotted around the people who are on the boards of the smaller courses, their brows furrowing. And I said, "look, if you want to hold point-to-point racing", right, which is amateur jump racing at your courses, which can, by the way, pull a very big crowd — I've been to point-to-point meetings where there have been 10,000 people there. Point-to-point, point-to-point, yeah etc. "But what are you holding? You're holding really low-end racing that no one wants to come to. So what's the point? I know it's a nice place for you to go to lunch six or seven times a year, whatever it might be, and to invite friends to and to be a local grandee, but it's destroying British horse racing."

应当压缩到 20 家赛场

British horse racing, probably, in my view, instead of 58 courses, the levy needs to be supporting — I'm going to be generous and say — 20. 20 courses. 20 courses that would then enable us to be competitive at the top end and just below the top end. And then it would enable us to drive a narrative around the top end of racing, which would make more sense to the general public, rather than saying to the general public, "oh, by the way, if you want to get interest in horse racing, you only have to follow 1,450 meetings every year."

It's an extraordinary thing to do. If you actually look in the newspaper, racing regularly has one to two pages devoted to it in the national newspapers. And it takes up the whole of that newsprint in race cards. Why? Because it's got four meetings a day. So all of its space that should be taken to drumming up interest in the big race on Saturday, like with all the other sports, is taken up outlining what the card is at, you know, Fontwell or Redcar — you know, which again is just so minority as to be an absolute joke.

World Pool：好消息的一面

So look, there's stuff that needs to happen. I will say that there are areas where things are improving. So for instance, the big tracks together with the Tote in this country have done a big deal over the last five, six, seven years with the Hong Kong Jockey Club to produce something called World Pool. Now this is effectively a mechanism that allows punters from Hong Kong and China to bet into British racing. And that is starting to kick off some big money.

So it's operated by the Tote here in partnership with the Jockey Club. It's a 100 days a year, of which I can't remember the exact number, but let's say 20 or 30 — 30-odd are here in the UK, but you also have days in various other racing jurisdictions when British people can bet into those pools as well. And that is now kicking off £20 million quid a year for the tracks here. So Ascot, Newmarket, Newbury, Epsom, Goodwood and York are getting between them an extra £20 million quid a year as their betting-off-take effectively from the Hong Kong market betting into the British market.

So that's an example of what you can achieve at the top end. Okay, but we really got to let go of the bottom end. The bottom end is not going to really get us anywhere in the next 10 years. I know it's bruised. I know it's Darwinian, but that is sport. Yeah, but it is sport and it's business.

So you're in support of what Ascot are doing here, basically. For those of us that don't understand, this is a good thing that Ascot have done. I think they've been brave. I think that they've been strategic. And they now need the support of the Jockey Club. Brilliant.

Well, you and I are going to be in attendance. We are. We're going to be there together, Charlie. So if anyone's wondering how Charlie's tips get on, I'll let you know next week. Hanging off all the Charlies. It's exactly the kind of thing where the person that knows far less than the one that knows a lot walks out with a bit of cash in hand. I wouldn't bet on it. To the challenge — if any of you are experts, come to me privately. Operation: let's beat Mef. Come on, let's do it.

Thank you again because that is really interesting, um, as always and a development on the last conversation. Really nice to revisit it and have a look at all of that. And the last time we did do it, it got a brilliant reaction. So I look forward to seeing what people say to all of this.

五、FIFA 世界杯：票务定价的战略灾难

一场表现导向之外的商业故事

Um, Charlie, this is not the only big thing happening, is it? Because the World Cup, finally. A little matter of the World Cup, which may not have as many people at all of its early stage games as Royal Ascot. Exactly. Yeah, we're talking about 250,000 people at the Derby. Well, there's a fair few people that they'd like to transition into the US for some World Cup games on current data.

Look, people have been saying to me, it's interesting, how are you going to deal with the World Cup? Because, you know, it's very performance driven, isn't it? And I think that is true to an extent. You know, there's a lot of what will happen over the next few weeks that will be very much driven on results. However, with this World Cup in particular being in the US, there are some really interesting underlying stories that have been a huge part of the build-up, and I would imagine will play a continued part actually during the tournament.

Normally — I don't know how you feel — normally I feel like, particularly with the last few when we've had them in Russia and we've had them in Qatar, even South Africa, I remember in 2010 there was a lot of conversation around stadiums not being ready, in the political environment. That quite often has stopped to an extent once the football starts. This feels like it could continue. And I fear a huge part of that is going to be because there's going to be a fair few stadiums that are far from full.

And if you've ever watched football, if you ever watched the Club World Cup in the US last summer… sorry, what was that? Exactly, the incredible trophy. Pizza trophy. Chelsea. Pizza trophy. Yeah, the Johnstone's Paint. You know, it was an extraordinary sight when you had these massive teams playing in virtually empty stadiums in the US. Which massive teams? Well, Paris Saint-Germain, Real Madrid, Man City, and the biggest of all, obviously, Palmeiras.

营收目标：90–130 亿美元

But there's a huge amount to unpick from this World Cup commercially from a business perspective. So I picked out a few of them just for us to have a quick discussion in now before the tournament starts. And they're things that I imagine we'll be coming back to.

Some top line numbers that are interesting for us to have a look at just on the total World Cup. So the revenue target for this World Cup for FIFA is between $9 billion to $13 billion. And that was $7 billion. So let's say $10 or $11 billion. Call it $11. We call it $11 billion median outcome. Qatar was $7.5 billion in 2022. So you're looking at a $3.5 billion uplift on $7.5. So you're looking at a 50% increase on revenue being targeted from FIFA. Keep that in mind because we're going to come back to that.

Breaking that down, how did they achieve that? Right.

  • Broadcast rights — and again, these are reported numbers, so, you know, bear with us — but reported numbers, broadcast rights are now $3.8 to $4.3 billion. So a 22% increase on Qatar. Excellent. Yeah. One-fifth. Well done. Brilliant. Well done.
  • Sponsorship — key one, particularly in the US with the value of this market and the appeal of this World Cup. And it was a challenging Qatar, wasn't it? Because there were certain brand categories that for local reasons couldn't really stretch their legs. Absolutely. So the $2.7 billion commercial market income here, again, anticipated, is a 37% uplift on 2022. Again, excellent. Well done.
  • Tickets and hospitality. Now, I'll emphasise reported because there's so much in and around this, but $3 billion is the reported target for FIFA on tickets. And that's up from about $700 million — $750 million. So you're talking about a $200 million increase on ticket revenue. Is that fair, Charlie? Is that right? $2.3 billion increase. Sorry. Sorry. It's what happens when you get into your 50s. No, no, but it also shows the extremity of that number, right? We're talking about $700, I think it was about $700, $750 million on tickets in Qatar. Yeah. Going to a targeted $3 billion. Yeah. So a $2.25, $2.3 billion increase on tickets.

And who stumps the cost for the majority of tickets? Fans. So you leverage your exposure for sponsorships — great, B2B generally. Broadcast rights — B2B, sell it in, can we extract value out of that? Great. Tickets and hospitality — the pockets of the people that you need most.

Infantino 的"全部售罄"声明

And this ticketing theory, we've had quite a few people comment on the show asking us to look into this. So I'm going to give you a quick story of this. It's probably the most interesting part of the whole business story. So just bear with me, but I'm going to run you through a little bit of what's happened.

And I want to start with a quote from Gianni, our favourite Gianni Infantino, who in February said the following: "Every match is already sold out. We keep some tickets back for some last minute sales, of course, but every match is sold out." February 2026.

Now, it was interesting, some FIFA officials quickly walked back a little bit of that and said that they're holding some tickets back. I think it was about 5 million tickets and 6.5 they said were available. So let's say they held back 1.5 million. Anyway, that was a very strange thing to say when even the numbers of tickets that had been sold at the time didn't suggest that the tickets had been sold out.

一切的起点：仓促的时间线

And what's the real issue with all of this? Where's this come from? Well, it started with the information that they gave up front. So, normally, let's use Russia 2018, right? Russia 2018, FIFA gave two years' worth of time to allow people to understand how the ticket process would work — from buying tickets to planning their travel. And they gave that information with plenty of time for the fans to get on board with it.

In this World Cup, they started in September. They announced the first bits in September, and I think they went on sale on October the 1st. The World Cup starts in June. So eight to nine months of time to plan, book travel, accommodation — everything in here. It's quite stressful for a fan.

The ballot, therefore, that you went into at the start was also — it was kind of like a blindfolded — what they call a blindfolded ballot. So you'd have this demand generated and people would get on the site, but you'd only see the price of some of these tickets when you got to checkout. So you may have gone in there expecting it to be $60 and suddenly it was $300. So the point at which you're making that transaction, you could say you've been cornered into that to an extent.

高价的三个借口

Now the most expensive World Cup tickets ever — the justification for that was:

  1. North American market maturity — i.e. this is a region that's used to paying a lot of money for these types of events, so it'll be absolutely fine. Comment on that in a second with the value of the domestic audience versus the international.
  2. Reinvestment into the game — the old gem: "the more money we make from this tournament the more we can put back into the game".
  3. And then there was another interesting one, which is more corporate, which was their lack of ability to regulate the secondary markets.

So what do I mean by that? They feared if they put tickets too cheap, then a lot of people would buy them up and they would stick them straight on secondary markets and make a huge amount of money selling them at $400 rather than the $100 that they bought them off FIFA for. So they decided, "hey, well, we'll just change that. We'll just put them at $400 in the first place ourselves." We'll act like ticket touts ourselves. Exactly.

市场成熟度的误判

Firstly, on those justifications for very high ticket prices. You said something to me earlier on the market maturity. Yeah, let's go back onto market maturity. So basically what they're saying is, "Americans are really rich, so let's sting them." Yeah. Yeah.

I think that was just a strategic, massive strategic mistake. Why? America is a mature sports market that is very used to paying very large sums of money for sports which it has a deep passion for. So you can charge whatever you want for Super Bowl because tens of millions, in fact, I think we even worked out hundreds of millions of Americans desperately want to watch it live. And therefore there's a supply and demand imbalance. You can only fit — it doesn't matter how big the stadium is, you could never come close to fitting in all the people who wanted to watch Super Bowl. And, you know, that drips down into NFL in general and other leading sports etc. And again, NBA can be very, very popular, sometimes a bit less. So yes, there is an American sports market that has got its head around paying an awful lot of money to watch American sports franchises. Correct.

Right? Does that translate yet to having a mature soccer market? I use the word advisedly because we're talking about America, who are used to the idea of paying a lot of money to watch soccer matches. No. Absolutely not. MLS matches are cheap. Not many people watch them anyway. Going to watch a US men's national team is cheap. Going to watch women's matches in the US is cheap. Football is an immature product in a mature market, right? So that's the first point I want to make. It's a misjudgement on the American market.

世界杯的本质：吸引外国游客

The second point is that World Cups are not actually about the domestic market. What makes the whole ecosystem go round is that, effectively, the host country pays a lot for hosting the event, knowing it's going to recoup a lot of that money by attracting huge numbers of fans from all around the world to come to their country and spend lots of money in that country — not just on ticketing, but on hotels, on food and drink, on clothes, on all these other types of things. And that's how the whole thing works, at least supposedly works on an economic basis.

And some of those people who come once will love the country and decide to go back again. Funny enough, the last time I was in the Middle East, I bumped into some people in the airport who were transferring to Doha. And I said, "where are you going?" We had a bit of time. And they said, "well, we're going to Qatar." I said, "where are you going? On holiday?" "I said, all right, why is that?" He goes, "well, we came to the World Cup. And do you know what? We absolutely loved it." And they now take their holidays in Qatar because they love Qatar, right? They just thought the whole thing was terrific. So there's also that. In South Africa that was very much a part of the thought from South Africa's point of view — let's drive it as a tourism destination.

So effectively what they've done is they've priced out the international market who have to think not just about the ticket prices but also very high hotel prices, flight prices, all of those types of things as well. And then they've misjudged their own domestic demand — not their own American domestic demand thinking that that's a mature market for football, soccer, when it's actually not a mature market for soccer. It's a mature market for other US sports. And the amount of dollar that they're willing to spend on soccer is relatively limited.

So the net of all of that is that they've ended up with the wrong kind of supply-demand imbalance where they've got huge amounts of supply and limited demand on their own website.

动态定价与各级票价

So as of about three weeks ago, Charlie — you're about to tell me because you've got the detail — as of about three weeks ago, if you went onto FIFA's own website, you would find huge numbers of tickets available for lots of the group games, but still at very fancy prices.

Completely. And I'll give you some context on those prices now that we'll talk about before actually going into that because you need to actually hear a few numbers on this, right? We have three tiers that were really put. You had a value tier, a standard tier, and a premium tier — in those orders of price.

So firstly, this is the first World Cup that's employed dynamic pricing. Dynamic pricing being a change in price based on the demand for tickets to an event. Very simple. If there's higher demand, the price rises. If it's not and you need to shift the tickets, you'll get it at better value. But generally that happens much closer to the actual event.

They've also priced games differently depending on who's played in them. Some people that's all got very upset about this. You know, "I think there should just be a standard price for all tickets regardless of who's playing." Other people, again, understand that.

But for England's match against Croatia on the 17th of June, let's just use that. Tickets cost in the value tier £198, standard tier £373, premium tier £523. So let's just leave the premium tier to one side and just look at the other two tiers and say that basically, bar the shouting, it's £250 to go and watch one England game. Yep, £800 for a family. Which is not, by the way, not far off, you know, a season ticket in the Championship. Yep. Right? So it's one match and it's about the same as watching 25 matches of a very high quality division back home. Right? So that's the level we're talking at here. £250 to £300 quid for one match ticket.

And as you say, if you're going with two or three members of your family, you're talking about — we've spoken about this before — you're getting about a thousand quid on the tickets, then you're talking about another several thousand quid on the flights, this is for one match, then you're talking about another couple of thousand quid on the hotels, you're probably talking about — just to go and watch one match — you're probably talking about a total cost of £10,000 quid. Incredible, isn't it?

Scotland's first two group games, if you look at comparison, a little bit cheaper. Scotland against Haiti, for example, £134, £298 and £372. Still £134 pounds. And how many Haitians can afford to go and do that?

失去的"嘉年华效应"

Now, of course, people who are actually currently in Haiti won't be allowed into the US in any case, but there's a significant Haiti population within the US, and I'm saying that very few of them would be able to afford to go to that game. So then you're going to lose that carnival effect that you get.

Do you remember that wonderful game in the last World Cup when Saudi Arabia played against Argentina? Oh, it was amazing, wasn't it? Right? It was terrific. Saudi turned over Argentina in the game itself, but the atmosphere in there because the Argentines have got a big — they've got a big travelling support — because Saudi was local, so they had a lot of Saudi fans as well. It was really colourful, really loud.

So that makes World Cups. It does. It makes World Cups. Exactly. Right, Charlie? So if you look before the start of the World Cup and you say, "okay, what are the games I'm really looking forward to?" Saudi Arabia, Argentina would not have been one of them. And yet it was one of the most memorable games that World Cup, for the fiesta, the whole sight and the sound and the smell and everything of it. It's the first game I think of when I think of Qatar. Really? Obviously, I think of the final. Well, it was incredible. But as in just literally, I can picture that stadium exactly as you just described it. And what a triumph for the Qatar World Cup. What a triumph for the Qatar World Cup to be — for the memory to be fixed on such a wonderful occasion as a group game. As a group game that was somehow symbolic of what — actually, as you say, once the tournament started, for the memory to be fixed.

For me, that was one of my favourite World Cups from a football perspective. From a football perspective, I thought it was terrific. There were lots of great games. I thought it was really passionate crowds. They get the ticket prices low — and this time they've just totally misjudged it.

淘汰赛与决赛的价格

And I mean, you know, then the knockout games, just to give you, and I'll move it on, but it's £500 as the standard — sorry, as the value tier for quarters and £700 pounds nearly for the semi-final. And then you get to the final. Trade descriptions, that's on value on that one. £3,119 pounds for a standard basic supporter ticket for the final. So, yeah, all of this, right, straight, it's just commercial. We talked about that $3 billion target at the beginning. It's not hard to see now where they're putting some of that dream and numbers on.

实际并未售罄：可用库存

However, you referenced really importantly the fact that if you go onto the sites now, you'll actually find that despite Gianni's attempt to say that this is absolutely unprecedented demand and we're all sold out, well, the sites say something different. Because FIFA's own site and… well, a few others. Like SeatGeek, like StubHub. Big secondary ticket sales. Touting websites. Touting websites. Let's call them what they are.

Biggest games, of course, will be sold out. We're not talking about those. But just over a week ago, TicketData, which is an independent site which tracks the major sporting events in the US, that painted quite an intriguing picture, as what they reported a week ago on Saturday was that there are still 74,000 tickets available across 86 of the 104 matches. Wow.

票务"消失"之谜

That's only part of the story though. So, FIFA's own resale site has been an interesting look at value creation, because any ticket bought on that site has a 15% fee for the buyer and a 15% fee for the seller. This is the secondary side. So naturally, FIFA wanted to have all the people that were going on a secondary market going onto the FIFA website where they can make off that transaction.

Now, that really wasn't working that well. No. Because they still had a huge amount of inventory — unconfirmed, but a huge amount of inventory within hours, basically. TicketData reported that the number of tickets on FIFA's face value site for its secondary market suddenly went to about half of that, to 32,000. Then that fell to 22,000 just a few hours later.

So either you've had a sudden flurry of tickets on that secondary FIFA website market, or encouraging people to go and buy through the FIFA market has failed and they've gone and had to dump a load of tickets on other secondary websites. Yeah, touting websites. Touting websites.

Right, so effectively what's happened here — now FIFA have not come out and… no comments, denied. They basically have just absolutely straight-batted it, refused to comment on this. This is people speculating based on figures they've got in front of them and understanding where these tickets could possibly have gone. These are ticketing experts who are making the judgment that a large number of tickets have disappeared from FIFA's website and have ended up on a touting website. And they are making the judgment, these ticketing experts, that that is FIFA dumping tickets onto the market because they themselves, FIFA, could not be seen to be lowering the prices by that much — or else they would open themselves up to difficulties with those who they've already charged vast amounts of money to for these tickets.

Now, interestingly, the touting websites themselves have said, "we don't have a direct relationship with FIFA." So in my view, this is my speculation, Charlie: I think there's been an intermediary here who has gone to FIFA and has said, "you've got a lot of unsold inventory. How about if we block buy a lot of those off you at a B2B price, which you don't have to declare because it's a group discount, for tens of thousands or whatever it might be. And we will then try and dispose of those at a profit to ourselves in the way which we see fit. But we effectively have been the — we actually have bought them. We actually have bought them for you. And it's now down to us to try and sell them however."

But at least you can wash your hands of it, right? So even if it means you end up not making as much money as you might have hoped to from the ticketing side, A, you won't have quite such empty stadiums, and secondly, you will at least be getting some money for them. You'll be getting some money for them. And the event's worst nightmare is obviously a ticket going to zero. Exactly. These touting agencies are the experts at selling tickets. It's what they do. It's their trade. The thing about FIFA being they only have one tournament every four years. They are not ticketing experts. This is not their expertise, right? They are commercial experts, not ticketing experts.

真实可买到的价格

So interestingly, just for some numbers that you could now get tickets for if you go onto the sites today and have a look. I was going to ask you about that.

  • Jordan vs Algeria: Big fall. Two tickets available on face value at £471 can now be bought on FIFA's own site for £171. That's 64% cheaper. So that's two tickets. So you're sorting per ticket under £100. Yeah. Excellent. Coming back into where people may go. But have any of those made their way on the SeatGeek? On SeatGeek, those tickets were listed at £192 and £172 on StubHub. So you're getting a very similar price on those sites. But what you're seeing is two tickets now for a game that wouldn't have been at the top of people's must-go-to list.
  • Czech Republic vs South Africa: Those were down to about £190 for the same.

Now, I just had a look interestingly last year just to see what some of the tickets on those empty stadiums in the Club World Cup games — I mean, what they, you know — you could see Chelsea Palmeiras tickets went down to $8 for the Club World Cup quarter-final just to get people in that stadium. It's amazing what you have to say to get people to watch Chelsea. It's amazing, isn't it? And it would be — so it'd be really interesting to see how far those numbers now actually will drop down. It'll be fascinating. It'll be absolutely fascinating.

司法介入：纽约 / 新泽西总检察长传票

But Charlie, there's a big story emerging here. A big story. I thought that was already a pretty big story. Well, it is, but there's a bigger one, I think, which is the New York and New Jersey Attorneys General have, a couple of weeks ago, subpoenaed FIFA over the manipulation of World Cup ticketing, basically creating what they say is fake scarcity to drive up price.

We love a good statement. So, Jennifer Davenport, the New Jersey Attorney General, said the following: "FIFA has turned buying a ticket for this World Cup into a gauntlet of confusion, fake scarcity and impossibly high prices, all at the expense of consumers and hardworking New Jerseyans."

There is also on top of this been accusations of those that bought tickets getting tickets that were not represented in the purchasing process — i.e. they think they bought a good ticket with X view and it's turned out to be one that is far less good. Because since those tickets came out in the beginning, FIFA added two new sections of exclusive high-value tickets at the front of stands. And so some people have basically been pushed backwards or moved sideways in stadiums.

So the AGs had two elements: one, creating fake scarcity; one, selling and misrepresenting a product.

主办城市的代价

And how much are the host cities paying for the privilege, by the way? About $250 million per city to host a game. Per city, right? So for you to be one of the World Cup host cities, as a city, you're already paying $250 million. And then you find that FIFA are allegedly screwing your own local voters on even their access, let alone foreign fans coming in their access to it.

大局：本可以是 90 亿，却追求 110 亿

Now, I want to bring this back to what's really happened here. Let's look at the big picture, right? Let's accept the fact that it's a mess. I don't think that's controversial, is it? And say, why has it landed up here?

So they did $7.5 billion of revenue in Qatar. And because the commercial environment was always going to be much better in the US, and because things like hospitality sales will be better, they've also got more matches. So that, in theory, should do better. And you've just got a country which a lot of people want to travel to in any case etc. So it was always going — TV rights as well likely to do better. So for all sorts of reasons, that $7.5 billion was probably always going to rise to $9 billion. Is that fair? Definitely. Right. $7.5 billion. So $7.5 billion to $9 billion. And Gianni could have stood up and gone "record World Cup. We've, you know, increased revenues by 20 percent." Okay. And everyone goes, "whoa, well done, Gianni."

Now, his determination to drive it from the $9 billion, which probably it should have been, to $11 billion — that is the $2 billion that is attempting to be extracted from fans. And it's that $2 billion that is causing the problem here, right? Which is if they'd simply gone, "look, the commercial sponsors are going to pay big bucks. The TV companies pay big bucks. The one thing that we now need to do is to make sure it's a great event. We need to safeguard the brand of the World Cup because we need to make sure that other countries want to host it. We need to make sure that when there's another World Cup that lots of fans want to go and all this type of stuff."

What they've done is an absolute misguided medium-term strategic disaster, which they've basically brought shame on the World Cup to try and screw an extra couple of billion dollars out of it, really because that's how Infantino props up his own power base by being able to hand out money to all the little federations around the world. And the more he hands out to them, the more they want to keep Gianni Infantino in position.

与 IOC 同样的治理病

And we've said this before with our friend Kirsty at the IOC, in a way, it's almost a bit unfair to blame them — although I do blame Gianni in this case — because the way the system is set up where each one individual country, rather like the minor racecourses, each one has one vote, means that countries for whom an extra couple of million here or there really makes a big difference to them, their vote is going to swing depending on whichever presidential candidate promises them the biggest handouts.

And we can talk about this at another time. That has increased this year. Each team's getting $12.5 million guaranteed, even if they go out of the group stages. And that's gone up. Look at the payments of the ones who don't turn up. That's the critical one. Because don't forget, the ones who are most important in the voting process aren't the ones who qualify. They're the ones who don't qualify.

长期价值的丢失

But you hit the point really, which is, look at the ones that you don't mind going up, the categories you don't mind going up. Sponsorship. Yeah. Broadcast. Yeah, absolutely. Increase. But why those two groups? Why are they paying more? Because they're buying an amazing experience with a brilliant reputation that is a prestige. And Qatar was a big success. And the success of the last one. So they can go, "right, that was brilliant for us. That was exactly what we wanted to buy. We had those moments of absolute incredible fandom." You've raised 37.5% on sponsorship. You've raised 22.5% on broadcast. Really, now, the minute this ends, you're right into that next cycle, right? And you're going to be looking at how to increase that.

If there's been a shit experience for the fans, which is — which is going to dominate a lot of the press in — that your association that comes into this being really positive, and is worth all of that increase, that's going to become really challenging for you to justify. So you're not increasing it. You maybe even start looking to reduce it because you realise that if this is going to be the operational model of this organisation, it's not probably the one that's going to get us the best exposure.

And now when competition — we talked about, Charlie, last week, Formula One and top brands — there's now more competition than ever for these dollars. Absolutely. In this kind of very short term targeting. Yeah. They have completely missed, to me, the long term value, and they've turned what should be the biggest fan-driven event into a commercial outcome.

Except that it works in the short to medium term to get Gianni Infantino re-elected to his position at FIFA. And I think that's where we have to leave it. That is the point of this whole exercise. And that is the structural problem with FIFA is that the president gets re-elected based on the votes of individual small countries. And the more money you give them, the more likely they are to vote for you again. Correct.

收尾

That's so interesting. I mean, look, we've just said it's going to be a lot about the performance side. I think there's some interesting business pieces. Ticketing is one huge one. We'll cover a couple of others that we've identified in the coming weeks. So we'll save them for that. But I can't wait for it to start. And obviously, well, this recording Wednesday, it's going to come out on Friday. We'll be one game in, more games to come now. It's very exciting.

But thank you so much as always. And happy birthday from everyone to you, from all of us to you. Go and enjoy the rest of your week. Thank you very much, Charlie. I shall enjoy all the parties that I haven't invited you to, and I shall see you at Royal Ascot next week. Fantastic.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 三个产业，一种相同的"代议制腐败"
如果把这期播客的三个看似无关的故事拼在一起——英格兰板球队的酒吧斗殴、皇家爱斯科赛马场的"内战"、FIFA 世界杯的票务丑闻——你会发现一个让人意外的**结构同构**：它们都不是"道德败坏"的故事，而是**"小选民俘获大组织"的治理病**。
- **板球**：ECB 让 Stokes、McCullum、Key 这群"心智仍是少年"的人互相监督，等于让被监督者投票决定监督力度。 - **赛马**：58 家赛马场每家一票，于是 30 多家小赛场结盟，永久否决任何提升顶级赛事质量的改革——正是 1980 年代英国足球联赛"Grimsby Town 投票决定曼联命运"的翻版。 - **FIFA**：每个国家足协一票，所以 Infantino 必须不停从粉丝口袋里榨出现金，去喂养那些根本不会进世界杯的小国足协，以换取连任。
**意外的洞察**：这三件事表面上完全无关，但它们都遵循一个**反向激励的几何学**——组织里"最不该有话语权的人"恰恰拥有了决定性的投票权重，而真正承担后果的人（球迷、顶级赛马场、英格兰队主帅本应代表的国家荣誉）则完全无投票权

item.title: [Bg2 Pod] The SpaceX IPO, Fable 5, AI Capex Update & Market Check w/ Gavin Baker, Andrew Fox & Clark Tang
item.dc:creator: Bg2 Pod
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的隐喻：SpaceX 正在重演贝佐斯 2009 年的剧本，但加了一层时间机器

最深的反讽并不在转录中明说，而藏在那句"这就是当年贝佐斯建 AWS 的逻辑"的对比里。贝佐斯用黑色星期五的冗余算力造出云计算；Musk 用为自家 Grok 训练而囤积的 GPU 算力造出 AI 超级云——同样是"主业的副产品成为下一代基础设施"。但 SpaceX 的版本有一个贝佐斯当年没有的反身性维度：它一边卖算力给 Anthropic、Google，一边用同样的算力训练直接和它们竞争的前沿模型。亚马逊从来没有用 AWS 去做一个能干掉零售商的零售业务，但 Musk 正在用 SpaceX 卖给对手的同款基础设施，去打磨 Grok 4.3 和 Cursor 训练出的下一代模型。这是垂直整合的极端版本——你的客户在补贴你的下一轮颠覆。

二、跨领域连接：丰田的"时间即成本"哲学，被搬进了 AI 数据中心

GB 说出"speed is literally cost"那一刻，他实际上在重述丰田生产系统的核心信念：在制造业里，库存就是问题，等待就是浪费，节拍时间(takt time)即利润边界。Elon 把这套工厂逻辑搬到 GPU 部署上——122 天 vs 行业 4 年。这意味着在 AI 算力这个看似由"芯片稀缺性 + 资本密集度"主导的行业，胜负手悄悄迁移到了电工、管道工、变压器供应链的协同调度——一个 1908 年福特就已经回答过的问题。市场把 SpaceX 当作"火箭+模型公司"来估值，但它的护城河其实是一支会盖工厂的施工铁军。整个 Mag 7 里没有一家具备这种"工业肌肉"。

三、令读者意外的观察：Cursor 收购的真实标的不是产品，也不是团队，而是"反互联网"数据

转录里最容易被滑过去的一句话是："Cursor 拥有的私有编程 token，比公开互联网上存在的还多。"这句话翻译过来意味着——编程智能的训练前沿正在脱离公开网络。整个大模型时代第一阶段的逻辑是"互联网即语料"，Common Crawl 是普惠资源；但 Cursor 案例预示一个倒转：最有价值的下一代训练数据藏在闭源 IDE 的击键流里。这把 M&A 逻辑彻底改写——X.ai 花的钱不是买公司，是买"反互联网"的私有维度。类比一下：这相当于一个体育转播巨头不再买电视台，而是买所有运动员的肌肉电信号。如果这个模式被验证，未来最贵的 AI 收购标的会是 Figma、Notion、Linear——不是因为它们的产品，而是因为它们承载的工作流数据是公开网络永远抓不到的暗物质。

四、隐藏的成本结构反转：当 GPU 变便宜，太空反而更便宜

这是被会议讨论触及但没有点透的一个反直觉拐点：陆地数据中心 60 亿/GW = 35 亿 GPU + 25 亿"壳子"(电力、冷却、土地)。GB 自己说，那 25 亿大概率是通胀的；而太空版本是 30 亿/GW，且"壳子"成本基本为零——电、冷却、土地都是自由变量。这意味着一个反直觉的结论：随着 GPU 持续摩尔下行，太空算力的相对优势会越来越大，而不是越来越小。市场默认"GPU 便宜了就不需要太空了"，恰恰相反——GPU 占总成本的比例越低，剩下那 25 亿"地面税"就越扎眼。SpaceX 的轨道算力业务可能不是一个"如果 AI 继续疯长"的看涨期权，而是一个"如果 GPU 价格继续下跌"的看涨期权——两个看似对立的未来都对它有利。

五、IPO 微结构的隐秘不对称：这是历史上最缺乏"内部人卖压"的大型 IPO

通常 IPO 后回撤 50% 的根因是锁定期解禁、早期员工套现、VC 退出三重叠加。但 SpaceX 是人类历史上少数提前 10 年、每 6 个月给员工提供一次流动性的公司——也就是说，过去想卖的员工已经卖了 20 次。Musk 本人锁 365 天且不需要流动性。这种"流动性需求已经被前置消化"的结构，使得标准的"IPO 后必跌"统计样本可能根本不适用——你在用钓鳃鱼的方法预测一条蓝鳍金枪鱼的行为。这不一定意味着不跌，而是意味着那张吓人的 50% 回撤图其实在分析一个不可比样本。

📝 转录文本

Cold Open

"I think we're all pretty AI-pilled, and if you're AI-pilled, that means we got to build a lot more compute than the world thinks, and that these models are going to be a lot more valuable than people think. You combine that with their core business — I don't know another entrepreneur or another business that is a better bet on the future than SpaceX. So I think for most institutional investors, it's a must-buy, a must-own, set-it-and-forget-it, in order to have a real bet on both the space and the AI future."

Intro: BG2 Is Back

Alright, here we go. Early morning, Silicon Valley. BG2 is back. We're chopping it up on all things tech and markets.

To do that, I have none other than GB in the house — Gavin Baker from Atreides Management. He's brought his main guy Andrew Fox, and of course I had to draft Clark Tang into the mix, my partner, to talk about some of the big questions of the day:

  • How should we be thinking about the SpaceX IPO? What are the big levers? There are big numbers out there for what's going to happen over the next few years — let's break that down a bit, help simplify it for folks.
  • Mythos launched yesterday. I want to talk a little bit about who's up, who's down in the race for superintelligence. Where are we? What did we learn with the Mythos launch?
  • And Clark was in Taiwan last week with Jensen at Computex and GTC — so what was our takeaway? What's going on with GPUs, memory, where are the bottlenecks, and where do we go from here?

To start everything off, maybe just kick it over to you, Gavin, talking about the SpaceX IPO. The IPO is in two days. You're a big shareholder, congratulations. We're also a shareholder. We also expect to be buying in the IPO. The Wall Street Journal and Goldman Sachs are both saying $160 billion in revenue in 2028. We know the IPO is $135 per share, $1.77 trillion. So when we think about what the big levers are — there are so many moving parts in this IPO — nobody's better than you at just breaking it down and simplifying. What are the key levers we ought to be thinking about?

The Two Big Levers Gavin Is Watching

Gavin: Sure — great to be here. Thank you for having me. I thought we were going to call it BG-GBP, but we can stick with BG2.

So I think there are two big variables people should focus on.

Lever #1 — Per-Gigawatt Profitability of Compute Deals

You guys have this chart — Clark, who I've known for many years, did a great analysis. He shows that X.ai's deal with Google for cloud computing generates more operating profit per gigawatt than Anthropic, than Meta, than Google, than OpenAI. Their deal with Anthropic also generates probably more operating profit than anyone but Anthropic.

Your colleague at Altimeter, Freda, calculated a 55% IRR on Colossus 1. If you can borrow money at 6, 7, 8% and invest in something with a 55% IRR — that math maths.

So the most important variable is how quickly they bring on terrestrial data centers. We do know from Jensen that Elon brings data centers up faster than anyone — 122 days. Speed is literally cost — because every day you're paying electricians and plumbers, that's cost. And they're now monetizing them at arguably the highest rate. That is a truly massive variable.

Lever #2 — Position on the Pareto Frontier

The second thing — we have a chart, and it's wildly out of date now, it's kind of freaking amazing. This chart is, I think, from 10 days ago. But in the 10 or 12 days since we made it — which shows the Pareto curves for Opus 4.7 for coding, for Codex from OpenAI — it was already out of date. Now we have Opus 4.8. And now we have Fable 5 and Mythos, which is freaking wild. In 10 days, we would have had to update the chart twice.

What the Pareto curve shows is how much intelligence you can get for a given amount of cost. And I do think all frontier-model revenue will accrue to the Pareto curve.

What I think is so impressive: you can see in the chart that Composer 2 was Pareto dominant at the lowest level of intelligence with very little training. This reflects something — and I know you know Cursor better than I do — my understanding is that Cursor and Anthropic have more tokens of proprietary coding data than anyone else. They have more tokens of proprietary coding data than exist on the public internet.

So they fed Cursor — used Kimi K 2.5, used their own private data, did some RL, some supervised fine-tuning — and they got a really good model. Then they spent three weeks in the Colossus 2 cluster and got a model that, 12 days ago, was Pareto dominant with Composer 2.5. That's on their own benchmark Cursor Bench, so take it with a grain of salt. But it suggests that the Cursor data is very valuable for coding, and when it's trained Chinchilla-optimal or beyond Chinchilla-optimal with RL, X.ai has a shot at being a real player in coding.

Breaking the Business Into Three Lines

Host: One of the interesting things — the way you answered the question. We didn't talk about launch. We didn't talk about Starlink or communications. Those, up until six months ago, were the business. Then we merged in X.ai, and we merged in Cursor, and we announced these deals where it was clear he was building AWS right under our nose.

Give us the breakdown of the three big lines of business:

  1. Communications / Starlink / launch
  2. AI compute
  3. X.ai (and now Cursor)

If we just go to the core business — what has to go right with launch and Starlink to achieve the numbers that are out there?

Launch — The Crown Jewel

Fox: The thing that is foundational to everything is the launch business. This is the crown jewel of SpaceX — something no one else really has — notably reusability and soon rapid reusability. This is what you need to believe in to get to economics in AI that make orbital compute attractive.

Rapid reusability is the main thing we're watching for. Elon talks about it a lot — getting these rockets to fly at a cadence comparable to an airline. Gavin has used this analogy: the old rocket industry was like boarding a plane, flying to California, getting off, and the plane explodes after.

What SpaceX is trying to achieve is having a Starship — both stages — fly 30, 40, 50 times before you retrofit. Then you're amortizing the cost of the vehicle. The company has been loud and clear: they're going to attempt to bring back the second stage later this year, make it reusable next year, and ramp up cadence. Driving down the cost of launch is what enables all the other businesses.

Host: So how many times — Starship 3 just launched. What's the consensus on launch cadence two or three years from now? Are we launching one of these every day, every week, every month?

Fox: Expectations now go from 165 launches last year up into the high hundreds in the next several years — and into the thousands probably in the next three years. The company has aspirations of thousands of launches.

Host: Thousands of launches — you're doing two or three a day.

Starlink — Still Sub-1% Penetration

Host: What does this enable? Obviously I'm here in Silicon Valley and I can't even keep a call on Sand Hill Road. Two decades into the mobile revolution. It's like a third world. We're all going to switch to Starlink mobile when it comes along because I don't want to lose that call on Sand Hill Road.

Walk me through high-level — it's a big portion of revenue growth expected over the next two to three years. My hunch is this is driven by direct-to-cell connectivity.

Fox: It's actually interesting — the broadband business is still very early stage when you think about percent of households actually penetrated to date. The percent of global households with Starlink is less than 1%. That's broadband — where you have a base terminal at your house, or in cars, in airlines too. I actually think broadband can scale to hundreds of millions of terminals, hundreds of millions of users.

Gavin: Hundreds of millions if — if they get rapid reusability of Starship, which is really hard. If there's not competition. Hundreds of millions is possible.

The AI Compute Surprise

Host: Clark, I would say the biggest surprise of the last six weeks is that Elon struck these huge deals with Anthropic and Google. I don't even think people were thinking about SpaceX in the AI compute game. If you looked at the models a few months ago, it was Connectivity / Starlink and X.ai the model. But this whole category — taking all this compute he's uniquely good at standing up, and reselling it in a way that's highly profitable — is now a major component of the forecast.

Clark: What they achieved is singular, never been done before. To put in perspective — 100,000 GPUs, that's easily the fastest supercomputer on the planet as one cluster. A supercomputer you would normally build takes three years to plan, then they deliver the equipment, and it takes one year to get it all working. We're talking about 19 days. Elon is an N of 1 in his ability to secure supply, stand it up, deploy it in a way that's coherent and effective for both himself and others.

Host: Walk us through that — it looks to me like a major component of the revenue story.

Clark: Totally — we were at the Macro Hard data center, and the level of engineering was evident. People always talk about Google and their ability to build a TPU and sell it to generate revenue. It's a similar dynamic here with Elon able to secure power, build sites faster than anyone else. If you look at the relationships he's forged with suppliers, with sites that want X.ai as a tenant, his ability to finance these deals at attractive rates — these are advantages that compound.

Google is obviously paying SpaceX a huge premium for this compute.

Fox: SpaceX can stand up compute quickly, coherently, and in one place and have it available. So that's most of the premium. But certainly people are going to space over time.

Host: There's a fundamental belief that we lack the compute needed to push the frontier on intelligence. So we have to build a lot of compute. Now there's competition — hyperscalers are building, AI-dedicated clouds are building, and now literally in a matter of weeks, we have a giant emerging in this category: SpaceX. Can they consolidate this market? Because Elon has a unique ability to get the supply, cut deals on the other side — nobody can stand it up like he can. I think there might be a real consolidation in AI compute where he emerges as the largest, strongest player.

Gavin: I think they are number four or five now. In 30 days we went from not being an AI hyperscaler to being number four. We passed a lot of companies including Oracle. CoreWeave is a huge business that we're investors in. But there are other players — and I'd say there are probably 50 neo-clouds being funded in Silicon Valley right now.

In the same way that Elon was able to re-engineer a rocket from first principles and make it reusable, he engineered an electric car from first principles. He thought about it differently. And I think he looked at data center design from first principles and designed something fundamentally different. I asked the team to be a little less public about things that are obvious to you — what you're doing is more differentiated than you perhaps realize.

Vernova only has a certain number of gas combustion engines. Who are you going to sell them to — X.ai or one of these startup neo-clouds? Everyone starts making more money when the GPUs get energized and sold faster. Speed is money for the suppliers — power, land, turbines.

Orbital Compute Economics

Fox: With orbital, the key thing is two-stage reusability — beyond that, rapid two-stage reusability. Today with Starship, they've shown they can successfully reland the booster. The second stage — we'll see what happens later this year. They're attempting to bring it back, then make it reusable next year.

But the important thing is cost per kg. We're talking about going from $1,500 per kg on Falcon to $250 per kg, or something lower. The more you reuse the rocket, the more that price comes down — you're depreciating the cost. Eventually you asymptote to the cost of the fuel. At that point we're talking about something well south of $250 per kg.

Then you look at the specs of these AI satellites. Elon did a great pod laying out: here is how you could design one and how heavy it is. How many could you fit into a Starship launch? When you back into the numbers, you get about 5 megawatts of capacity per Starship launch. There's 100 metric tons in one of those Starships.

You can back into the math: how much will it cost per gigawatt to launch this compute into space? Before accounting for bad GPUs — about $5 billion per gigawatt.

For comparison, terrestrially — switchgear, generators, transformers, the shell, getting the power — that today is about $20–25 billion per gigawatt. Talking about a 5x reduction on half your bill for the data center.

Host: Which is a huge number. Just to say it: it costs $60 billion to put a gigawatt on the ground today — $35B is GPUs and silicon, $25B is land, shell, power, and cooling. I hypothesize those elements are probably going to be inflationary, so those $25B may not go down. Because space, power, cooling are effectively free in space — there's no land in space, but there is space in space.

Fox: There's a lot of space in space. You're talking about putting a gigawatt in space for $30 billion and having lower operating cost. One would have to consider reliability and maintenance. As long as these satellites aren't failing at an astronomical rate, the math maths — as GPUs melt and lasers fail. We know this happens in data centers, particularly during big training runs. As long as reliability isn't dramatically lower, the math is there once we have reusability for Starship V3.

The Sleeper: The Model Itself (X.ai + Cursor)

Host: When we look at this — we went through Starlink and said it stands to reason we're going to have direct-to-cell. Then terrestrial data centers — Elon's going to build a much bigger business there. Then you have this call option on space that would drop the price further.

The one thing we haven't talked about is their model. Six months ago X.ai was competing, doing pretty well. But they've done something dramatic over the past couple months: they bought Cursor.

Cursor is 700–800 people, already doing incredibly well from a revenue perspective. Projections were they could exit this year at $10 billion revenue — one of the leading coding agents. But they also had an incredible team with the potential to build a frontier-level model. They were compute constrained. All of a sudden, they get bought by X — X has massive compute they can now train on.

How much of revenue is going to be core X.ai business powered by the Cursor team?

Gavin: Right now Composer 2.5 was Pareto dominant 12 days ago — trained on the Kimi K 2.5 base model. Now Grok 4.3 — a 1.5 trillion parameter model — is training. One would hypothesize based on scaling laws that will be a better base model. Cursor data is being injected into the pre-training process, not just reinforcement learning.

That is going to be an important data point when it comes out. Just keep in mind: once you are at multiple places on that Pareto curve, if you have compute, you can scale really rapidly.
