Primary Risks and Political Honesty

Sax: Let me ask Senator Fetterman a question. So, I appreciate your common sense views on AI, on capitalism, on all the things we're talking about, and I think that the people in Pennsylvania appreciate it. I think you're very popular there. But I'm curious — do you ever get worried that you might get primaried in the Democratic party when you come up? Because the Bernie Sanders wing is powerful. They have a lot of activists. They're very noisy. I appreciate your maverick streak and your independence — but how do you think about that risk?

Fetterman: Well, a seat or a job is not — I'm always going to be honest, and my votes or my opinions are going to be what I happen to believe is true. It's not a seat worth trying to maintain if I'm going to lie or to pretend that things aren't absolutely true.

Yeah, absolutely, I can be primaried, and there are some Democrats that are angry at me. If they're angry at me for supporting Israel, hey, have at it. If they're angry at me that I think it's wrong to shut down the government, that's fine. If they're angry at me because I refuse to call him a Nazi or a fascist or a piece of shit — I'll never do that. You can hold me on that too.

But what's really strange is that the truth is, somebody that votes like a 93% Democratic line — and if I change my party, there was a lot of palace intrigue that I'm going to change the party — I'm never going to do that. And if I did, I could announce right now: I am changing it right now. My votes aren't going to change. My views, my views — I wouldn't vote any differently. The things I happen to believe. And if Democrats run me out of the party, they will be attacking a committed Democrat that votes their line 93% of the time, because we're in a unique time right now that I think things are more important than trying to —

I know what pays the bills as a Democrat now. Literally what campaigns are based on. Trump literally in their campaign commercials, in their emails — and outrage. It's constant rage bait. I refuse to engage in that. And I'm going to be an example of what I think is entirely appropriate. And a lot of the conversations are driven by people that represent very safe blue states. That's why Pennsylvania keeps people honest.

And people are going to remember in 2028: "Oh, I want to be bipartisan. I want to get along." People don't believe you. They remember what they've said and how people behave. So that's where I'm at. Yeah, I could be primaried. But what's more important is to be truthful about what I think are important values and remind people that the things we used to believe in — I think.

Data Centers, AI, and the Shale Parallel

Host: I want to just move to domestic policy and economics for a second. Dave, you said this incredible story in the green room, which maybe you can start off with — which is the quantum of people that reach out to you every week to mostly complain about various companies. But just use that as an insight maybe to talk about AI, because that's a huge part of your state. It's also a huge part of the American economy. And maybe compare it to what happened with shale and oil because there's a lot of commonalities. I think it's quite interesting.

McCormick: Yeah. I don't know if he gets the same amount, but on an average week, we have 14 million people in Pennsylvania almost. On an average week, I'll get something like 100,000 outreaches to me — letters, emails, phone calls across different issues. And you can almost see when the activists are mobilizing groups to come and make the case on certain things. And I do a call every two weeks. It's sort of an open mic where you get about 10,000 people to show up.

And the number of data center calls — data centers have become a much bigger and pervasive thing. And just to put this in perspective, David was there. Dina and I hosted an energy and innovation summit in Pennsylvania last July. We had $92 billion of investment committed. We had the energy CEOs, the AI CEOs, big investors, global investors. We had Senator Fetterman come, Governor Shapiro came, the president came, the cabinet came. It was amazing. And that's, by the way, what you need. Winning is a team sport, and winning in AI is a team sport, and you need all those players at the table.

And so it was a real success. And since then there's been enormous investments made in Pennsylvania. I visited one just the other week — Homer City. 4.4 gigawatts of power from a coal plant that's being transitioned to natural gas. 3.4 gigs is going to go into a data center complex. A gigawatt going back on the grid to hopefully bring down energy prices. So this is happening across Pennsylvania, but the opposition is growing.

Host: Is there any organic part to this? Because it's very hard to understand what's happening when you have these kind of dark forces, dark pools of money. Is there a legitimate "I don't want to have this in my backyard for X, Y"?

McCormick: There's enormous misinformation, and I think that misinformation is largely being driven by China and outside forces. And then there's just legitimate not understanding, not knowing. And we need to do a much better job of making sure that the outlines of what these data centers mean to communities is clear. And frankly, there needs to be a much clearer covenant of what it means. If you're going to have a data center in your community, you're a township supervisor — here's what you know. They're going to bring more energy, they're going to use, they're going to protect their water with closed loop. They're going to give this much to the tax base. They're going to build schools or infrastructure, whatever going to build.

Host: Isn't that first part the only thing that matters in people's minds? "I just don't want my energy bill to go up."

McCormick: No. Because as soon as you get through that, they move to the next thing, which is water. And then "the schools are going to be crowded" — and there's like — so the schools are going to be crowded because there'll be new jobs, no, because of new people, and they don't make the connection to the tax base. "The roads are going to be crowded." So there's a whole comprehensive case that needs to be made.

And what Chamath was making the point — we went through this before. John and I both lived through this with fracking. Huge campaign against, huge misinformation, lack of understanding. And 15 years later, I'd say there's pretty broad-based support.

Fetterman: No, yeah. There's unlimited money. They find some B-roll kind of a comment and they put $10 million behind it and they can turn you into the most extreme kinds of things. Ironically, originally people tried to convince people that I'm a socialist, and now people are like, "Now I'm MAGA" or whatever. I'm like, no, I'm just — I haven't changed. My views are my views.

And I would tell people: energy is national security. Look at what's happening in Europe and other parts. If you don't have energy security, then your national security is in danger. And AI is coming. And what he's done — he created this amazing symposium to remind people that we need both. We need energy, and Pennsylvania produces a lot of that. And also AI is coming — otherwise we'd still be using this spinny Jenny or whatever that's called.

Why do we talk about data centers? Because people, forces are describing them as a scourge or a cancer that's going to bankrupt you or destroy your community. Why do we have that right now? Because this is something that's going to transform our society. Absolutely there could be a downside, but overall it's going to be much more productive, and that's going to create new opportunities.

I refuse to become a party of Luddites. Honestly, if I had to vote and claim that that's all cancer, it's danger, it's all downside, then I'd be lying. And that's why I'm saying: Republican or Democrat, that's a fact. These things are very important.

Shale → AI: The Misinformation Pattern Shifts

Chamath: Can I just double click on this thing when you compare it to shale? So in shale, I'm going to assume that when shale was escalating in importance, the pushback were from corporate interests that would have economically lost out. That's what I'm guessing — the majority.

McCormick: Environmentalist.

Chamath: Environmentalist.

McCormick: Yeah, environmental.

Chamath: But it seems like in AI we're dealing with foreign state actors. Is that fair? Is it as reductively simple as that?

McCormick: Well, if you go to those two parts: one was the campaign of misinformation and the other is a lack of understanding. There's much more of a campaign of misinformation from outside forces this time around. But what happened in the case of shale: methane destroys the water table, "don't want these in our backyards." What happened, of course, was these enormous royalties for the farmers. We're the fourth largest natural gas reserves in the world. If we were a country, Pennsylvania would be number four. This is the economic engine for our state. Our state is the second largest energy producer in the country. So this unlocks everything for Pennsylvania.

And as those jobs started to be created, you saw the economic benefit. The misinformation about the methane and the water table became clear slowly, and much too long — 15 years later — we're now at a place where I bet 80% of the people would have support fracking.

Fetterman: Last night's event — you described that we have six to eight months ahead of China. Right? Is that right? Yeah, okay. So now you know, my party is driving for a moratorium for data centers. Do you think China doesn't love that? That's why — they're part of behind some of driving this view. You want to give that race to China? It's coming. It's the choice: do you want America to build that chassis, or do you want the Chinese to do that?

Sax: And at the core of that, John, is what you said earlier, which is — it starts with TDS, and then it goes with give China AI.

Fetterman: I would say this: vote me out. If you think it's okay to give China a benefit to win the AI race, vote me out. You know.

Sax: Your favorite person in the audience. My wife is clapping for you.

Fetterman: Oh, yeah. Yeah, that's exactly your favorite.

Sax: I mean, that's exactly the way I see it as well. So we have great bipartisan agreement here.

The Blue-Collar Boom

Sax: So Dave, I was at your summit last year, the Energy and Innovation Summit, where you announced $90 billion of new investments. The thing I was struck by was how broad-based the representation was at that summit. You only had — you had software companies, you had hardware companies, you had manufacturing, you had robotics, you had energy, nuclear, fracking. And then most interestingly, you had the trades there. And that's when a light bulb went on for me that, oh, this is a blue-collar boom because this is a construction boom, and blue-collar workers are really going to benefit. And the trades who were represented there spoke to that.

And since then we've had hundreds of thousands of new jobs, construction jobs created, and 25 to 30% wage increases for construction workers. So I'm curious: does that help make the cause more popular in Pennsylvania? Are those trades, are the unions still on board with this, or have the politics of that changed at all over the past year?

McCormick: It's a great question and it's been extraordinary. So just in the last 15-16 months — and these are related, by the way — it's not just energy projects and data centers. It's US Steel and a new next generation mill. It's Eli Lilly building a new plant. It's Hanwha and the Philadelphia shipyard. But they're coming in part because of the data centers and the energy projects.

So I've spent a lot of time with the trades lately. The most secure job in Pennsylvania right now is a seasoned welder. An electrician. These folks are coming out, they're getting the training. They're in their late teens, early 20s. They're making more than 100 grand a year in these sites and they can't hire quick enough. They can't find enough people to meet the demand.

And the data centers — I've looked closely at that. There's one coming in Berwick, Pennsylvania, right next to where I went to high school. The way to think about it is there's sort of four buckets of economic activity that comes, jobs that come. There's of course the deals that come with them. But there's the thousands of jobs to build the data centers and the energy that goes side by side. So these are 5,000-10,000 folks on the site. Then there's the hundreds of people to run the data centers. Then of course every three to four years the hardware and everything in the data centers gets upgraded, which brings in hundreds, sometimes thousands of jobs. There's the energy that's going side by side. Then there's the restaurants, the hotels. I met with the trucking association the other day — they guess that it's about two logistics jobs for every one job in a data center.

These things are economic engines. So once you get that covenant straight, people are willing to make the decision. And if a township supervisor, a county commissioner — no senator is going to tell them to do this. This has to come from the ground up in terms of making the decision. But once they decide to do that, it's a huge engine for their communities. If they decide not to do it, my point is, okay, where's your plan? What is the plan? In Pennsylvania, we've been losing people for 20 years. We can't get our kids to stay. This is like a rebirth. It's amazing what's happening, but we got to break through the misinformation.

Why Democrats Resist the Facts

Host (Freeberg): Well, I mean, I think on this point — why do you think it's so difficult for the Democrats to embrace the facts on the ground? Because you have an empirical story to share about job creation, value creation, and there's no empiricism on the other side that there's job displacement or job loss in the white-collar jobs which is generally part of —

McCormick: Two things I would say. The notion of AI broadly is getting munched with the notion of data centers in a narrow thing. Data centers of course are an enormous component of this, but those two things are unrelated. Second, I don't think it's just Democrats. We have —

Host: No, the America First movement is now in the horseshoe exactly aligned with the —

McCormick: We have the far left and the far right —

Host: Converging on an anti —

Sax: We even have some besties who occasionally buy into the jobs apocalypse. So —

Freeberg: No, no, I'm — no, never misrepresent me, Sax. I will call you out every time. I just believe there'll be jobs.

Sax: You could also use the word job mobility.

Freeberg: Well, I think I'll let — finish my sentence please. I'm speaking. Job displacement is occurring and new jobs need to be created to fill that in. And I think that's the blind spot of the people who have Trump dedication syndrome — is that they will never actually do anything that would modestly criticize the president. And that's part of the challenge.

Money in Politics

Host: I want to have a last just about the amount of money being donated by special interests. Do we need to get money out of politics? Because that seems to be the root of a lot of these problems — that billionaires can give 50 million, 50 million, 100 million, 200 million, whether it's Soros or friends of ours. It feels like there's way too much money in politics. Does that have to change for this type of issue around data centers, etc., and special interest? And can we ever get there?

McCormick: Yeah. Well, I would say of the five things I would do to fix America right now, that wouldn't be in the top five. But yes, conceptually, I'd like to get money out of politics. My race was $500 million.

Fetterman: We raised 200 million on my side, 300 million on the opposition side. We couldn't keep up.

McCormick: I was a record before in '22 — we raised, you know, my race was 300, 330 million, and now that's quaint. Wait till '26, wait till '28. This is going to look like quaint.

Fetterman: Or get rid of the primary process too. And open it to other people. These primaries — they identify the extreme kinds of views and put out someone, and that's part of it now too. Collectively, $300 million was spent to destroy our reputations. Think what $300 million could do for Pennsylvania or for people, that was spent to destroy reputations. "You're terrible." "I am terrible." That's the absurdity of the American politics, right?

Closing

Host: On that note, I actually want to say both of you are incredibly rational and reasonable and incredible Americans. So I just want to thank you, Senators Fetterman and McCormick. Very much appreciate.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：意识形态光谱的「马蹄铁」正在 AI 议题上完成闭合
整段对话中最值得停顿的一刻，是 Sax 那句几乎被忽略的判断："the America first movement is now in the horseshoe exactly aligned with the far left." 这句话在节目里只是一闪而过，但它指向一个正在发生但尚未被命名的结构性转折——**反 AI/反数据中心**成为了美国左翼极端派与右翼民粹派**第一次在产业政策上自发对齐**的议题。
Fetterman 的潜台词更尖锐：他暗示反数据中心运动背后是 CCP 关联资金。如果属实，这意味着外部国家行为者第一次发现可以**同时**给左右两端的极端派提供叙事弹药——左边讲"环境正义/算法剥削"，右边讲"小镇消逝/科技寡头"——而两条叙事最终会汇聚到同一个政策结果：减缓美国 AI 算力扩张。这是一种**双通道意识形态作战**，过去在页岩气、转基因、疫苗议题上都没成熟过的形态。
## 二、跨领域连接：页岩气的 15 年舆论周期，正在被 AI 压缩成 5 年版本
McCormick 给出

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] The One Thing AI Can't Commoditize – Alex Imas and Phil Trammell
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

稀缺性的幻觉：人类不是因为"少"才值钱

最反直觉的观察是：人类劳动的溢价不来自稀缺，而来自"关系性"本身。经济学的默认假设是——任何东西只要供给增加，价格就会下降。但这个艺术品实验揭示了一个奇怪的不对称：人类制作的版画从1件增加到500件时价格暴跌，而AI作品则从一开始就是商品，无论1件还是500件都没区别。

这意味着人类创作的"价值"其实分两层：一层是稀缺溢价（可被供给击穿），另一层是连接溢价（只在"一对一"的幻觉下存在）。一旦人类自己开始"批量生产"，他就把自己降格为AI——不是被技术取代，而是自我商品化。

跨领域连接：经济学的"horse vs human"，与佛教的"能所"

Imas 用马做对比时其实碰到了一个古老的哲学区分：马是"工具性存在"（instrumental），人是"构成性存在"（constitutive）。这和佛教里"能所不二"的思路惊人地相似——观察者本身改变了被观察物的本质。

把这个迁移到其他领域看： - 奢侈品：爱马仕手工包贵的不是皮，是"某个法国老匠人摸过它"——这是同一种关系性溢价。 - 心理治疗 vs 冥想App：CBT的疗效在元分析里和某些App接近，但人们仍愿意为治疗师付10倍价格，因为"被一个人看见"本身就是疗效。 - 手写信 vs 印刷品：婚礼请柬这个例子，本质上是花钱购买"未被复制的注意力"。

这暗示了一个更大的图景：未来的经济会沿"是否可证明是一对一"这条轴线重新分层，而不是沿"是否被AI替代"分层。

出人意料的推论：AI会让"低效"变成奢侈品

Trammell 担心的问题——"如果关系性偏好不够强、覆盖的行业不够多怎么办？"——其实指向一个更诡异的可能性：

AI越是高效，"刻意的低效"就越是身份信号。

未来可能出现这样的市场分裂： - 医疗诊断由AI完成（更准），但"听你说话的医生"成为顶级私立服务。 - 法律检索归AI（更全），但"为你担忧的律师"按小时收费翻10倍。 - 教育内容AI生成（更个性化），但"记得你名字的老师"成为精英学校的核心卖点。

这里的洞察是：AI不会摧毁人类劳动市场，但它会把人类劳动重新定义为"表演性低效"——人之所以有价值，恰恰因为他"本可以用AI却没用"。这是一种新的凡勃伦商品（Veblen good）：稀缺的不是技能，而是有意为之的非自动化。

这就引出一个让人不安的问题：当大多数职业都变成"关系性表演"时，那些不擅长情感表达的人——工程师型人格、自闭谱系、内向者——会不会成为新经济中的结构性输家？过去的工业革命淘汰的是体力弱者，AI革命可能淘汰的是情感劳动弱者。这是一个从未被认真讨论过的不平等维度。

📝 转录文本

关系性行业中的内在价值

What got me kind of also thinking about this — the idea of the relational sectors work that I was doing — on the fact that there does seem to be this value, this intrinsic value, that is — it's not just because it's scarce, it's because there's some intrinsic preference that people have for like empathy and connection, and you know, getting interacting with another person.

艺术品实验：人类 vs AI

So like one of the experiments that we ran was like — there's an art print, right? And we actually have an incentive compatible way of like basically saying like, how much are you willing to pay for this art print? People actually paying real money for. And then we say like, look, there's only one of those art prints, and it's either made — and these are between-subject conditions — by AI or by a person.

So with one, you get the effect that the person-produced art print is valued much, much higher than the AI version.

供给变化下的价格分化

And then what we do is to say — there's, in a set of other conditions, there's 500 of these being produced. So for the human-made one, the price goes down a lot, because it's no longer seen as like you're making a connection with this one artist. Versus with AI, there's no difference. AI is already viewed as like a commodity.

Right.

人不是马：输出价值的构成性差异

And you know, we need to do a lot more research on this, but it seems like that's kind of like the key difference between, you know, something like let's say a horse, right? There's — a horse was an input into an output where you can replace the horse with something else. You only care about the output.

The only way this relational story works — and this is what we need more data on — is if it's not a human is not a horse, in the sense that it is providing value from the output, where if you replace the human, the value of the output decreases.

故事成立的边界条件

And if that's not strong enough, and if it doesn't hold for enough sectors, if it doesn't hold for enough jobs, then this kind of story doesn't work anymore.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Biohub: The Future of Biology is Open-Source with Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Rives
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：这是一个"明确宣告自己不会治病"的治病项目

整段对话里最具颠覆性的一句话被 Mark 重复了两次："The theory isn't that we're going to cure the diseases. We're not." 这不是谦虚，而是一种深刻的战略反转——一个承诺"百年内治愈、预防和管理所有疾病"的组织，却拒绝亲自治任何一种病。

这其实是把硅谷最古老的一条经验偷渡进了生物学：做平台的人永远比做应用的人活得久、影响大。Linux 不治病、TCP/IP 不送信、CUDA 不训练模型，但它们决定了上面所有应用的天花板。BioHub 正在把自己定位成"生物学的 CUDA"。这也解释了为什么他们坚持开源——不是道德姿态，而是结构性必然：平台只有在被广泛采纳后才成为平台，闭源的"平台"只是一家公司。

二、被忽视的跨域共振：扎克伯格的"社交媒体哲学"正在被原封不动地搬进细胞建模

注意 Mark 在末段说的那句话："It's why you create something like social media, right? To give people a voice." 然后他几乎不换气地接到 Llama 开源、再接到 BioHub 开源。

这不是话术过渡——这是同一个世界观的三次部署： - Facebook：把发布权从媒体公司分发到每个人 - Llama：把模型权重从少数实验室分发到每个开发者 - BioHub：把"理解蛋白质 / 细胞 / 免疫系统"的能力从顶级药企分发到每个研究生

这三件事在表面看毫不相关，但底层是同一个对"中心化超级智能"的对赌。你可以不同意它，但应该看清它的一致性：扎克伯格在过去二十年里在不同领域反复下注的，是"边缘节点的累积创造力 > 中心节点的集中规划力"这一假设。BioHub 是这条赌线的第三段。

三、最容易被错过的伏笔："集邮"如何在十年后变成黄金矿脉

Priscilla 讲了一个看似温柔的小故事：早年他们资助 single-cell sequencing 时，科学界批评他们"just stamp collecting"——只是在搜集数据碎片，提炼不出智慧。他们当时没有反驳的答案。 然后 LLM 出现了。

这是整段对话里最重要的一个隐喻，而它适用范围远远超出生物学：

"集邮"在前 LLM 时代是浪费；在 LLM 时代是基础设施。

人类基因组计划、CellxGene、各种组学数据库，这些当年被嘲笑"只堆数据不出洞见"的工作，现在恰好成为模型唯一能学的语料。这暗示一条更普遍的规律：每当一种新的"理解机器"诞生时，过去被认为没意义的存量数据会突然估值重估。这对今天还在被嘲笑的领域（环境传感器数据、长期纵向健康数据、罕见病登记表）是一个非常实在的预言。

四、被深埋的方法论彩蛋：ESMFold 重演了 GPT 的"涌现剧本"

Alex 说了一句几乎被一笔带过、但应该被放大的话：

"We didn't design a model for antibodies. We didn't design a model to bind one particular target. We just designed a model that could understand proteins."

这是 GPT 涌现叙事的逐字翻拍——"我们没设计翻译模型，我们只是训练它预测下一个 token，翻译能力自己冒出来了"。蛋白质设计、抗体亲和力、纳摩尔级结合，全部作为副产品出现。

这意味着一件事很多人还没意识到：生物学正在被卷入和语言一模一样的 scaling 范式。如果蛋白质语言模型真的复现了 LLM 的涌现曲线，那么"我们什么时候能搞定免疫系统"这种问题，本质上变成了"我们什么时候能凑够 token 和算力"——这是一个工程问题，不再是生物学奥秘。这才是 Mark 说"百年时间表现在太保守"背后的真正逻辑。

五、隐藏的组织设计洞见：为什么"非营利"在这里是速度优势而非道德优势

通常人们把非营利和"慢、温和、低效"联系在一起。但仔细听 Mark 的逻辑：不必赚钱 → 不必聚焦 → 可以做层级化的全栈基础设施 → 数据可以横跨蛋白质/细胞/系统三层共同建模。

如果他们是创业公司，VC 会逼他们选一个适应症、一条流水线，因为单一管线才有 IRR。结果是公司加速，但科学减速——因为生物学的真正瓶颈在层与层之间的连接组织，而那部分没法被一条管线挑出来。

这是一个对"组织形态决定科学速度"的有力示范：非营利在这里不是慢，是逃离了"专注度"的诅咒。这一点对任何思考"基础研究 vs 应用研究"分工的人都值得反复琢磨。

📝 转录文本

0 · Cold Open

"We just want to give tools to the whole scientific community." "We want to understand how biology works. I want to understand the genetics of this person. I want to understand the risks they have to different illnesses. My goal is to be able to treat the individual as an individual, understand the mechanisms and be able to intervene." "We'll have a bigger impact by getting this in more scientist hands quicker by doing it as open source projects instead." "It's not just like there's some factory somewhere that you can pay to produce the data. You actually need to invent new novel scientific approaches." "The theory isn't that we're going to cure the diseases. We're not. It's that we want to help accelerate the pace of progress for the whole scientific field." "We folded over 1.1 billion proteins and predicted their structures. And we didn't design a model for antibodies. We didn't design a model to be able to bind one particular target. We just designed a model that could understand proteins." "If we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone."

Today on No Priors, we're joined by Mark Zuckerberg, Priscilla Chan, and Alex Rives. We'll be talking about Biohub and all their various efforts to now start applying AI at scale to do world models of cells and different levels of interactions across biology.

1 · Why BioHub Became The Mission

Host: Mark, Priscilla, thank you for doing this. Mark / Priscilla: Yeah, thanks for having us. This was fun. Host: Alex, congratulations on new missions. Alex: Thank you.

Host: You guys made BioHub your primary philanthropic effort and then committed $500 million to this virtual biology initiative. Can you tell us a little bit about, you know, why do that and how did you go from "we should fund this" to "this is like who we are"?

Priscilla: So, Biohub in its current form, we're super excited about. We feel like it's a really good fit for who we are and what we bring to the table and what we can achieve together. But this work started 10 years ago when we were thinking about how can we give back, and Mark wanted to build an organization that could cure, prevent and manage all disease by the end of the century. And we had a series of hilarious meetings with scientists — famous Nobel Prize winning scientists were just laughing at us.

Host: Is that — was that your starting line? "We're just going to cure all disease."

Priscilla: No, no. And to be clear, we don't think that we're going to be the ones curing the diseases. Our goal was always to build tools that could accelerate the whole scientific field. That way, the scientific field collectively could cure all the diseases. But still, I thought that "by the end of the century" was a stretch. Now, I think it's too conservative.

And so we kept being like, "Okay, well…" We had these series of funny, awkward, educational conversations where we're like, "Okay, but why? Like why do you think it's impossible?" And the person in the room is just like, "Well, I don't know why. You tell me." Finally we got people to — they're like, "Fine, if you really must know." And we're like, "No, we do. It seems important."

They were like, "Well, we work in silos. When you publish, information doesn't get shared, it gets locked up for long periods of time, and we don't have tooling." They gave the example of: "We build a great tool by one postdoc in a lab and it lives on their computer, and when they graduate the tool is gone." It was very hard to build shared tools to move science faster, build the shared knowledge base. And that's sort of where we began in thinking about: okay, if those are the problems, what can we contribute?

2 · From CZI to a Network of Biohubs

Mark: The original biohub model was basically focused on long-term tool development by bringing together engineers and scientists across multiple universities. And it basically worked. We started off with CZI doing a number of different things, and over time we just felt like, okay, the science piece is really working — and we just kept on investing more and more and more in it until now it is basically the primary and main thing that we're doing.

We've expanded the original San Francisco Biohub to a handful now — there's New York, there's Chicago. The real focus and the unifying theme at this point is the virtual biology initiative: taking the unique datasets that are able to be generated in order to model — effectively starting with the smallest pieces of proteins, but then eventually cells and whole biological systems.

That's kind of how we've evolved. This idea that some of this is an AI problem and you want to build a frontier AI lab, but you need to couple that with a frontier biology effort that can do the work of being able to understand and get the data that you need to actually be able to build these models. Because unlike language models — well, there's just a lot of data out there on the internet — that's not really the case with biology.

There are obviously a bunch of different datasets that exist that academia and scientists have generated over the decades, but a lot of the stuff that I think we want to put into this, it doesn't exist. You want to be able to visualize things that people haven't been able to see before, which is why we're doing the imaging work. You want to be able to record things that are going on inside the body, which is why we're doing the cellular engineering work. You want to be able to measure things like inflammation in ways that haven't been possible, which is why the Chicago Biohub is focused on building those kinds of devices.

That will fundamentally create new types of datasets that will allow new types of models. The scientific field primarily needs tool development that is going to empower scientists across the field to be able to do their work faster — that's what we think we can provide through this kind of long-term focus on tool development.

3 · The Through-Line: From Single-Cell to LLMs

Priscilla: There's a fun through-line from where we started and the work that Alex is driving now. Our very first request for application (RFA) here was around single-cell sequencing. We wanted to look at the RNA that is transcribed in individual cells. That was possible, but it was still pretty early on in understanding how different cells were expressing their DNA — to the point where at the beginning we were just funding methods: getting people to describe how to do it so that others could share that methodology.

Then that became us funding the Human Cell Atlas, which is now one of the largest databases of single-cell transcriptomics. It was getting hard for scientists to annotate the data, so we built CellxGene, which was a very simple annotation tool that scientists could use to make use of that data. Then a community came around CellxGene and started contributing more and more data that we had nothing to do with creating or funding. Now CellxGene is a corpus of knowledge that a lot of the transcriptomic-based models are based off of and is used regularly by the scientific community.

But still there were always critiques: "This is just stamp collecting. You're just gathering bits of data, and we're not going to be able to pull scientific knowledge and wisdom and insights out of it." And we didn't have an answer for a while.

Then imagine our delight when large language models became a huge topic of conversation — that could make sense of large amounts of data. For me it was: what if we could actually understand how biology worked? Move it from a discovery-based science to an engineering-based science where we could systematically understand how living beings, living cells worked, and be able to understand why things go wrong. When we saw that moment, we're like: this is it. Something really big could happen here.

4 · Why Alex Joined — Frontier AI ⨯ Frontier Biology

Host: Alex, you started at Meta FAIR, but you were on the path — you had assembled a team at Evolutionary Scale, you raised venture, you were making progress in your models. What was the pitch from Mark and Priscilla that said "that's actually the right way to go after the mission"?

Alex: For me, it was really the moment when I understood that they really saw this as an integration of frontier AI and frontier biology. I had developed conviction that this is a new era of science that's just beginning — what's going to be possible with artificial intelligence. We're in the age of information theory at scale, and we have these systems that can basically predict the next token and learn world models from that. They can learn biology from the data.

To build the next institution for the next era, you would really need to have frontier artificial intelligence. You would have to have frontier biology. You would need to start to put those things in feedback and really have models that are learning from the biology. And you need the right scale and the right people. So this just really felt like the way to do that.

5 · Modeling Biology Hierarchically — Proteins → Cells → Systems

Host: There's a variety of different models that you all have been working on. Some of the earliest breakthroughs in biology were things like AlphaFold, where a Google model showed that you could do protein folding at scale in a really interesting way — pre the really big transformer waves that came later. Now you're working on things at different scales: molecular modeling and protein folding, cell-based stuff, larger-scale biological systems.

How well do you think that extends from the micro to the macro? Modeling cellular behavior is very different from modeling protein folding. The data is different. The modeling is different. Is it all similar — just data, you train stuff — or are there real differences in how you have to deal with these systems?

Mark: I mean, there are probably some differences. Each layer is going to end up being somewhat qualitatively different. But you need to be able to understand the protein interactions in order to be able to understand how cells work. So you can't just go straight to cells without understanding the protein modeling. And if you're trying to understand something like the way the immune system works — a bunch of cells interact together — it's tough to do that without first understanding cells.

You might be able to, at a very high level of abstraction, simulate a system, but if you really want to understand how it's going to work, you want to build the simulations at each level hierarchically. So that's basically the approach we're going through, starting with the building blocks and the protein. There's going to be different types of data that you want to collect for each, and different modeling techniques. But a big part of the strategy is this view that you need to build it up hierarchically.

Priscilla: One of the things that's unique about us in the space is we were very intentional that the AI efforts and the wet lab efforts were a single effort, and we've done a lot of work to bring them together. The really neat thing that we can do is really try to pull and gather data that helps us connect across the hierarchy.

You can look at transcriptomics with space within a cell and look at where it's localizing. We can look at translucent zebrafish and look at the development across different cells, and when the brain develops. We have sensors that allow us to look at cell-cell communication and different molecules. So we can be strategic about the types of experiments and data we want to collect that helps us bridge across these — that there's some connective tissue that helps drive the modeling magic that happens.

Host: The reason I asked the question, by the way — I used to be a biologist. I have a PhD in biology and I worked in wet labs for almost a decade and everything else.

Mark: Are you looking for a job?

Host: Uh, we can talk about that later. At this point in my career — (laughs) I love my aggressive recording. I'm like Danny Glover in Lethal Weapon, I'm almost at retirement. But one of the things that was always lacking was this integrative nature across the different layers of biology. The developmental biologists would work on their own, the molecular biologists would be doing different experiments. So that's what I was curious about. Typically there's a reductionist view of biology and there's a systems view, and those people didn't really work together deeply. So one of the exciting things about what you're doing actually is how you're bridging that.

Alex: We're in the age of this kind of information theory in biology. There are levels of complexity and hierarchy in biology, and each level is constituted by the lower levels. As you want to have that more complete description, and you want to have systems that can really generalize and begin to actually answer experimental questions digitally that you could ask in the lab, you need to have the right basis for modeling at every level.

What's really unique about what we can do is to really build information at each of these different layers, collect them, collect those connection points, but then also do it at the scale that will reveal that underlying information architecture. That's going to be really critical to actually be able to build digital representations that can answer new experimental questions.

6 · Mechanistic Interpretability for Proteins

Host: One of the things that inspires me most about this effort is really what Priscilla said: there's so much we actually don't understand about biology, and what if we could? I think that is actually very different from lots of other incredibly interesting and useful AI problems we attack — we're trying to replicate human behavior, and a lot of that data is on the internet or captured.

One of the most interesting things in your release was the mechanistic interpretability stuff you alluded to — can we actually extract new knowledge from what the model believes is happening? Can you talk a little bit about that?

Alex: Yeah, I'm really excited about that. In mechanistic interpretability, traditionally it's been applied to large language models with the goal of understanding the representation space of a large language model — how does it compute things, and does that really connect to our intuitive understanding of the world? There's a really rich toolkit that has been developed to start to ask those questions.

So what does that mean for biology? One of the classes of models that we train are these protein language models. They're trained on the codes of proteins, and so anything they learn about biology is emergent. We've seen that they can learn things like biological structure and biological function — that's just emergent from the token prediction training task.

As we think about mechanistic interpretability in those models, we're really seeing the unknown, because the models have been trained on billions of protein sequences. They've been trained on both known and unknown biology. And yet they're developing these representations that start to capture things that we can see correspond to that reductive picture of biology that's been built up over the centuries. So you can start to connect the dots between proteins where we really don't know anything about them with proteins where we do know something — because there's that underlying structure grammar that's linking them in the representation space of the model.

Host: At the extreme, it could be — we're going to understand systems in the body that we didn't before, or the mechanism of action for a new treatment, because we can ask the model — interrogate that representation.

Alex: That's right. The hope is that you kind of really learn the underlying basis for how it's making the predictions, and so you open up the black box and you can actually understand the biology that the model is representing.

7 · Why Open-Source Nonprofit, Not Venture-Backed

Host: Asking for a friend — you guys all believe in venture-backed companies as a way to have impact on the world. What was it like collecting data on zebrafish, or the span of the data, or the wet-lab work, or just the scale? What makes this a better fit for this big nonprofit ecosystem effort versus a venture-backed company?

Mark: Well, I think we just want to give tools to the whole scientific community. In order to have the biggest impact — it's not actually clear that we couldn't run it as a business if we wanted to. I just think that we'll have a bigger impact by getting this in more scientist hands quicker by doing it as open source projects instead.

It's an interesting question. I'm not sure that — obviously you were doing it as a — when we were doing modeling before, you run into certain issues. You have to raise a large amount of money in order to build compute clusters. In a lot of ways the data is actually even more of a constraint. If you look at the scale of these models compared to language models, they're smaller — but they're smaller because the amount of data is less. In order to get the data, it's not just like there's some factory somewhere that you can pay to produce the data — you actually need to invent new novel scientific approaches.

For example, the type of cellular engineering we're doing in New York or the types of devices in Chicago. Which is why, when we're talking about this concept of frontier biology and frontier AI, the frontier biology is: you need to do real science to advance different biological methods in order to be able to observe the things that create the data that go to the model. It's not just an off-the-shelf thing that you can create.

That's a pretty big effort. I don't know that there are that many things like that that are done as biotechs. It's the scale of the ambition of what we're doing, the horizon over which we're committed to doing it. Part of the theory is, if you're building tools that are this complicated, you want to have a 10-to-15-year time horizon on building out these efforts. And then the scale of capital required. There's no rule that said you couldn't do it as an incredibly well-funded startup, but this just made more sense. It also is simplifying strategically to not have to think about how you're going to make money with the different things. We just want to get the models in people's hands. We release them as open source.

Again, the theory isn't that we're going to cure the diseases. We're not. It's that we want to help accelerate the pace of progress for the whole scientific field.

Priscilla: As the person least experienced with making money here, I would say that the sort of neutral nonprofit nature of our work actually helps harness more people to enter this effort. To actually achieve the mission of understanding the totality of human biology and to cure, prevent, manage all disease, you actually do need the entire academic biotech industry to come together and to work on this in a sort of unified way. In part because there's a lot of talent out there, and it's not helpful to exclude any talent from the effort.

And there's a super-long tail of diseases. There are the common ones, and even the common ones — if you unbundle heart disease, cancer, neurodegenerative diseases, even if you unbundle dementia or depression, there are many, many subcategories that become more and more niche. And that's not even looking at the long tail of rare diseases. Those often get orphaned and don't get brought along when we're sort of looking at what the most efficient way to impact the lives of many.

But if you decentralize the effort and put the tools in many people's hands, you start getting people who are like, "You know what, I am super interested in spinal muscular atrophy, and that's something I care deeply about." And if you put the tools in that person's hands, they're going to be able to make progress. If you had to focus your efforts and make big bets, you probably wouldn't, because it's just a niche, individual, small-group disease — that actually, in turn, if we can understand that disease process, helps us unlock knowledge about a lot more about how the human body works.

8 · Which Diseases First? — The Wrong Question

Host: Do you have any thoughts or predictions in terms of what disease areas this work will impact first? I know it's very hard to be predictive, but just given the nature of the work and the nature of the models, are there areas you're most optimistic about in the short to medium term?

Priscilla: That's actually not how I think about it, at least.

The way I think about it is: we want to understand how biology works. The ideal world is you would say, "I understand the genetics of this person." I want to think about people at the individual level. I want to understand the genetics of this person. I want to understand the risks they have to different illnesses. I want to understand the mechanistic connection between, say, a gene variant, a protein, and a disease process — because if you understand that through-chain, then you can design a protein, design a drug bespoke to them, and actually make an intervention.

Right now — and I'm sure we've all had experiences being sick — if you have something that's even remotely non-standard, you go into PubMed, you look up a paper, you look up the supplement, and then you start going through the methods, and you're like, "Am I represented in this paper?" And we're just making guesses. We really have no mechanistic understanding. We're saying, "Okay, you're kind of like these people that we studied, and this drug kind of impacts the pathway that we think is implicated. Let's try and see if anything happens." And time passes, and sometimes it works and sometimes it doesn't.

So my goal is to be able to treat the individual as an individual, understand the mechanisms, and be able to intervene. And there are different diseases that are at different stages of filling out that whole through-line. For some diseases, you just want to understand which gene variants actually cause disease and which don't — and that in itself can be super empowering to patients. Beyond that, there are some diseases where we understand the chain, we just can't intervene and change a specific protein function. That's super exciting too: if we could design a protein to actually change the physiology, then we can actually cure someone. But to me, that is just as exciting as contributing to our understanding of how someone gets sick in the first place.

Host: That's a very exciting vision because you're basically saying you can bring generalizable tools to provide very personalized things for each individual person.

Priscilla: Yes. And that's the power of the approach — you have these big models that you build that can then apply anywhere.

9 · Timelines, Systems Thinking, and Inflammation

Host: I know that you mentioned earlier that you were going to try and cure/prevent all diseases within a hundred years, and you mentioned, "Hey, it could actually be sooner now given all the advances in AI." Do you have some thought of when we think we'll be closer to that goal?

Mark: I'm optimistic it'll be sooner. The thing that's complicated is that it's a dynamic system. If you fix something, there will obviously be future things that you need to work on. So I don't think the current set of things that we're aware of are going to be the only things that need to get worked out. But the progress with AI is very exciting on this.

The other thing I'd say, adding to what you were saying a second ago: we really look at more kind of systems than specific diseases. For example, one area that seems really important to understand is inflammation. We talked about this a bunch. This is a big focus of the Chicago Biohub. There's a lot of data on that. It's quite clear that it's connected to a bunch of different diseases. But rather than studying the specific diseases, we think that by trying to understand inflammation more broadly, that will make it so that other companies that can then use these tools can work on specific therapies.

Another example: the immune system is a very good case to study for some of the work that we're doing in cellular engineering and when we're laddering up from proteins to cells to whole dynamic systems within the body. That one makes sense — it's sort of privileged. The cells can travel around through the body. Obviously, that has a big part in addressing different diseases: how do you make the immune system function better? Exactly how do you connect that last mile, I think, is going to be more something that biotech or other academics individually studying things will be better suited to do. So this is how we think about building out the toolset that just helps accelerate all these other folks.

10 · Translating to the Clinic

Host: Whether the timeline is, hopefully, less than 100 years — I think it's useful for maybe your average doctor or patient or human being (everybody's a patient) to think about: what's externally visible in the progress here? You worked with patients for a long time at UCSF. What should doctors look out for? What should people look out for if you're actually accelerating progress?

Priscilla: This is the part — I'm super excited about the progress for us, especially with this launch that Alex and his team have put forth. And I think it's very clear that science is going to start moving pretty quickly. The thing that's less clear to me is exactly how we translate to the clinic and what that looks like. I think what has to change is actually the way we do clinical research.

My hope is that we're really shortening the distance between bench research and patient impact. But there's a lot of steps there that we need people who actually take care of patients to think creatively about and think about how to deploy safely. That's a gap that we have some work in. We partner with Jennifer Doudna's Cures program at UCSF. We're dipping our toe in understanding how the deployment of research needs to change given how quickly research will be progressing. But that one is still shaping up.

11 · The New ESMFold Launch

Alex: Maybe I could say something about our most recent launch, because I think it's also —

Host: Please, we should explicitly —

Alex: Yeah. So it was just about a week ago now that we announced the new ESMFold. This is basically an open system for scientific discovery in protein biology. It's a world model of protein biology that's been trained — it's language-model–based. It's been trained on billions of protein sequences, learns these emergent representations of protein biology, and then we can use it to make predictions of atomic-resolution protein structure.

And it's really fast. So it's blazing fast. It's kind of illustrating this Pareto-optimal frontier of speed and accuracy in structure prediction. This allows us to characterize really vast stretches of the protein universe. So we folded over 1.1 billion proteins and predicted their structures, and identified features connecting all of them through mechanistic interpretability.

But the thing I thought was most exciting about this model is it's this really general model of protein biology. So you can use it as a world model. You can actually really start to search the space of the world model to design new proteins. It's hitting state-of-the-art across pretty much every structure-prediction benchmark, and especially on protein–protein interactions and protein–antibody interactions, which is really critical for therapeutic design.

What we found is, you can actually now use the model to design proteins and to design single-chain antibodies. You can do all of this digitally — and then, really in a small number of experimental trials — basically like a 96-well plate — you select from hundreds of thousands of trajectories digitally, actually synthesize 96 proteins, test them in the lab in a really short, easy experimental cycle. And we found nanomolar binders there. That's really the level for therapeutic activity.

It's showing that you can have these general-purpose models that — we didn't design a model for antibodies. We didn't design a model to be able to bind one particular target. We just designed a model that could understand proteins, and you kind of get protein design as an emergent property.

I also think it illustrates the power of open science and open source — we release this as basically an open discovery engine, and so really anyone can build on it. It takes what are these really intensive laboratory experiments where you have to screen through hundreds of thousands or millions of antibodies in high-throughput screens in the lab, and now you can really just spin up an instance in compute and be able to generate antibodies.

Priscilla: You should say more about — we took that data when we did an antibody screen and then we validated, we looked at PD-L1 in cells, and then we looked at it under the cryo, and sort of how all that complemented and validated what you were seeing in the models.

Alex: That's right. It's really critical to actually go and characterize these molecules in the lab. We have a structural biology center here where we have incredibly powerful cryo-EM microscopes, and so we're really able to look at these proteins biophysically and functionally. We designed proteins for several therapeutically relevant targets and were able to confirm their function — and —

Host: When it works the way it's supposed to.

Alex: Yeah, it's very amazing. We're able to look at the structure also, so you can see atomic resolution at the binding interfaces.

Host: Correct.

12 · The 15-Year, $1.5B Drug-Development Problem

Host: I know a lot of your work is really focused on basic research and building out the fundamentals. If I look at actual translation into drugs or drug development, often a clinical trial will be 15 years. It'll cost $1.5 billion. About $50 million of that often is the molecule and pre-clinical work, and it's a few years of work. The other $1.45 billion in decade-plus is actually the drug development side of it.

A lot of that seems to be gated on regulatory issues, recruitment, a variety of things, but a lot of it also has to do with the failure of drugs in trials around things like absorption or toxicity. Have you considered at all tackling that other chain of molecular design and thinking, or is the primary focus more on the basic biology and the initial molecules?

Priscilla: At least my hope, in building this comprehensive model of how cells work, is actually also being able to predict off-target effects. You can do some of that with biological models, because right now some of the off-target effects are: we just didn't know your kidney cell also expressed this receptor, and then when we test it in human, we see it happening and we see renal toxicity.

If you have a single-cell atlas that looks at all the different cell types — some of which actually were not predicted before we modeled them — you can start looking at which cells actually do have receptors for the target you thought you were exclusively targeting, and be able to predict some of these downstream effects before we get into the human trials. That's actually one of the more exciting applications of a transcriptomic model: to understand actually how the different cells will react when you intervene and do something.

When you think about delivery mechanisms and patient care, that's where you start having to be creative about — what disease do you want to cure first? There are certain diseases that will be easier to deliver a therapeutic to, or where the risk/reward makes more sense. We were all inspired by baby KJ, I think last year now, when the team at CHOP was able to deliver a CRISPR therapeutic to edit a mutation that would have inevitably led him to significant neurotoxicity and altered his life.

That disease was very carefully chosen, because we needed to target his liver cells, and if we could easily deliver a product that would work in his liver — I think that's when the creativity, the wherewithal to choose the right applications, can help us unlock the first applications.

Alex: Maybe something to add to that — you described the conventional drug development process. These tools have the potential to have a lot of impact on that process, but what's interesting is to really start to think about the new paradigms that can open up. What does it mean if the barrier to develop a drug, to design a molecule, to get through all of those stages, is so much lower? You have programmable biology, and you can really start to create a medicine for every individual patient. That has enormous implications for how we do drug development and what the future of medicine looks like.

Host: Yeah. It'll be an exciting day when the FDA accepts a virtual clinical trial for phase one or something — based on some personal view of that person.

Alex: Yeah. Or even short of that — thinking about the specific mechanisms where you see this acceleration — I imagine if people feel like they can predict impact in kidney cells, or have a stronger perspective on tox because they have this broader understanding, they'll be willing to try many more programs.

13 · Rare Diseases and the Long Tail

Mark: Recruitment could also change. We have this program — Rare As One — and the basic idea is that a lot of people focus on the most common diseases, but there's this long tail, and the economics don't quite work out for companies to focus on those diseases. But if you can make it so that the groups of patients can come together and organize and say, "Hey, we would take an experimental drug on this," then — because of the cost we're talking about, and how that's a huge amount of the overall cost — if you can flip that, then it actually makes the economics make a lot more sense, if you can generate something more easily and you can pair it with a group of people.

One of the interesting things from science and engineering is that often you can hit your head against the wall on the common problems — and in this case diseases — but a lot of times you learn a lot more about a system from finding some kind of rare or weird side thing that's happening in edge cases. That's always been an interesting part of this that actually connects pretty well to this — because now you're going to be able to enable a long tail of new kinds of ideas to get tried, and enable them to potentially get tested more easily.

Priscilla: That's a really good point. On our rare-disease cohorts — first of all, they're incredibly inspiring and powerful. Patient groups are self-organizing patient registries, natural history registries, biobanks. They're organizing their own clinical trials. There's gene therapy that one disease group has moved forward over the course of, like, 3 to 5 years rather than decades. The speed is so fast, because the patients themselves have organized the resources that a scientist or a clinician might need. It's incredible.

Mark: But to some degree you're going to need something like this, because there are going to be many more new things that can get created. That doesn't mean that for the general population you're not going to want the same level of vetting that we've had historically. But making it so that people who want to be on more of the frontier have the ability to do that is also going to be pretty helpful.

Host: Letting people opt in to be part of trials, I think, is one of the big shifts that is starting to happen but could really help accelerate biology in general.

14 · Open Ecosystems — A Common Philosophy

Host: All three of you have mentioned at different points the power of open ecosystems in such a large space. Some of that logic around open source and the breadth or diversity of data collection — it should also apply in the language model world and the multimodal AI world. Do you think that's right? Does any of the work you're doing here change how you think about AI and Meta?

Mark: I think it's sort of a similar philosophy overall. Priscilla was talking about this — a lot of our focus is building tools that empower individuals to do things. That's a common theme across a lot of the things that I work on: putting the technology in individuals' hands. We don't believe in this very centralized future where there should be a small number of institutions that basically are advancing all this stuff.

Our vision is not that there's going to be some central super-intelligence that solves all of science. People are really important, and I think we'll be more important in the future, and giving people more tools to be more productive is going to be a critical part of any kind of positive future. That's how progress has always been made historically, right? It's not through centralization. It's through empowering individuals to try things that are somewhat out of the mainstream — that other people didn't think were good ideas because they thought they were good ideas that already had been done.

That's very central to the whole ethos. To some degree it's why you create something like social media, right? To give people a voice. A lot of the stuff that I care about — in terms of empowering people with individual AI — open source is one instantiation of it. It's not the only way to do it. It certainly is one way that you basically are saying: we're going to take this technology and put it in everyone's hands.

In terms of science, I think it really makes sense and we're deeply committed to open source. There are obviously interesting considerations on this that are important too — there's a lot of considerations around biosafety and things like that that we're going to need to balance. But overall, this is very deep in the ethos of the work that we're doing both at Biohub and probably a theme for a lot of the stuff that I do: we believe that a positive future is one where you build a technology as a tool, you put it in individuals' hands, and that's how society makes progress.

15 · Recruiting AI Talent to Biohub

Host: You have this incredibly ambitious mission at Biohub, and yet the AI scientists that work here could also go work in commercial enterprises. How do you think about the talent and how to bring people to Biohub?

Mark: Where do you want to start? It's a very hot market for AI researchers, but part of what that means is that they're very in-demand and can work on the things that they want to work on. This gets back to this point again about frontier AI and frontier biology. The AI researchers who work here could go work on language models or things at any of the main labs. But those labs don't have the frontier biology part attached to it. So there's a very large mission component of this — there's an ability to do this unique work here that you just can't really do at the other places. If that's what your focus is, I don't actually think that there's any other organization in the world that's doing both the frontier biology and the frontier AI.

Host: Why are you here, Alex?

Alex: I mean, it's really simple. Our mission is to cure, prevent, and manage disease — and I think —

Host: You say with a straight face, in a less-than-100-year timeline.

Alex: It's very serious now. No more — yeah. It's a really powerful mission. Scientists are very motivated by that. It's something people are deeply motivated by. And we're at this moment in time where that actually seems like something that can be achieved. We're building a really unique place where we're tackling that problem, and we have the resources and the right things to actually really go after that and do that.

Host: That resonates with me as somebody who talks to and hires a lot of research scientists. They want to know: do you have the data, do you have the tools, do you have the compute, do you have the talent, and then what's the mission? So I think that's super competitive.

Mark: The other thing is that you don't need a very large team. It's an interesting thing about the world — people care about different missions, and that's good. That's part of why giving people the ability to explore what they care about, whether it's across science or just across everything, is such a powerful way to make progress in society — people care about different things.

In order to make progress in AI you don't need many hundreds of AI researchers or thousands or anything like that. I think you can really make progress with a very strong group of a dozen or a couple dozen people. Finding people who care about this mission is not a particularly hard thing. This is a super-important thing in the world. It's just a cool thing about the world that people are drawn to different missions.

16 · From ESMFold 2 to Zero-Shot Therapeutics

Host: I think the simplest mental models that folks have — even if they're paying attention to the space — are essentially: okay, structure prediction models for proteins, and protein–protein interaction models. So there's this one piece, which is fundamental understanding. And then there's this theory of: someday we're just going to be able to like zero-shot things into either the clinic, or the clinic with much better hit rate. What needs to happen for us to go from ESMFold 2 to this other piece — is that feasible?

Alex: That's a great question. I would say I'm really optimistic on that. On the one hand, these are problems that historically people could spend an entire career working on — like how do you figure out how to effectively optimize a drug? How do you get it through pre-clinical? How do you do the early safety?

When you have a new scientific paradigm, questions that were once hard become simplified through the new paradigm. So I'm very optimistic that many of these core problems will be solved in an emergent way through these models. One great example is toxicity: if you can really digitally simulate everything and be able to predict where a drug is going to distribute and bind across the human body, you kind of have the beginning of a solution to that problem. Once you have these accurate representations at the molecular level, we're going to start to see really rapid progress on a lot of these core problems.

Host: What is the most exciting use or experimentation with the models you've seen in the last week since release?

Alex: It's just been great to see it get integrated in all kinds of things. One of the really interesting things we've been seeing is people connecting it with agentic systems to do automated design — to just automate that whole process. It's another example of how you can see bringing together agentic and frontier AI with the ability to have a world model for biology and actually reason about biology, and really start to automate the entire design process.

17 · The Virtual Cell — What's Next on the Research Agenda

Host: How do you decide what the next step in the research agenda is? It's world model for biology, and then — I could scale it up, I could add more data. Adding data is a non-trivial thing in terms of new methods and domains. Do you take input from the larger ecosystem about how people are using it, and what would make it more useful? Or is it really like, we understand the next step of structures or coverage that we're looking for?

Alex: There's two things. We have a view on the next big challenge, which I think is the virtual cell — really being able to ladder up the hierarchy of biological complexity to the cell.

Host: Sorry, very basic question — this virtual cell model, what is the input and output I should expect?

Alex: There's different views on that, but I think what you ultimately want is a system that can really model each of the levels of complexity. So the proteomic layer, the genetic layer, the transcriptomic layer — and connect that to the phenotype. And you need enough generality so that you can ask the model questions about a new intervention in a context that it hasn't been trained on and get an answer from it. The gap that we need to close as a field is being able to really make those predictions that can generalize. So that's going to require an enormous effort to generate data.

Mark: In terms of what you decide to do next, this is a pretty normal process of constraint management. Every lab in every field across the world probably feels compute-constrained. That's probably true here too. There's always questions like: okay, should we double down more on advancing the protein piece? Should we do more of the cellular stuff? Those are ongoing debates in terms of how you sequence that. Within that, there's being at the Pareto frontier about how much you want to train the different models, and the size of the models is also dependent on the scale of the data that you have.

It's there's some of that is just where you want to be on the curves and then normal constraints — but this is probably the same process that any research organization goes through: you want to go in all these different directions, and you're just trying to constraint-optimize and make enough progress to do world-class work at one thing at a time, while planting some seeds that can blossom over the next couple of years as well.

18 · 5-Year Look-Ahead

Host: This has been the most dynamic period of technology, at least I've seen over my career. It's so exciting in terms of everything that's happening with AI, and every week there's something new that's changed. Are you tired or invigorated?

Mark: I feel like everybody's in manic phase.

Host: Yes — it's a combination of invigorated and exhausted.

Mark: Yeah, it's wonderful.

Host: Things are very unpredictable right now. It's really hard to know what's coming. We have these almost early signs of exponentiation on the model side, with agentic flows that we're starting to see in really interesting ways, models starting to help more and more with models — it's still very early days for that. If you're thinking back 5 years from now, and you were to define what success was relative to your efforts — and I know things are very dynamic, things changed a lot, but you have this common thread of tooling for the BioHub, you have a common thread of empowering scientists at scale — looking back 5 years from now, is there a specific thing that you really want to make sure that you've accomplished or achieved, or a primary goal?

Mark: We have a pretty clear view of this hierarchical set of world models that we want to build around biology. The other part of that is that we want to do the highest-quality work in the world. We're basically set up to do that between having a world-class AI research team and this collection of biohubs, which are world-class life-sciences research organizations. That's fundamentally a setup that no other organization in the world has.

But you can have a lot of great ingredients, and that doesn't guarantee that you succeed. So to me, 5 years from now, looking back — I'm sure other labs or efforts will try to produce things that approximate what we're trying to do, and I just think that we should be able to do something that is meaningfully better and a unique intellectual contribution to the world. That's what you're trying to do whenever you do any kind of research. So if we do that, I think we'll all feel very good.

I would also expect that at some point we'll just start seeing a lot more idea generation from the people using the models. But I have enough faith that that part will materialize, that for me it's more just about making sure that we do world-class work — and I think if we do, the rest almost will take care of itself.

19 · The Last Year's Biggest Update

Host: Very last question for you. Snapshot of it's mid-2026. What's the biggest update in your own thinking about Biohub or the domain from the last year?

Mark: From the last year — I mean, you joined in the last year. The biggest thing is that we basically rotated, and in the last year we basically formalized that Biohub is the main focus of our philanthropy. So that's been a very big shift.

Alex and the team coming in I think has been interesting, not only because it's a world-class group — I mean, you guys have worked together for a while — you talked about how stuff is changing so much in the field. One thing that's underrated is that this is an extremely talented group of people who also know each other and work well together, and are stable and good. That also is underestimated in terms of the compounding benefit of people being able to work well in a stable environment over time. That's a really important piece.

Part of what we wanted to do was — prior to Alex leading the effort, the previous leaders of the Biohub were primarily biologists who were interested in technology. Now this is the point where we really flipped that, where — obviously you have a background in biology as well, but you are primarily an AI researcher who has a background in biology. That's a deep reflection on the way that we expect this is going to drive more value in the future. So those are probably the biggest updates in the last year in terms of the work that we're doing. And it's a new leader, not just a leader, but a team. That has been a really good — and then yeah, I mean, I think on the rest of the industry, it's on track.

It's this crazy thing — when you have an exponentially growing curve, the way that an exponential curve feels is, it's growing so quickly that the emotional feeling is, it can't possibly keep going. But the nature of an exponential curve is, it doesn't just keep going — it keeps accelerating. Exponential growth is accelerating. That has all these emotions and psychology attached to it. But fundamentally, when you look at the curve in the industry, the fundamental thing is, it is on track. It has remained on that curve, which has all these very profound implications for all of these domains. But certainly, it validates and makes one feel very good about making a very big investment in the things that will play out if you stay on that track. And it seems like we are. So that, I think, is very good news.

Host: I think the most important aspect of what you're doing there is you're actually closing the loop with the actual biology. Because with code and research, it's closed-loop systems, and so they're very fast to iterate. This is an open-loop system, so you're closing a loop, and that's really crucial to progress.

Priscilla: For me, one of the biggest changes with the strategy we're driving now — and Alex at the helm — is, before, we had amazing teams moving generally in the same direction and understanding the potential collaborations and interconnectedness of our work. But now we are arms-linked, moving together. It's very directed, and it's very exciting. It's a little bit scary, but it's truly a team playing off each other in trying to make progress towards this goal. That has taken a lot of work, but also the maturity of our teams being able to have their work at a level of maturation where it actually does make sense to interlock.

Host: Amazing. Well, to teams being on the curve, thank you guys for doing this.

All: Thank you for joining us. Thank you. Thank you.

Outro

Find us on Twitter at @NoPriorsPod. Subscribe to our YouTube channel if you want to see our faces. Follow the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you listen. That way, you get a new episode every week. And sign up for emails or find transcripts for every episode at no-priors.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：这是一个"明确宣告自己不会治病"的治病项目
整段对话里最具颠覆性的一句话被 Mark 重复了两次：**"The theory isn't that we're going to cure the diseases. We're not."** 这不是谦虚，而是一种深刻的战略反转——一个承诺"百年内治愈、预防和管理所有疾病"的组织，却拒绝亲自治任何一种病。
这其实是把硅谷最古老的一条经验偷渡进了生物学：**做平台的人永远比做应用的人活得久、影响大**。Linux 不治病、TCP/IP 不送信、CUDA 不训练模型，但它们决定了上面所有应用的天花板。BioHub 正在把自己定位成"生物学的 CUDA"。这也解释了为什么他们坚持开源——不是道德姿态，而是结构性必然：**平台只有在被广泛采纳后才成为平台**，闭源的"平台"只是一家公司。
## 二、被忽视的跨域共振：扎克伯格的"社交媒体哲学"正在被原封不动地搬进细胞建模
注意 Mark 在末段说的那句话：*"It's why you create something like social media, rig

item.title: [Sequoia Capital] NVIDIA's Jensen Huang on Building the Dynamo of the Intelligence Age
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Capital
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、Jensen 偷偷调用了 19 世纪的"杰文斯悖论"，但他不说破

他讲放射科那一段——AI 视觉超人类、12 年前就能识别影像、但放射科医生反而变多了——这其实是 1865 年英国经济学家 William Stanley Jevons 观察蒸汽机时发现的同一个机制：蒸汽机越省煤，全英国烧的煤越多。效率提升不是替代需求，而是释放被压抑的需求。医院之所以没增加放射科医生，不是不需要，而是单位人力扫不完；AI 一来，瓶颈打开，积压的病人涌入，人力反而稀缺。

让人意外的是：这意味着对 AI 最恐惧的行业，往往是最有可能扩张的行业——只要那个行业存在大量"被压抑的需求"（医疗、法律、教育、心理咨询）。反过来，那些需求已经被满足的行业（比如客服）才真的危险。Jensen 没有说破这一层，但他的整套论证悄悄站在 Jevons 一边。

二、"从检索到生成"——其实是一次回到口传时代的文明级倒带

Jensen 说过去 60 年的计算机是"检索机器"（data center 这个名字就是证据，他点破得很巧），未来是"生成机器"。但他没说的是：人类在古登堡印刷术之前，认知模式本就是生成而非检索的。荷马、《吠陀》、维京萨迦——没有"原稿"，每次说唱都是吟游诗人根据听众即兴重新生成。文字、印刷、硬盘，这三千年是一段人为压缩的"检索插曲"。

这就带出一个跨领域的奇怪含义：未来读者会更像古希腊听众，而不像 20 世纪读者。同一本书，每个人读到的版本不同；同一条新闻，每个人收到的叙事不同。这不是反乌托邦，这是回归——只是这一次，"吟游诗人"是 GPU。Jensen 在用 Siemens、电网、互联网来类比 AI，但真正的历史类比，是从印刷术回到口传。

三、五层蛋糕的讲述顺序，暴露了 Nvidia 的真实身位

Jensen 描述"五层蛋糕"时永远从能源讲起（能源 → 芯片 → 基建 → 模型 → 应用）。这不是教学顺序，是一个战略叙事。

传统讲法应该从用户价值开始（顶层应用 → 模型 → 算力 → 能源）。但他反着讲，原因有二： 1. 底层视角天然显得更"不可替代"——你可以换模型公司，但你换不掉电、换不掉芯片。 2. 每讲一层，听众的注意力都被往下拉一点，最后停在能源；而 Nvidia 恰好卡在第二层（最接近能源的"不可替代"那一端），同时它的 NVLink/Dynamo 也在往第三层（基础设施）侵蚀。

更隐蔽的细节：他说"每个 gigawatt 工厂 500 亿美金成本、产出 3000–4000 亿美金 token 价值"——这是 6–8 倍的资本回报。当一个 CEO 主动给出这个数字时，他不是在介绍行业，他是在为下一轮 capex 募资做铺垫。Jensen 不是在讲 AI 是什么，他是在写一份针对 60 个国家主权基金的招股说明书。

四、一个被多数听众漏掉的关键句

他说："如果你不知道一个东西怎么工作，你怎么把它做得更好？AI 每年都在变好，所以那些说'我们不知道它怎么工作'的人在说谎。"——这句话表面是反"AI 末日论"，底下却是一个非常强硬的认识论主张：可改进性 = 可理解性。

但这在科学史上是站不住脚的——人类几千年里都能让冶金、农业、药物变得更好，却完全不理解机制。改进 ≠ 理解，这是哥白尼之前的全部技术史。Jensen 知道这一点，但他选择用一个修辞性命题去对冲恐惧叙事。这个细节让你看清：他不是在解释 AI，他是在为一项 20 万亿美金/年的产业清扫监管阻力。理解他在哪一层"修辞"，比相信他说的内容更重要。

📝 转录文本

Opening: The AI Factory at the Center of the Buildout

Thank you so much, Jensen.

So, uh, we are in the middle of a massive AI revolution. Uh, it is probably bigger and faster than even the industrial revolution, and you have called out what's happening right now as the largest infrastructure buildout in human history. At the center of that buildout is the AI factory, and the company enabling all of that is Nvidia. Can you tell us what is an AI factory and why is it the best investment for any enterprise in the next decade?

Two Views of AI — What It Can Do

Okay, so um, you could understand AI in a particular number of ways. The way that you understand AI probably most is through a chatbot through a web browser. Uh, you're interacting with it. You give it a prompt. It says something back to you. And even those of you who have been using AI for some time, uh, you've seen in the last couple two three years a very significant evolution improvement in the capabilities of AI.

Two years ago, you heard about ChatGPT. And ChatGPT basically is a computer software that understands the input you give it. It can perceive, understand information, and it can translate and generate the information into something else. Okay? So, you can give it a prompting. You can say, "Here's this PDF I gave you. Um, I would like you now to summarize it." It went from text to text. You could also tell it, "Here's a PDF I gave you. Um, I would like you to now generate an image of that story." It go text to image. You could use go from image to text, meaning you could give it a picture and you can what's happening inside this picture. It goes image to text. Does that make sense? Anything to anything else.

And AI in the last — in two years time, two years ago was largely able to do this translation. We called it generation, generative models. Okay, generative AI. But the thing that is very big deal inside that word generative AI is, in order to do something even more valuable than generation, understanding and generating is thinking. Well, you can't think if you don't generate words. And so the foundation of generative AI gave us the ability to generate internal thoughts — thinking, reasoning, step-by-step reasoning, problem solving.

From Generation to Action — Tools, Robots, Self-Driving Cars

It also allowed us to do another thing that is now very important, which is generate intelligence to control something else, to generate control to use a tool. Does it make sense? To use a browser, use a spreadsheet, use Photoshop, use PowerPoint, use something, use AutoCAD, use another tool? Now that tool today is digital, but someday that tool will be mechanical. So if I generate a command to a mechanical system, that would be called robotics. If I generate commands for uh a machine with steering wheel, that would be called self-driving cars. Does that make sense?
